Restoring: Luk 15:20 (OBS-103-057) — the father's love restores the broken son-relationship through compassion and embrace. Joh 21:17 (OBS-103-153) — Jesus' love restores Peter's relational capacity after denial. Disordered love impairs relational capacity: love-of-self (filautos — OBS-103-137) produces the self-enclosed orientation that cannot genuinely connect to another. |
| OBS-103-T2-165 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 7:47, 1Jo 4:19 | The three sequences (Q&A-202) reveal a common underlying structure: love is not the starting point of the inner-being movement but the outcome of a prior transaction. In the forgiveness sequence: guilt is resolved by forgiveness, and love flows from the resolution. In the divine-love sequence: lovelessness is met by divine love, and responsive love emerges. In the receiving-rest sequence: striving is displaced by received love, and rest follows. The common revelation: love is the product of reception rather than the self-generated initiating state. The person does not begin with love and then encounter God; they encounter God's love and then love in return. This sequence has a directional implication for formation: love cannot be trained from the outside in (as if instruction alone could produce it); it must be received first, and then expressed. The before-state is always some form of lacking — guilt, lovelessness, or striving — that love's prior reception resolves. |
| OBS-103-T2-166 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 7:47 | All four — suffering deepens, tests, reveals, and produces love in the evidence. **Deepens:** the sufferer's appeal to love in affliction (Psa 63:3 — 'because your steadfast love is better than life' written by David in the wilderness) shows suffering deepening the value assigned to divine love. **Tests:** Pro 19:7 (OBS-103-073) — poverty dissolves conditional friendship; adversity-testing reveals which loves are genuine. Pro 27:6 (OBS-103-059): 'faithful are the wounds of a friend' — love expressed through painful honesty under pressure. **Reveals:** Joh 11:36 (OBS-103-149): Jesus' grief at Lazarus' tomb reveals his love — suffering-response is love's most visible evidence (grief = love under loss). 2Sa 1:26 (OBS-103-095): David's lament reveals the measure of his love for Jonathan. **Produces:** Luk 7:47 (via SD-020) — love produced through the suffering-and-forgiveness pathway: forgiveness of guilt-suffering generates love. The suffering-love relationship is reciprocal and multi-directional. |
| OBS-103-T2-167 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Joh 14:15 | Love plays a dual role in the sanctification arc: it is both the **process** and the **goal**. As process: love operating through the life — obedience, community, training — is itself sanctifying. Pro 3:3's heart-inscription implies that sustained exercise of chesed changes the person over time into the kind of person whose heart has it written into it. Joh 14:15 (OBS-103-140): sustained love of Christ produces the commandment-keeping pattern that constitutes sanctification's behavioural dimension. As goal: the 1Cor 13 portrait is the telos of sanctification — a person who loves with no envy, arrogance, or self-seeking is the sanctification's outcome. 1Jo 4:18 (via SP-103-029): 'perfect love casts out fear' — perfect love is the eschatological form of sanctification's completion. Love is not one virtue among the list of sanctification's outputs; it is the organising content from which all other virtue flows and toward which sanctification moves. |
| OBS-111-T2-002 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Jo 4:10 | Yes — Scripture explicitly and repeatedly attributes mercy to God. The attributions cluster around three patterns: (1) God as merciful in character (*rachum*, *oiktirmōn*, *eleēmōn*); (2) God's mercy as motivated and purposive (Psa 78:38 — compassion motivates atonement; 1Jo 4:10 — love motivates propitiation); (3) God's mercy as the ground of human identity (1Pe 2:10 — mercy reception constitutes God's people). That God possesses and exercises mercy reveals that mercy is a perfection — a quality of the divine nature that is good without qualification. In the human person, mercy thus reflects the divine image most directly when it is shown toward those who have no claim on it. OBS-111-030, OBS-111-031, OBS-111-052, OBS-111-053. |
| OBS-111-T2-003 | OBSERVATION | Col 3:12 | The verse evidence indicates that human mercy equips the person for relational participation in the mercy-logic that structures the divine-human relationship: Mat 5:7 (the merciful receive mercy — mercy enables participation in the mercy-reality); Mat 18:33 (received mercy is meant to flow outward — mercy equips the person for generous relational extension); Col 3:12 (mercy as first inner garment of the new humanity — mercy is constitutive of the character set that makes community life possible). What the evidence does not address is whether mercy is part of the original pre-fall design or whether it is specifically a post-fall response. The atonement vocabulary presupposes the fallen condition — it has no function where there is no guilt. This is a gap: OBS-111-075 and the atonement cluster evidence do not speak to mercy's pre-fall status. OBS-111-032, OBS-111-055, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-006 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Col 3:12 | Mercy expresses the divine image through the correspondence structure: "Be merciful as your Father is merciful" (Luk 6:36) names the human-divine alignment of inner disposition. The human person who shows mercy toward those who have no claim on their help is imaging the divine character as the data package description states. Col 3:12 ("compassionate hearts... as God's chosen ones, holy and beloved") grounds the character command in election and divine love — the image is expressed because the person has received mercy and now reflects it. OBS-111-030, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-008 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Col 3:12 | Mercilessness (*aneleēmōn*, *anileōs*) appears in vice catalogues marking constitutional inner collapse (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034; Jam 2:13 — OBS-111-070). The absence of mercy is not merely virtue-failure but image-defacement — the inner person has lost the capacity that most visibly reflects the divine character. Conversely, the Beatitude (Mat 5:7) and the new humanity character list (Col 3:12) present mercy as the marker of those in whom the image is being restored. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-032, OBS-111-069, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-020 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Col 3:12 | Four distinct modes of operation emerge from the data: (1) Disposition mode — mercy as stable inner orientation (Col 3:12 — "compassionate hearts"; Mat 5:7 — "the merciful"); (2) Atoning mechanism mode — mercy as atoning act covering guilt (Lev 4:20, Psa 78:38); (3) Petition-response mode — mercy as the dynamic of supplication answered (Luk 18:13; Gen 25:21; Dan 2:18); (4) Naming/institutional mode — mercy given spatial and calendar form (mercy seat, Day of Atonement). *New finding: mercy operates in four structurally distinct modes — dispositional, mechanical, dialogic, and institutional — that together constitute its full operational range.* OBS-111-015, OBS-111-016, OBS-111-047, OBS-111-056. |
| OBS-111-T2-025 | OBSERVATION | Col 3:12 | Sustained mercy-reception produces: (1) A transformed inner orientation toward others — Mat 18:33 implies that received mercy should reshape the inner disposition so that mercy-extension becomes natural. (2) The character complex of the new humanity — Col 3:12 places mercy as the first in a sustained character formation sequence. (3) Sustained mercy-seeking as practice — Dan 6:11 (Daniel's daily prayer) and H8467 *techinah* (24 active verses of systematic supplication vocabulary) suggest that the mercy-encounter is not single but habitual. OBS-111-055, OBS-111-069, OBS-111-063. |
| OBS-111-T2-031 | OBSERVATION | Col 3:12 | The registry's dimension assignment is "Relational/Social" and the cluster assignment is C17 (relational-grace cluster). The primary dimension is Relational Disposition — mercy is fundamentally a disposition toward another person (or toward God, or from God toward the person). The group landscape (OBS-111-015, OBS-111-016) confirms that the largest clusters (mercy disposition and character groups — Theme B) are Relational Disposition/GOD and Relational Disposition/HUMAN. OBS-111-016, OBS-111-075. |
| OBS-111-T2-032 | OBSERVATION | Col 3:12 | Multiple evidence streams: (1) The divine-human correspondence structure (Luk 6:36 — "Be merciful as your Father is merciful") defines mercy as a relational orientation. (2) The person-type vocabulary (*eleēmōn*, *oiktirmōn*, *rachum*) designates mercy as a stable inner character quality directed toward others. (3) The Beatitude structure (Mat 5:7) and the new humanity character list (Col 3:12) place mercy in the relational character domain. OBS-111-030, OBS-111-032, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-039 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Col 3:12 | The heart vocabulary appears in heart/R183's shared anchor at Col 3:12 ("compassionate hearts" — *splanchna oiktirmou*, literally "bowels of compassion") and Luk 1:78 ("tender mercy... from the heart of our God" — *dia splanchna eleous*). The *splanchna* (bowels/heart) vocabulary links mercy to the inner affective centre. Heart/R183 shares Col 3:12 as an anchor. However, the data does not have a direct verse saying "mercy is in the heart" — the link is through the splanchna-vocabulary which bridges bowel and heart imagery. OBS-111-069, OBS-111-025. |
| OBS-111-T2-040 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Col 3:12 | The *splanchna* (bowels/heart) location of mercy reveals that it engages the affective-integrating function of the heart — mercy is a felt orientation, not merely a cognitive judgment or volitional decision. Luk 1:78 ("tender mercy from the bowels of our God") names mercy as arising from God's deepest affective centre. This aligns with Col 3:12's command to "put on compassionate hearts" — the heart is the site where mercy is either present as inner garment or absent as inner nakedness. OBS-111-068, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-043 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Col 3:12 | The *splanchna* (bowels/viscera) vocabulary appears in mercy texts (Luk 1:78; Col 3:12; 2Cor 1:3 context). This is the visceral, gut-level register of the inner person — neither heart nor mind in the narrow sense, but the inner body-soul interface. If *splanchna* is treated as a distinct soul subset (as the Biblical evidence seems to suggest — it is not simply heart or mind), then mercy has a gut-level location. The data does not have a more explicit soul-subset mapping beyond this. OBS-111-068. |
| OBS-111-T2-053 | OBSERVATION | 1Jo 4:10 | The origin is multiple in its expressions: divine mercy (the ultimate source), humanly received mercy (the intermediate channel), and humanly extended mercy (the derivative expression). The three are not independent origins — they form a chain. OBS-111-052, OBS-111-055. |
| OBS-111-T2-055 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Col 3:12 | Yes — the evidence shows mercy moving across constitutional levels in both directions. (1) Soul → body: mercy-seeking is expressed somatically (breast-beating, Luk 18:13; OBS-111-047). (2) Body → soul: blood-based atonement mediates mercy's effect to the *nephesh* (Lev 17:11; OBS-111-037). (3) Spirit-soul interface → relational expression: mercy as inner disposition moves outward into relational action (Col 3:12 — the inner garment expressed in community life). *New finding: mercy exhibits triadic constitutional movement — from inner disposition through embodied expression, from bodily mechanism through soul-transformation, and from spirit-soul interface through relational extension.* OBS-111-037, OBS-111-047, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-073 | OBSERVATION | 1Jo 4:10 | Yes — mercy is agentive in all three directions. God acts: atones, forgives, sends the Son (1Jo 4:10 — OBS-111-049), has mercy on whom he wills (Rom 9:15 — OBS-111-052). The mercy-seeker acts: cries out, beats breast, stands far off, persists (Luk 18:13, Mar 10:48 — OBS-111-047, OBS-111-054). The mercy-extender acts: shows mercy to the oppressed, forgives the fellow-servant (Dan 4:27 — OBS-111-062; Mat 18:33 — OBS-111-055). OBS-111-047, OBS-111-054, OBS-111-062. |
| OBS-111-T2-088 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Jo 4:10 | Yes — this is the most extensively evidenced direction in the entire registry. God-to-human mercy operates across all major vocabulary clusters: divine compassion as atoning act (Psa 78:38 — OBS-111-038); divine mercy constituting a people (1Pe 2:10 — OBS-111-053); divine propitiation in Christ (1Jo 4:10 — OBS-111-049); God being merciful toward iniquities (Heb 8:12 — OBS-111-035); God sovereign in mercy-extension (Rom 9:15 — OBS-111-052); divine compassion as Father's inner character (2Cor 1:3 — OBS-111-068). OBS-111-038, OBS-111-049, OBS-111-052, OBS-111-053, OBS-111-068. |
| OBS-111-T2-095 | OBSERVATION | Col 3:12 | The enabling inner conditions for human mercy-giving: (1) Prior reception of divine mercy (Mat 18:33 — the servant received mercy first; failure to extend was inexcusable because of this). (2) Solidarity with the condition of the needy — Heb 2:17 establishes the principle that mercy-extension requires experiential solidarity (OBS-111-033); Christ's incarnational solidarity with humanity is the mechanism for his priestly mercy. (3) The inner character quality of mercifulness (*eleēmōn*, *oiktirmōn*) — which Col 3:12 frames as a garment to be deliberately appropriated. OBS-111-033, OBS-111-055, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-105 | OBSERVATION | Col 3:12 | Mercy produces transformation at both levels. Condition: atonement changes the person's moral standing before God — guilt is covered, forgiveness is granted, the person is no longer "not a people" but "God's people" (1Pe 2:10 — OBS-111-053). Orientation: received mercy is meant to reshape inner disposition toward others (Mat 18:33 — OBS-111-055; Col 3:12 — OBS-111-069). DIM-111-001 (OBS-111-075) names this duality explicitly. *New finding: mercy produces transformation in both registers simultaneously — objective condition (moral standing changed) and subjective orientation (inner disposition reshaped). These are not sequential but co-occurring.* OBS-111-053, OBS-111-055, OBS-111-075. |
| OBS-111-T2-113 | OBSERVATION | Col 3:12 | Yes — Col 3:12 (OBS-111-069) places mercy ("compassionate hearts") as the first item in a deliberate character formation list — the new humanity's inner character complex is to be cultivated progressively. The "put on" (*endysasthe*) metaphor names it as ongoing formation, not one-time reception. The Beatitude (Mat 5:7 — OBS-111-032) designates the merciful as those whose character has been formed toward mercy. The daily prayer practice (Dan 6:11 — OBS-111-063) shows mercy-seeking as sanctifying practice, not isolated event. OBS-111-032, OBS-111-063, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-114 | OBSERVATION | Col 3:12 | Mercy functions as the foundational inner garment of the character formation arc (Col 3:12 — mercy listed first). The formation sequence in Col 3:12 (mercy, kindness, humility, meekness, patience) suggests mercy is the generative starting point from which other social virtues develop. *New finding: in the character-formation sequence of Col 3:12, mercy is positioned as the primary formative orientation from which the social-relational virtues develop — the first and generative inner garment of the new humanity.* OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-119 | CROSS_REGISTRY | 1Jo 4:10 | Multiple sequential patterns: (1) Guilt → mercy (OBS-111-047, OBS-111-054 — the condition of guilt/need generates mercy-seeking; guilt is the pre-condition, mercy the response). (2) Mercy → forgiveness (Lev 4:20 — atonement → forgiveness; OBS-111-036). (3) Mercy → joy (Job 33:26 — OBS-111-060). (4) Mercy → shame (Eze 16:63 — OBS-111-039). (5) Love → mercy/propitiation (1Jo 4:10 — OBS-111-049 — love is the source, propitiation the instrument). (6) Compassion → atoning act → anger restraint (Psa 78:38 — OBS-111-038). *New finding: mercy appears consistently in the middle of inner-being sequences — it follows a negative condition (guilt, despair, need) and produces a positive outcome (forgiveness, joy, identity constitution, peace). Mercy is the inner-being bridge from negative-condition to positive-outcome.* OBS-111-036, OBS-111-038, OBS-111-039, OBS-111-049, OBS-111-060. |
| OBS-111-T2-120 | CROSS_REGISTRY | 1Jo 4:10 | The sequences are causal, not merely correlational. Lev 4:20 uses wayyiselach ("and he shall be forgiven") as the consequence of atonement — forgiveness follows atonement causally. Psa 78:38 — God being compassionate causes the atoning act, which causes anger-restraint. 1Jo 4:10 — love sending the Son for propitiation is explicitly purposive (hina — "in order that"). The causality runs in both directions: mercy causes subsequent states (forgiveness, joy), and prior states cause mercy-extension (compassion causes atoning act; love causes propitiation). OBS-111-036, OBS-111-038, OBS-111-049. |
| OBS-111-T2-122 | CROSS_REGISTRY | 1Jo 4:10 | Yes — love produces mercy (1Jo 4:10 — OBS-111-049: divine love → propitiation; Eph 2:4 in SD004: God rich in mercy because of great love). Compassion produces the atoning act (Psa 78:38 — OBS-111-038: being compassionate, he atoned). Guilt/need produces mercy-seeking (SD002, OBS-111-047). The source characteristic is divine love; the proximate motivator is divine compassion; the occasion is human guilt/need. OBS-111-038, OBS-111-049. |
| OBS-111-T2-144 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | 1Jo 4:10 | Three key argument structures: (1) Jam 2:13 — bipartite argument: premise (judgment without mercy to those who show no mercy) → conclusion (mercy triumphs over judgment). Internal logic: mercy-extension determines mercy-reception; therefore mercy > judgment. (2) 1Jo 4:10 — chain argument: divine love (ground) → sending of Son (act) → propitiation (mechanism) → sin covered (result). Love is the motive; mercy/propitiation is the instrument. (3) Mat 18:23–34 — analogical argument: the king's cancellation of debt proportional to the servant's refusal to cancel a smaller debt; the conclusion ("should you not have had mercy?") is argued by proportion. OBS-111-049, OBS-111-055, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-064-T2-153 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Directional: forgiveness moves from God toward the offender (Psa 130:4 — forgiveness is "with" God and moves toward the one who calls); from the offended toward the offender (horizontal mode). Relational: forgiveness is constitutively relational — it requires at minimum two parties and a prior breach. Constitutional: forgiveness received constitutes the person differently — it produces love (Luk 7:47) and reverential fear (Psa 130:4), reshaping the inner-being orientation of the recipient. OBS-064-018, OBS-064-019, OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-155 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Four constituent modes (from v1 Q&A-017): (1) Covenantal-objective — external ritual mechanism producing declared forgiveness; (2) Divine relational-personal — personal declaratory act with inner access-postures (faith, repentance, confession); (3) Human horizontal — commanded release of inner claim against offender, from the heart; (4) Intercessory — seeking forgiveness on behalf of another, grounded in God's character not the community's merit. These four modes are layered — each assumes the prior modes are operative. OBS-064-014, OBS-064-015, OBS-064-016, OBS-064-031. |
| OBS-064-T2-156 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Forgiveness is the relational act of releasing a legitimate claim against an offender — the cancellation of a debt rather than its denial. It operates in four modes (covenantal-objective, divine relational-personal, human horizontal, intercessory), each sharing the single act-structure of releasing-a-hold. It is grounded in divine character (the offended party's disposition, not the offender's merit), produces two specific downstream inner states (love, reverence), and creates the relational condition for restored access. OBS-064-003, OBS-064-018, OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-160 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Five operational modes (expanding v1 Q&A-017 with the G0863 sub-entry analysis): (1) Covenantal-objective; (2) Divine relational-personal; (3) Human horizontal (commanded); (4) Intercessory; (5) Access-permitting (G0863I — the releasing/permitting mode that enables or blocks access — "let the children come to me," "do not forbid"). The fifth mode is distinct in direction: it does not concern debt-release but access-granting, though it shares the releasing act-structure. OBS-064-009, OBS-064-014. |
| OBS-064-T2-162 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Forgiveness has a verbal/declaratory mode — the pronouncement "your sins are forgiven" (Luk 7:47-48, Mar 2:5). This is a speech-act form: the declaration itself effects the forgiveness, not merely announces it. Jesus' forgiveness pronouncements function as sovereign declarations that produce the state they name. The horizontal commanded forgiveness may also have a declaratory component (verbal declaration to the offender), but "from your heart" (Mat 18:35) requires the inner-being act to precede or accompany the speech-act. OBS-064-022, OBS-064-031. |
| OBS-064-T2-163 | VERSE_PATTERN | Luk 7:47 | Two specific immediate inner states are named: (1) Love — Luk 7:47 establishes a quantity-proportional causal chain: "she loved much because she was forgiven much; he who is forgiven little loves little." Love is the immediate inner-being response to received forgiveness. (2) Reverential fear/awe — Psa 130:4 ("there is forgiveness with you, that you may be feared"). Encountering forgiveness as a divine quality produces reverence rather than presumption. OBS-064-020, OBS-064-021, OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-164 | VERSE_PATTERN | Luk 7:47 | The immediate response varies by mode and direction. Receiving forgiveness personally: love (Luk 7:47), relief of guilt-burden (implied by the "you are forgiven" pronouncement pattern). Encountering forgiveness as divine quality: reverential fear (Psa 130:4). Extending forgiveness: inner release of the claim in the withholder (implied by Mat 18:35 — heart-level release as the act itself). Withholding forgiveness: inner-life poison/torment (Mat 18:34, OBS-064-001). OBS-064-029, OBS-064-021. |
| OBS-064-T2-166 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Sustained forgiveness-received establishes: (1) Freed relational access to God (Jer 31:34 — the guilt-barrier removed permanently); (2) Love-orientation (Luk 7:47 — the love-response is proportional to the forgiveness received and presumably grows as understanding of the forgiveness deepens); (3) Worshipping capacity (Mat 5:24 — forgiveness as prerequisite for acceptable worship); (4) Capacity to extend forgiveness (Mat 18:35 — the logic implies received forgiveness generates the capacity to extend it). OBS-064-017, OBS-064-028, OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-167 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Yes — immediate response (love, reverence) gives way to sustained conditions (freed access to God, enabled worship, capacity to extend forgiveness). The sustained effect is characterological — the person who has genuinely received forgiveness is progressively formed into one who can genuinely extend it. OBS-064-029, OBS-064-032. |
| OBS-064-T2-169 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | What blocks reception: (1) Pride/self-sufficiency — the Pharisee of Luk 7:36-50 "forgiven little" because his self-assessment requires little forgiveness (OBS-064-029); (2) Prior unforgiveness in the withholder — Mat 6:14-15 implies that refusing to extend forgiveness creates a barrier to receiving it; (3) The outer limit of the unforgivable sin (Mar 3:29) — permanent closure of the receptive condition. OBS-064-029, OBS-064-032, OBS-064-027. |
| OBS-064-T2-195 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Luk 7:47 | Yes — Luk 7:47 establishes a constitutive movement: forgiveness received → love produced. The movement is from the state of being forgiven (relational-status change) through the inner being to the emergence of love-orientation. Jer 31:34 establishes: forgiveness → direct knowledge of God. The constitutional movement is from guilt-bearing (blocked relational condition) through forgiveness to freed inner access. OBS-064-017, OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-196 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Luk 7:47 | Sequence: guilt-bearing (blocked relational state) → forgiveness received (status change) → love emerges (inner-being response) → capacity to extend forgiveness (characterological formation). The sequence is directional and productive — each stage generates the next. OBS-064-017, OBS-064-029, OBS-064-032. |
| OBS-064-T2-198 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Forgiveness deepens and opens perception — specifically perception of divine character. Psa 130:4 (forgiveness encountered → reverential fear produced) implies that the encounter with forgiveness as a divine quality sharpens perception of who God is. The Pharisee's limited love (Luk 7:47 — "forgiven little, loves little") implies limited perception of the magnitude of the forgiveness — perception of the gift's magnitude determines the love-response. OBS-064-020, OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-199 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | The pattern: perception of forgiveness's magnitude determines the love-response. Shallow perception → shallow love. Deep perception → deep love. Forgiveness both requires and deepens perception of divine character. The perceptive faculty is both the gate to receiving forgiveness and the beneficiary of having received it. OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-201 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Forgiveness enables cognition — specifically, it removes the guilt-barrier to direct knowledge of God (Jer 31:34). It deepens self-cognition: accurate assessment of what one has been forgiven is the prerequisite for proportionate love-response (Luk 7:47). The withheld-forgiveness condition impairs cognition: the unforgiving withholder is cognitively trapped in accounting the offender's debt (Mat 18:23-35). OBS-064-017, OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-205 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Yes — deeply. Love (agapē) is the named downstream affective state produced by forgiveness received (Luk 7:47). Reverential fear (awe) is produced by encountering forgiveness as a divine quality (Psa 130:4). The visceral compassion (splagchnizomai — Mat 18:27) is the named affective precondition for extending forgiveness. Forgiveness therefore engages affect at both ends: as a prerequisite (compassion producing the act) and as an outcome (love and reverence produced by receiving the act). OBS-064-020, OBS-064-021, OBS-064-022, OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-206 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Forgiveness deepens affect in the receiving direction (love produced, Luk 7:47 — proportional to the depth of the forgiveness received) and deepens reverential fear (Psa 130:4). In the giving direction, forgiveness requires and expresses somatic compassion. Withholding forgiveness degrades affect — the torment-state of Mat 18:34 is an affective state of inner imprisonment. OBS-064-029, OBS-064-032. |
| OBS-064-T2-211 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Yes — both the forgiving act (agentive release of claim) and the seeking-forgiveness act (intercession — Dan 9:19 — agentive plea on behalf of others) involve agency. Jesus' forgiveness pronouncements (Luk 7:47-48) assert divine agency explicitly — the controversy passages in the Synoptics dispute who has the authority (agency) to forgive sins. OBS-064-015, OBS-064-022. |
| OBS-064-T2-215 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Forgiveness deepens moral evaluation — it requires the offended person to accurately assess both the reality of the offence (not denying it) and the decision to release the claim regardless. It also requires the recipient to accurately assess the magnitude of the forgiveness received (Luk 7:47 — proportionate love requires proportionate assessment). OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-226 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 7:47 | Yes — the primary and dominant relational direction in this registry. God is the exclusive subject of H5545 (salach). The NT forgiveness pronouncements (Luk 7:47-48, Mar 2:5) are divine-to-human. Eph 1:7 ("through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses") names the divine mechanism. Col 2:13 ("having forgiven us all our trespasses") — divine agentive act. OBS-064-003, OBS-064-022. |
| OBS-064-T2-244 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Both. Condition: forgiveness received transforms the person's standing before God (from guilt-bearing to guilt-free — the forensic condition changes). Orientation: the sustained receipt of forgiveness produces love-orientation (Luk 7:47) and reverential fear (Psa 130:4) as stable inner dispositions. OBS-064-029, OBS-064-020. |
| OBS-064-T2-246 | VERSE_PATTERN | Luk 7:47 | Sequence: (1) Offence/guilt present (prior condition); (2) Access-posture adopted (faith, repentance, confession — reception-preparation); (3) Forgiveness received (status-change); (4) Love-response generated (Luk 7:47); (5) Capacity to extend forgiveness formed (Mat 18:35 structural logic); (6) Extended forgiveness to others (horizontal expression). For the new covenant: steps 3 and 4 produce permanent new conditions (Jer 31:34 — known by God, sins remembered no more). OBS-064-017, OBS-064-022, OBS-064-029, OBS-064-032. |
| OBS-064-T2-247 | VERSE_PATTERN | Luk 7:47 | The sequence reveals forgiveness as a relational-transitional event — it moves the person from one inner condition (guilt-bearing, blocked access) through the act to a different inner condition (love-oriented, access-freed). Forgiveness is not a terminal state but a transitional act that produces further inner states. The "torment" of the unforgiver (Mat 18:34) reveals the alternative sequence when the transition is blocked: retained claim → inner imprisonment. OBS-064-001, OBS-064-029, OBS-064-032. |
| OBS-064-T2-248 | OBSERVATION | Luk 7:47 | Two mechanisms: (1) Declaratory mechanism — the forgiveness pronouncement effects a status change. God's declaration ("your sins are forgiven") or the human's inner release creates the new condition. (2) Atoning mechanism — the blood of Christ (Eph 1:7) is the structural ground that enables the declaration. The atonement mechanism is the basis; the declaration is the application. OBS-064-022, OBS-064-028. |
| OBS-064-T2-260 | VERSE_PATTERN | Luk 7:47 | Causal in key sequences: guilt → forgiveness is not merely sequential but addresses-and-resolves (forgiveness is the specific answer to guilt's condition). Forgiveness → love (Luk 7:47) is explicitly causal and quantitatively proportional. The sequence is not merely developmental but causally structured: guilt causally occasions forgiveness; forgiveness causally produces love. OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-263 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Luk 7:47 | Forgiveness is constituent of: reconciliation (R130 — Mat 5:24 shared anchor; reconciliation presupposes forgiveness as its inner ground); the new covenant (Jer 31:34 — forgiveness is one of the defining elements of the new covenant); love-formation (Luk 7:47 — forgiveness is constitutive of the love-response). OBS-064-017, OBS-064-028, OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-285 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | Luk 7:47 | Luk 7:47 argument structure: "her many sins have been forgiven — that is why she loved much; but one who is forgiven little loves little." The causal logic: magnitude of forgiveness → proportional love. This is a diagnostic-interpretive argument: Jesus uses the love visible in the woman's act to reveal the magnitude of her forgiveness. The argument structure is: love-visible → forgiveness-inferred; forgiveness-magnitude → love-magnitude (bidirectional inference). OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-064-T2-287 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | Luk 7:47 | Jer 31:34 is the architecturally complete verse — it names the divine action (I will forgive), the comprehensive scope (their iniquity), and the permanent cognitive-relational consequence (remember their sin no more). Psa 86:5 is the most complete divine character statement (good and forgiving and abounding in steadfast love). Luk 7:47 is the most developed inner-being consequence statement. No single verse holds all three — the primary anchor trio is Jer 31:34, Psa 86:5, and Luk 7:47. OBS-064-017, OBS-064-019, OBS-064-029. |
| OBS-099-T2-002 | OBSERVATION | Luke 6:35 | Scripture explicitly and extensively attributes kindness to God in three forms: (a) direct predication — "God is kind to the ungrateful and evil" (Luke 6:35); "the Lord is kind in all his works" (Psa 145:17 [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-095]); (b) superlative attribution — "abounding in steadfast love" (Exo 34:6), "steadfast love is better than life" (Psa 63:3 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-084]); (c) instrumental attribution — "God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance" (Rom 2:4, OBS-099-076). The significance for the human person: divine kindness is God's defining attribute; the call for human kindness is ontological grounding — to be kind is to be what the image-bearer was created to be. The absence of kindness is not merely behavioural failure but dimming of the image. |
| OBS-099-T2-021 | OBSERVATION | Luke 6:35 | Yes — three contextual variations: (a) Divine vs. human scope: God's chesed inexhaustible; human chesed creaturely and can be withdrawn [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-083, Q&A-008]. (b) Comfort vs. affliction: Job 6:4 (afflicting presence) vs. Psa 23:4 (comforting presence) — same term, different mode by context and divine purpose [OBS-099-067/066]. (c) Purposive vs. sustaining: Rom 2:4 — kindness aimed at repentance; Luke 6:35 — kindness to ungrateful without stated aim [OBS-099-076, OBS-099-078]. Purposive dimension present in some contexts; absent as explicit feature in others. |
| OBS-099-T2-049 | OBSERVATION | Col 3:12 | Yes — strongly. (a) Deliberate choice: Rut 3:10 — Ruth's kindness greater than the first — a choice when she did not have to [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-086]; 2Sa 10:2 — David resolved to show loyalty [OBS-099-071]; Gen 44:32 — Judah placed himself under obligation by choice [OBS-099-063]. (b) Love as volitional act: Mic 6:8 — "love kindness" — love in OT covenant context is a volitional commitment [OBS-099-052]. (c) Putting on: Col 3:12 — "put on... kindness" — deliberate clothing [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Kindness strongly engages the will; it is chosen, not merely felt. |
| OBS-099-T2-055 | OBSERVATION | Luke 6:35 | Most extensively evidenced direction in the registry. H2617A group 536-001 (180 relevant verses, dominant subject GOD [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-083]) documents the full range. (a) How: specific historical acts (Exo 15:13; 2Sa 7:15 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]); through his presence (Psa 23:4 [OBS-099-066]); through forgiveness (Exo 34:6–7); eschatologically through Christ (Eph 2:7 [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]). (b) Basis: covenantal (to those who love him — Exo 20:6) and unconditional in ultimate attribute (never ceases — Lam 3:22 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-085]; reaches ungrateful — Luke 6:35 [OBS-099-078]). Conditionality is in manifestation; attribute itself is unconditional. (c) What it reveals: God whose primary inner disposition toward the creature is committed faithful generous good will — chesed before, through, and after judgment. |
| OBS-099-T2-064 | OBSERVATION | Col 3:12 | Yes — central formative testimony. (a) The chasid formation arc: H2623 documents the person whose character is shaped by chesed over a lifetime — the outcome of sanctification in the chesed-direction [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. (b) Garment of kindness: Col 3:12 — "put on... kindness" as part of "putting on the new self" (context v.10) [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Sanctification language — kindness is part of new-self formation. (c) Fruit of the Spirit: Gal 5:22 — the Spirit's long-arc work produces chrēstotēs as stable fruit [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-092]. Kindness is both product and marker of sanctification progress. |
| OBS-099-T2-085 | OBSERVATION | Luke 6:35 | The evidence is not entirely silent on relational boundaries but is thin. Luke 6:35 — "love your enemies and do good... and you will be sons of the Most High, for he is kind to the ungrateful and the evil" — explicitly extends kindness beyond the deserving and beyond the covenant partner to include enemies and the ungrateful [OBS-099-078]. This is the most direct boundary-crossing statement in the evidence: kindness is not restricted to those who merit it or belong to the covenant community. The chesed evidence (Exo 20:6 — showing chesed to those who love God and keep his commandments) suggests covenantal conditionality in its sustained manifestation, but Luke 6:35 shows it reaching across that boundary in its initiating mode. The precise scope — how far the boundary extends and what distinguishes sustained chesed from initiating kindness — is not fully resolved from the evidence and requires Session D cross-registry examination. |

**SD pointers — direct (5)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-103-SD003 | MEDIUM | G0025 | Mat 5:44 — love of enemies co-occurs with prayer for persecutors and promise of sonship. Agapao + seeking + will + pray intersection. The enemy-love command may be the most cross-registry verse in the NT. Registries implicated: love (103), seeking (140), will (173), pray (212). Question: does the enemy-love command require a prior orientation of seeking God (140) and an act of the will (173) as its structural preconditions? |
| DIM-103-SD013 | MEDIUM | G0025 | Mat 5:44 — love of enemies also involves prayer for persecutors and good deeds and lending. The enemy-love complex shows love operating simultaneously across will (173), prayer (212), and action registers. Question: is the enemy-love command the programme's clearest example of a command that requires integration of all three levels of the inner person — affective orientation (love), volitional act (pray, do good), and cognitive reorientation (the rationale from divine character)? |
| DIM-103-SD026 | MEDIUM | G0025 | C20 cluster (strength/might/authority/dominion — Regs 187/198/197/199) shares all 8 dimensions with love and co-occurs in 69-105 verses. This may be a registry-breadth effect, but the co-occurrence is genuine and large. Question: does the biblical vocabulary connect love and power structurally? Is love the form that power takes in the kingdom of God, and power the form that love takes in creation? The 1Cor 13 sequence — love as greater than gifts of power and knowledge — suggests love and power are in deliberate tension in the NT. |
| DIM-103-SD027 | HIGH | G0025 | Surrender/yielding (Reg 156/180) has 33/4 co-occurrence/shared anchor records with love. The self-giving pattern of agapē (Joh 15:13 — greater love has no one than to lay down his life) and surrender (giving up the self) may be the same inner movement named from different angles: love as orientation, surrender as act. Question: is surrender the inner movement that makes love possible — does love require the prior surrender of self-assertion before it can give itself to another? |
| DIM-103-SD028 | MEDIUM | G0025 | Reg 73 (guilt) has 32 co-occurrences with love — unexpected and analytically significant. Luk 7:47 — 'she loved much because she was forgiven much' — establishes guilt/forgiveness as the prior state that love responds to. Question: is guilt the inner state that love-as-forgiveness-response resolves? If guilt and love are consistently co-present at the verse level, there may be a causal chain: guilt → received forgiveness → love as response. Cross-registry: love (103), guilt (73), forgiveness (64). |

### G3870 parakaleō — to plead/comfort

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G3870` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R043 desire · Legacy C-cluster: C04

4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 202
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=510` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
6. Mounce short-def: to ask, beg, plead; to comfort, encourage, exhort, urge; to call, invite

**Verses:** 105 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

| 602 | 510-001 | Term names the comfort directed toward the afflicted inner being — the act of comforting another in suffering, grief, or distress, and the inner state of being comforted |  |
| 603 | 510-002 | Term names the urgent plea or earnest appeal arising from inner need, urgency, or desperation — the inner state of the petitioner expressed outward |  |
| 604 | 510-003 | Term names the pastoral or apostolic appeal — the inner relational care and authority of the one urging another toward faithful conduct and inner transformation |  |
| 3600 | 510-003-a | Term names the pastoral or apostolic appeal — the inner relational care and authority of the one urging another toward faithful conduct, endurance, and inner transformation; grounded in the mercies of God and the authority of the gospel | Created by DIR-20260507-M05-004..010 |
| 3601 | 510-003-b | Term names the social or interpersonal request — inviting, urging, asking someone to do or refrain from something in the context of ordinary social or relational life, without the pastoral or apostolic authority dimension | Created by DIR-20260507-M05-004..010 |

| DIM-192-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | 2Cor 1:4 | Comfort vocabulary splits cleanly: God-given comfort (Spiritual/God-ward) and community-given comfort (Relational/Social). Paraklēsis spans both dimensions across its three groups. Session B should examine the structural relationship between divine and human comfort — the data suggests divine comfort both enables and models the human comfort that flows from it. |
| DIM-52-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | 1Cor 1:10 | The Greek division vocabulary in the division registry (schizō G4977, schisma G4978, merizō G3307, diamerizō G1266, meris G3310, meros G3313) shows a differentiated inner-being landscape: schisma/schizō name communal fracture over identity evaluation; merizō names internal volitional division; diamerizō names interpersonal fracture from ultimate allegiance; meris/meros name relational belonging and volitional orientation toward Christ. Session B should examine these terms in relation to each other and to the Hebrew portion vocabulary (chelek, cheleq, chalaq). |

### G0018 agathos — good

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G0018` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R065 generosity · Legacy C-cluster: C08
3. Sub-group: `M05-D` (Kindness and Goodness)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 505
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=881` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
6. Mounce short-def: one who does good

**Verses:** 90 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (5)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1096 | 881-001 | Term names goodness as the exclusive and defining attribute of God's inner being — the standard by which all human goodness is measured and found derivative |  |
| 1097 | 881-002 | Term names goodness as the inner character quality of the person — the good heart, good conscience, good treasure — from which good action necessarily flows |  |
| 1098 | 881-003 | Term names goodness as the inner moral orientation and activity that God commands and will judge — doing good, pursuing good, overcoming evil with good |  |
| 1099 | 881-004 | Term names goodness as the eschatological and providential good that God works for and toward — the good things to come, good hope, all things working for good |  |
| 1100 | 881-005 | Term names the inner conflict between the person's desire for good and their inability to perform it — the good I want but cannot do, the good that exposes the power of sin in the inner person |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (3)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-65-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW |  | agathos (G0018) groups in Reg 65 frame the inner being's relationship to goodness in four registers: (1) goodness as character source (good heart — Character/Disposition, 881-002), (2) goodness as moral command (do good — Moral/Conscience, 881-003), (3) goodness as inner conflict (want but cannot — Volitional/Will, 881-005), (4) goodness as divine attribute (only God is good — Theological/Divine-Human, 881-001). Group 881-005 is analytically most significant — the gap between inner desire for good and capacity to perform it is the heart of Pauline anthropology. Session B should examine how these four registers of goodness interact and what they reveal about the inner being's relationship to moral reality. |
| OBS-067-T2-118 | ROOT_FINDING | Mic 6:8, Gal 5:22 | Yes — vocabulary sharing is extensive. From the XREF structure: R103 (love) shares H2896A and G0019; R042 (delight) shares H2896A; R099 (kindness) shares G5544; R065 (generosity) shares G0019 (via agathos root family — G0015, G0016, G0017, G0018 all in R65 XREF). The 42 XREF terms in R67's inventory flow to multiple registries, confirming that the tov and agatho/chresto root families are shared across a wide range of programme registries. (OBS-067-OBS-018, OBS-067-OBS-019, SBF-067-Q019, SBF-067-Q124) |
| OBS-067-T2-119 | ROOT_FINDING | Gal 5:22 | Yes — root-level architecture is confirmed for both root families: TOV root family: H2896A (tov adjective, R67 OWNER) / H2895 (tov verbal — be pleasing, R103 XREF) / H2896B (tov adjective, various) / H2896C (to.vah — welfare, various) / H2898 (tuv — goodness nominal, R103). The TOV root generates terms distributed across R67, R103, and adjacent registries. AGATHO root family: G0019 (agathōsunē, R67 OWNER) / G0018 (agathos, R65 OWNER) / G0015 (agathopoieo, R65 XREF) / G0016 (agathopoiia, R65 XREF) / G0017 (agathopoios, R65 XREF) / G0014 (agathoergeō, R65 XREF) — all agatho- compounds distributed across R67 and R65. CHRESTO root family: G5544 (chrēstotēs, R67 OWNER) / G5543 (chrēstos, adjacent) / G5541 (chrēsteuomai, adjacent) / G5542 (chrēstologia, distortion form). (OBS-067-OBS-018, SBF-067-Q019, SP-067-001, SP-067-011) |

**Findings — via anchor verse (10 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| 112-F002 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 12:2 | The mind is a moral organ before it is an intellectual one. It can be debased, blinded, corrupted, renewed, and transferred (mind of Christ). Romans 12:2 presents renewal of the nous as the locus of personal transformation. |
| 182-F008 | TERM_BEHAVIOUR | Luk 12:19 | The soul's self-address in Psa 42-43 (the psalmist speaks to his own soul, diagnoses it, gives it a counter-command) is the OT's most psychologically sophisticated move. The person who can observe their own soul from outside and command it is more whole, not less — this reflexive capacity is a distinctive feature of the soul vocabulary. |
| DIM-29-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | 2Cor 9:8 | Both contentment terms name a stable inner condition independent of circumstances — a learned/acquired disposition of sufficiency, not a transient emotional state. Session B should examine whether contentment constitutes a distinct sub-dimension of Character/Disposition, and how it relates to the Philippians 4 context where it appears alongside joy and peace. |
| DIM-43-003 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Rom 7:18, Rom 7:19 | Group 513-001 names the divided inner will — the person wills good but does what they do not will (Romans 7 pattern). This is analytically foundational for understanding desire's relationship to action. Session B should give this sustained attention as a key inner-being mechanism. |
| DIM-52-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Luke 10:42 | The Greek division vocabulary in the division registry (schizō G4977, schisma G4978, merizō G3307, diamerizō G1266, meris G3310, meros G3313) shows a differentiated inner-being landscape: schisma/schizō name communal fracture over identity evaluation; merizō names internal volitional division; diamerizō names interpersonal fracture from ultimate allegiance; meris/meros name relational belonging and volitional orientation toward Christ. Session B should examine these terms in relation to each other and to the Hebrew portion vocabulary (chelek, cheleq, chalaq). |
| OBS-103-T2-008 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Mat 5:45 | The data provides an unusually direct answer. Joh 13:35 (OBS-103-129) — 'by this all people will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another' — makes love the externally legible mark of the divine image in the community. 1Jo 4:8 (via flag) — 'whoever does not love does not know God, for God is love' — states that lovelessness constitutes non-knowledge of God. The absence of love is not merely a moral deficiency; it is an epistemic and ontological condition: the person who does not love does not know God and therefore does not bear the divine image in its proper operation. Conversely, the presence of love — particularly love of enemies (Mat 5:44-45, OBS-103-144) — constitutes identity as a son/daughter of God. Love is thus the diagnostic marker of the divine image's condition: present and active = image bearing; absent or disordered = image distorted. |
| OBS-111-T2-123 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Jam 3:17 | Both directions are evidenced: (1) Mercy as constituent of wisdom: Jam 3:17 (mercy is named as a component of wisdom from above — OBS-111-025; SP-111-017). (2) Atonement as constituent of mercy: the atonement vocabulary is not a separate characteristic but the structural mechanism through which mercy operates — atonement is mercy's embodied mechanism (DIM-111-001, OBS-111-075). (3) Holiness as mercy's companion: the holiness-mercy axis within R111 (OBS-111-050, OBS-111-051) suggests holiness and mercy are constitutively related rather than external to each other. *New finding: mercy has both constituent status within wisdom (Jam 3:17) and constituent elements within itself (atonement as its mechanism, compassion as its motivation, holiness as its relational character).* OBS-111-025, OBS-111-050, OBS-111-075. |
| OBS-117-T2-095 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 3:17 | The data establishes several inner conditions for genuine peace-giving: (1) Having received peace: the apostolic blessing (Rom 1:7) is the outflow of the apostle's own experience of grace-and-peace from God; one extends what one has received — Joh 14:27's "my peace I give to you" is the paradigm: Christ gives what he IS; (2) Authentic inner orientation toward peace: Psa 120:7 ("I am for peace") — the person who genuinely extends peace is one whose fundamental orientation IS peace; the one whose heart is toward war cannot genuinely extend peace (Psa 28:3 — speaking peace while evil is in the heart is its corruption); (3) Seeking the other's welfare: Jer 29:7 — the extension of peace to the exile city requires an inner orientation of care for those who might be regarded as enemies; (4) Wisdom: G1516 eirēnikos — peaceableness is the fruit of heavenly wisdom (Jam 3:17) — wisdom is the inner condition enabling peaceful extension. |
| OBS-117-T2-140 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 3:17 | Yes — multiple person-type terms: (1) Ish sha.lom (Psa 37:37 — "man of peace") — the person whose character is peace; the construct chain ish + sha.lom makes peace the defining characteristic of the person; (2) Ani sha.lom (Psa 120:7 — "I am for peace/I am peace") — the most concentrated self-designation; sha.lom as personal identity not merely possession; (3) Anshei sha.lom (Jer 20:10, 38:22 — "men of sha.lom") — plural person-type term; men defined by their sha.lom-bond; used notably in the betrayal context; (4) G1516 eirēnikos — the peaceable person, one whose character is eirēnē (Jam 3:17, Heb 12:11); (5) Eirēnopoios (G1518 — "peacemaker," Mat 5:9) — owned by R103 but in the peace vocabulary's scope: the person whose characteristic act is making peace. The person-type vocabulary is rich: the registry defines not only the characteristic but the person whose nature is the characteristic. |
| OBS-117-T2-146 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 3:17 | The peace registry draws from all major biblical literary forms: (1) Prophecy (dominant in OT): Isa 9:6/7, 26:3, 30:15, 53:5, 54:10, Jer 29:11, Mic 5:5, Eze 34:25, 37:26 — the prophetic corpus carries the eschatological and messianic peace evidence and the refusal-of-peace pattern; (2) Psalms: Psa 4:8, 28:3, 32:3, 34:14, 62:5, 85:8, 119:165, 120:7, 131:2 — carries the experiential/devotional inner-peace evidence; (3) Wisdom: Pro 3:2/17, Ecc 9:17, Jam 3:17/18 — carries the peace-wisdom correlation; (4) Epistle: Rom 5:1, 8:6, Phili 4:7, Gal 5:22, 1Th 5:23, Col 1:20, Eph 2:14 — carries the NT theological synthesis; (5) Torah/Narrative: Num 6:26, Deu 28:65, Deu 29:19, Gen 28:21 — foundational covenant structures; (6) Lament: Lam 3:17 — the peaceless-soul at depth. The breadth of literary form is among the widest in the programme — peace is not a genre-specific concept but spans the full biblical literary spectrum. |

**SD pointers — direct (1)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| SP-067-011 | HIGH |  | The programme assigns G0018 (agathos — adjective, 90 OWNER verses in R65 generosity) and G0019 (agathōsunē — abstract noun, 4 OWNER verses in R67 goodness) to separate registries on the basis of the adjective/abstract-noun distinction (doing good vs being/virtue of goodness). The verse count differential (90 vs 4) is stark. Session D should test whether this boundary holds under the verse evidence — specifically whether the R65 agathos corpus includes verses that engage goodness as inner disposition (R67 territory) and vice versa. Evidence: XREF structure showing 6/9 XREF terms flowing to R65; G0019 Gal 5:22 glossed as 'generosity' (R65 semantic range). Source: OBS-067-OBS-004. |

### G1577 ekklēsia — assembly

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G1577` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R019 calling · Legacy C-cluster: C19
3. Sub-group: `M05-BOUNDARY` (Boundary)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 193
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=4833` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
6. Mounce short-def: church, congregation, assembly; a group of people gathered together. It can refer to the OT assembly of believers (<ref='Act.7.38'>Act.7:38</ref>), or a riotous mob (<ref='Act.19.32'>Act.19:32</ref>), but usually to a Christian assembly, a church: as a totality (<ref='Eph.3.10'>Eph 3:10</ref>), or in a specific locale (<ref='Col.4.15'>Col. 4:15</ref>). In the NT a church is never a building or meeting place

**Verses:** 85 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

### G0026 agapē — love

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G0026` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R103 love · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-A` (Love)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 133
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=562` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`
6. Mounce short-def: love, in the NT usually the active love of God for his Son and his people, and the active love his people are to have for God, each other, and even enemies; love feast, the common meal shared by Christians in connection with church meetings

**Verses:** 68 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (3)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1542 | 562-001 | Term names God's love as the inner source and ground of all love — poured into hearts, demonstrated in Christ's death, inseparable from the believer |  |
| 1543 | 562-002 | Term names love as the defining inner virtue of the believer — the fruit of the Spirit, the greatest commandment, the fulfilment of the law, that which never ends |  |
| 1544 | 562-003 | Term names mutual love among believers as the communal inner orientation — the mark of discipleship, the bond of the community |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (1)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-103-T2-109 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Lam 3:22, Song 2:16, Isa 49:15, 1Cor 13:4 | Primary Hebrew terms and root meanings: **chesed** (H2617A) — root implies binding loyalty, covenant-fidelity; possibly related to the idea of steadfastness or bending toward; the most theologically laden term in the OT love vocabulary. **ahavah** (H0160) — root ahav (to breathe after, to desire) — implying love as a form of directed longing or desire. **racham** (H7355) / **rachamim** (H7356B) — root rechem (womb) — love as womb-like envelopment and tender compassion; the most somatically grounded root. **dod** (H1730G/H) — root unclear; used for lover/beloved in the Song — the erotic-relational vocabulary. Primary Greek terms: **agapē** (G0026) / **agapaō** (G0025) — of uncertain pre-classical etymology; LXX/NT coinage whose meaning is constituted by Scripture rather than importing a prior secular meaning (OBS-103-016). **phileō** (G5368) / **philia** (G5373) / **filos** (G5384) — root from philos (friend); classical Greek for natural affectionate love. Root meanings together reveal: love spans willed-covenant (chesed), longing-desire (ahavah), somatic-compassion (racham), erotic-beloved (dod), self-giving-willed (agapē), and natural-affection (phileō). |

**Findings — via anchor verse (159 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| 182-F010 | VERSE_PATTERN | Phi 2:2 | The soul is constitutively relational: to God (Psa 42:1, 62:1, 63:1), to other souls (1Sa 18:1, Act 4:32), to its own orientation (dipsuchos — inner division). Soul-to-soul bonding (sumpsuchos, isopsuchos) documents genuine union of orientation between persons — community is possible when souls share direction. |
| DIM-97-003 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Gal 5:22 | Group 378-001 (chara — abiding inner state) is assigned Character/Disposition rather than Affective/Emotional because it names a stable characterising inner quality. This parallels gratitude in Registry 69. Both registries produce at least one group where the inner state is stable and dispositional rather than episodic. Session B should examine whether there is a category of joy-as-character-quality distinct from joy-as-affective-experience. |
| DIM-192-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Phili 2:1 | Comfort vocabulary splits cleanly: God-given comfort (Spiritual/God-ward) and community-given comfort (Relational/Social). Paraklēsis spans both dimensions across its three groups. Session B should examine the structural relationship between divine and human comfort — the data suggests divine comfort both enables and models the human comfort that flows from it. |
| OBS-067-T2-003 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Gal 5:22 | The verse evidence indicates that goodness as a created characteristic equips the human person for three interrelated purposes: (1) Moral orientation — to recognise, assess, and align conduct with what is genuinely beneficial (884-002, 884-004, 884-006). Mic 6:8 defines this as doing justice, loving kindness, walking humbly — the equipped person has the capacity to live in right orientation toward God and others. (2) Relational connection — to receive God's goodness and extend it toward others (884-001, 884-002, 886-002). Rom 15:14 names the goodness-filled person as one "able to instruct one another" — goodness equips for community. (3) Volitional alignment — to prefer and choose what is genuinely good (884-008). The volitional-preference idiom's 40 verses show goodness functioning as the criterion of the will's proper operation. The three purposes converge: the person equipped with goodness has the capacity to be good (character), do good (conduct), and choose good (volition). (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-027, OBS-067-OBS-039, OBS-067-OBS-040) |
| OBS-067-T2-004 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Gal 5:22 | The evidence supports both, distinguished by term. Gen 1:31 (884-007) establishes goodness as pre-fall and pre-relational — the Creator pronounces creation "very good" before any human moral life begins. This is goodness as original created quality, not a response to fallenness. The agathōsunē coinage (G0019, 885-001), however, is pneumatologically concentrated — a Spirit-produced fruit listed in Gal 5:22. The Spirit-production context implies the fallen condition: the natural human condition is characterised by complete absence of goodness (Rom 3:12), so Spirit-produced goodness is explicitly a redemptive response. H2896A in 884-002 (human moral character) and 884-003 (experiential good) could reflect both original design (the human created to be and experience good) and the ongoing challenge of maintaining that orientation under fallen conditions. The registry carries both registers: goodness as created design (Gen 1 ontological goodness, doxological goodness of 884-001) and goodness as restored/Spirit-produced capacity (885-001, 886-002). (OBS-067-OBS-036, OBS-067-OBS-046, OBS-067-OBS-048) |
| OBS-067-T2-005 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Gal 5:22 | The evidence is suggestive but not fully explicit. 2Th 1:11 ("may God fulfil every resolve for good and every work of faith by his power") implies an eschatological completeness — the resolve for good is present now, but its fulfilment is anticipated as God's completing act. Eph 2:7 places God's display of chrēstotēs "in the coming ages" — goodness has a future-facing dimension of fullness. Gal 5:22 as Spirit-fruit connects goodness to the present work of the Spirit that anticipates the fullness of Spirit-transformation. However, the data does not contain a verse that explicitly narrates goodness as moving toward eschatological fullness in the way that glory or resurrection does. The eschatological trajectory is implied by the pneumatological framework (Spirit-production → Spirit-completion) but not explicitly named. This is a partial gap. (OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-016) |
| OBS-067-T2-007 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Gal 5:22 | The evidence supports genuine sharing of the same lexical and moral category — tov names both God's goodness and human goodness without semantic differentiation in H2896A. Psa 119:68 addresses God as good and then asks God to teach statutes so the human can also know and do good — the same category of goodness applies to both. G5544 (chrēstotēs) is explicitly shared: it names God's inner disposition (886-001, Rom 11:22; Eph 2:7) and the Spirit-produced quality of the believer (886-002, Gal 5:22; Col 3:12) using the identical term. This is not merely analogy — the NT explicitly frames human chrēstotēs as Spirit-produced, meaning the divine goodness is the active source of its human expression (not merely the pattern for it). The relationship is participatory: human goodness participates in divine goodness rather than merely imitating it. However, Gen 1:31 (the creative-pronouncement group, 884-007) has no human analogue — only God can evaluate and declare creation good in that ontological-constitutive sense. (OBS-067-OBS-017, OBS-067-OBS-037, OBS-067-OBS-045) |
| OBS-067-T2-012 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Gal 5:22 | H2896A (tov): the primary Hebrew term is an adjective of extremely wide semantic range. At the definitional level, it names what is genuinely and concretely beneficial, pleasing, and right — a quality observable in persons, things, actions, words, and states. It does not begin with an abstract ethical definition; it begins with the quality of things that genuinely satisfy and benefit. G0019 (agathōsunē): a NT-era abstract noun derived from agathos (good). At the definitional level, it names goodness as a stable inner virtue — a characterological quality that the NT isolates from the broader agathos adjective to name as a specific inner-being phenomenon. G5544 (chrēstotēs): derived from chrēstos (useful, serviceable, good). At the definitional level it names a relational quality — the disposition of generous goodwill and kindness — that is the outward-facing expression of inner goodness. The three terms thus reveal, even before lexical analysis, that goodness operates at multiple registers: experiential-phenomenological (tov), characterological-virtuous (agathōsunē), and relational-dispositional (chrēstotēs). (OBS-067-OBS-007, OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-009, OBS-067-OBS-088) |
| OBS-067-T2-014 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Gal 5:22 | Goodness is primarily a quality — a settled characterological property of the inner being. H2896A functions predominantly as an adjective, naming what someone or something is. Agathōsunē as an abstract noun confirms this: it names the virtue, not the act. However, the registry's 12 distinct modes (OBS-067-OBS-048) reveal that goodness also operates as: a disposition (chrēstotēs — the inner orientation of generous goodwill, 886-001 and 886-002); a condition (884-009 — the inner well-being state, shalom-condition); and an evaluative act (884-007 — the divine pronouncement-of-good as a creative-constitutive act; 884-004 — the cognitive comparative assessment). The primary kind is quality, but goodness is one of the more multi-modal characteristics in the programme — it operates across quality, disposition, condition, and evaluative act depending on context and term. (OBS-067-OBS-048, OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-017) |
| OBS-067-T2-015 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Gal 5:22 | Goodness is structurally complex — it has constituent elements at first encounter. DIM-67-001 (resolved via OBS-022, OBS-023, OBS-048) confirmed that the 12 modes of operation are genuinely distinct inner-being phenomena. The constituent elements at definitional level are: (1) the ontological pole — what God is in his being (divine goodness as character attribute); (2) the moral-character pole — what the human person is in their inner life (human goodness as quality); (3) the experiential pole — what is genuinely good for the person (goodness as the content of genuine satisfaction and benefit); (4) the volitional pole — what the person chooses and prefers (goodness as the criterion of the will). These four poles are constitutive of goodness as a category. They are unified theologically (all derive from and respond to God's goodness) but they are analytically distinct phenomena. (OBS-067-OBS-022, OBS-067-OBS-023, OBS-067-OBS-048) |
| OBS-067-T2-016 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Gal 5:22 | Best working description: Goodness is a characterological quality of the inner being — constitutionally located at the level of moral character and disposition — that engages moral evaluation, volition, and relational capacity simultaneously. It originates in God's own character and is received, expressed, and cultivated in the human person through encounter with divine goodness, Spirit-production, and volitional alignment with what is genuinely beneficial. Its impact on the person is threefold: it orients the will toward what is genuinely good (volitional alignment); it shapes the character to be and do good toward others (moral-relational formation); and it produces inner stability when rooted in nearness to God (experiential well-being). When absent, the person's will is misdirected, their moral assessment is inverted, and their inner stability is fragile. The registry description's formulation — "structurally beneficial... not just pleasing or approved but actually doing good in the deep sense" — accurately captures the ontological weight of the term. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-029, OBS-067-OBS-048) |
| OBS-067-T2-019 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Gal 5:22 | From the data: Goodness is not merely pleasant or approved — the registry description explicitly names this. Tov's aesthetic sense (pleasant, beautiful) is part of its range but goodness-as-inner-characteristic is the moral-constitutive register, not the merely pleasing. Goodness is not merely outward action — the programme's structural decision to assign G0015 (agathopoieo — to do good) to R65 (generosity) and retain agathōsunē in R67 reflects this: the act of doing good is adjacent to but distinct from the inner quality of goodness. Goodness is not identical to love (R103), kindness (R99), or delight (R42), though it shares vocabulary and anchor verses with each. The precise boundary between goodness and love at Mic 6:8 ("to love kindness" — where love-of-kindness is listed as one expression of what is good), and between goodness and kindness (R99 shares G5544), requires Session D cross-registry boundary work. The data names the proximity but does not fully draw the line. (OBS-067-OBS-018, OBS-067-OBS-090) |
| OBS-067-T2-021 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Gal 5:22 | Yes — three axes of operational variation are evidenced. (1) Subject: goodness operates differently when God is subject (ontological-creative, covenantal, dispositional) vs human subject (moral-character, volitional, experiential). (2) Direction: when goodness operates God→human (divine generosity, covenantal promise, chrēstotēs) it is initiatory and enabling; when it operates human→God (praise, trust, walking with God) it is responsive; when it operates human→human (kindness, justice, instruction) it is expressive. (3) Constitutional level: agathōsunē (885-001, Volition/04) operates at the level of resolve and Spirit-enabled virtue; chrēstotēs (886-002, Moral Character/05) operates at the level of settled relational disposition; H2896A in 884-003 operates at the experiential-orientation level. The operational differences map to the 12 modes but cluster into these three axes. (OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-017, OBS-067-OBS-048) |
| OBS-067-T2-028 | VERSE_PATTERN | Gal 5:22 | Three enabling inner conditions converge (SBF-067-Q014): (1) Proximity/orientation toward God — Psa 73:28 ("it is good to be near God"). The inner condition of nearness, of having God as one's refuge, enables reception of what is genuinely good. (2) Fear of the Lord — Pro 15:16 ("better is a little with the fear of the Lord"). The inner posture of reverential dependence is the ground of receiving the good. (3) Spirit-filling — Gal 5:22 / Rom 15:14. The Spirit's presence in the inner being produces goodness and enables its reception and expression. These three are not independent conditions — they converge on a single posture: the person oriented toward God, filled by his Spirit, whose inner life has been reoriented toward the source of goodness. Additionally, trust/faith is named as the condition for ongoing experience of divine chrēstotēs (Rom 11:22 — "provided you continue in his kindness"). (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-029, SBF-067-Q014) |
| OBS-067-T2-033 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Gal 5:22 | Secondary dimensions confirmed: Cognition (03) — 884-004 (comparative wisdom evaluation) and 884-006 (moral verdict assessment) both require the cognitive evaluation faculty. Volition (04) — 884-008 (volitional preference idiom, 40 verses) and 885-001 (Spirit-produced goodness as resolve, 2Th 1:11). The secondary dimensions do not compete with the primary — they represent the multi-modal nature of the characteristic. Moral Character is primary because it characterises the person's inner being as good; Cognition is secondary because the moral evaluation faculty is engaged in assessing what is good; Volition is secondary because goodness both criteria the will and reconstitutes it. The WARN item #3 (no dim_review record) is noted: these assignments are based on Stage 2a observation and are analytically grounded but have not passed a formal dimension review process. (OBS-067-OBS-011, OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-014, OBS-067-OBS-016) |
| OBS-067-T2-034 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Gal 5:22 | Spirit-level location is confirmed through the pneumatological framework of the NT evidence. Gal 5:22 names agathōsunē and chrēstotēs as "fruit of the Spirit" — the Spirit is the active producing agent and goodness is the fruit produced. The locus of Spirit-production is the human spirit/inner being. 2 Cor 6:6 lists chrēstotēs alongside "the Holy Spirit" in a single phrase, further linking the characteristic's operation to the Spirit's presence. Rom 15:14 ("full of goodness") implies an infilling — a Spirit-level saturation of the inner being with goodness. The Hebrew side does not use "spirit" language directly for tov but the covenantal-renewal context of Eze 36:31 is followed by Eze 36:26–27 (God's Spirit placed within) — goodness recognised and received is structurally connected in the Ezekiel context to Spirit-indwelling. The spirit-level location is confirmed for NT goodness and implied for the covenantal renewal context. (OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-025) |
| OBS-067-T2-035 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Gal 5:22 | For Spirit-produced goodness (885-001, 886-002): yes — the Spirit is the originating agent. For the Hebrew H2896A doxological and covenantal groups (884-001, 884-005): the origin is God himself, whose goodness is encountered and received by the human inner being. The characteristic does not originate in the human spirit unaided — in all cases the origin is external to the person (God's character, God's Spirit, God's word). The human spirit/inner being is the site of reception, not generation. This structural finding — that goodness is not self-generated — is consistent across all 12 modes. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-039) |
| OBS-067-T2-036 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Gal 5:22 | Spirit-level location reveals that goodness, in its fullest NT expression, is not a surface behaviour or a cultivated habit — it is a deep constitutional transformation produced at the level where the Spirit operates in the person. The contrast with the fallen condition (Rom 3:12 — complete absence of goodness in the unregenerate inner being) confirms that genuine goodness requires Spirit-level intervention. The NT's pneumatological concentration of goodness (agathōsunē as Pauline coinage, chrēstotēs as Spirit-fruit) signals that the deepest form of goodness is not achieved but infused — it comes from the deepest level of divine engagement with the human inner being. This implies that human attempts to produce goodness without Spirit-engagement will produce the surface form — the Haman problem (Est 5:9) — rather than the genuine article. (OBS-067-OBS-046, OBS-067-OBS-041, OBS-067-OBS-039) |
| OBS-067-T2-048 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Gal 5:22 | Origin is consistently external to the person — received from God and bestowed through the Spirit. Three origin paths: (1) Divine character as origin — God is good and goodness flows from his character (Psa 119:68; 884-001); (2) Covenantal word as origin — God's good word is spoken and received in the human inner being (Jos 23:14; 884-005); (3) Spirit-production as origin — agathōsunē and chrēstotēs are Spirit-fruit (Gal 5:22; 885-001, 886-002). The key negative evidence: Rom 3:12 names the complete absence of goodness in the unregenerate condition — confirming that goodness is not naturally generated from within. The origin is not generational (no evidence for generational goodness transmission as a constitutional mechanism, though the covenantal promise "to thousands" implies relational continuity). (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-039, OBS-067-OBS-046) |
| OBS-067-T2-049 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Gal 5:22 | The origin is formally singular (God as ultimate source) but operationally multiple in its delivery mechanisms. The ultimate origin is God's own goodness — this is singular and consistent. The delivery mechanisms are: God's character encountered (experiential), God's word received (covenantal), God's Spirit infused (pneumatological). These three mechanisms are not competing origins but three modes through which the singular divine source of goodness reaches the human inner being. This is analytically significant: the programme's 12 modes of goodness all trace back to the same originating source (God's goodness) but through different delivery paths. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-039) |
| OBS-067-T2-050 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Gal 5:22 | The ultimate origin (God's goodness) does not change. What changes is the delivery mechanism and the register of goodness received. In the Hebrew experiential register (884-003), the origin reaches the person through encounter and nearness. In the covenantal register (884-005), through God's word and promise-keeping. In the NT pneumatological register (885-001, 886-002), through the Spirit's production. These represent a progressive clarification and concentration of how goodness is delivered — from encounter and word in the OT to Spirit-production in the NT — but the source is constant. What shifts is not the origin but the mechanism of delivery and the specificity of naming. The NT's pneumatological precision is a clarification, not a change of origin. (OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-099) |
| OBS-067-T2-051 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Gal 5:22 | Constitutional movement is present and traceable. The primary movement is: Spirit-level origin (Spirit-production, Gal 5:22) → soul-level reception and orientation (Lam 3:25; Psa 73:28 — the soul seeks and is satisfied) → heart-level engagement (knowing in hearts, Jos 23:14; glad of heart, Est 5:9) → outward expression in conduct (Mic 6:8 — doing justice; Rom 15:14 — instructing others). This is a top-down and outward movement: from the Spirit's action in the innermost level, through the soul's orientation and the heart's response, to visible conduct and community expression. The inverse movement (body feeding back to soul) is not strongly evidenced for goodness. The constitutional movement is consistently inward-to-outward. (OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-039) |
| OBS-067-T2-052 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Gal 5:22 | The sequence is: (1) Divine source acts (God's goodness, Spirit's production, God's word) → (2) Spirit-level reception in the inner being (Spirit-fruit, infilling with goodness) → (3) Soul reorientation (Psa 73:28 — repositioned toward God; Lam 3:25 — the soul seeking) → (4) Heart engagement (knowing, gladness, moral recognition) → (5) Volitional alignment (choosing good, willing submission, Jer 26:14) → (6) Outward expression (conduct, justice, instruction of others). This is a formation sequence, not an instantaneous transformation. The 2Th 1:11 language ("fulfil every resolve for good") implies that step 5 (volitional resolve) is followed by step 6 (completion by God's power) — the full sequence requires both human resolve and divine completion. (OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-039, OBS-067-OBS-040, SBF-067-Q031) |
| OBS-067-T2-065 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Gal 5:22 | Yes — Volition (04) is a confirmed secondary dimension. The volitional engagement is extensive. 884-008 (40 verses, volitional preference idiom, anchor Jer 26:14) is the single largest group: the "good-in-one's-eyes" / "what seems good to you" formula names goodness as the predicate of volitional choice and evaluative agreement. 885-001 (Spirit-produced goodness as resolve): agathōsunē in 2Th 1:11 is "every resolve for good" — the Spirit enables the will toward goodness. Goodness engages volition both as the criterion (what the will should choose) and as the reconstituted capacity (Spirit-enabled resolve toward goodness). Two groups carry Volition: 884-008 (Hebrew volitional-preference) and 885-001 (Greek Spirit-enabled resolve). (OBS-067-OBS-014, OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-039, OBS-067-OBS-040) |
| OBS-067-T2-078 | VERSE_PATTERN | Gal 5:22 | Goodness deeply enables and integrates conscientiousness. The Spirit-produced goodness (885-001, 886-002) provides the inner motivational and characterological ground from which conscientious action flows — it is not merely adherence to a standard but an inner quality that produces sustained integrated responsiveness. The six-step constitutional movement identified at Q&A-064 is itself a description of conscientiousness being developed from Spirit-origin through soul, heart, will, and into sustained action. The absent or distorted form impairs conscientiousness: Rom 3:12's "no one does good" names the condition of total disintegration — the absence of goodness produces the collapse of conscientious moral action.
