### H0160 a.ha.vah — love

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H0160` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R103 love · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-A` (Love)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 38
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=537` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`

**Verses:** 35 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (3)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1578 | 537-001 | Term names God's love for his people — the covenantal inner disposition of steadfast love that persists through and exceeds all human response |  |
| 1579 | 537-002 | Term names the intense mutual love between persons — including Jonathan's love for David and the burning love of the Song |  |
| 1580 | 537-003 | Term names love as moral loyalty and kindness — the inner orientation of faithfulness between persons that mirrors divine chesed |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (1)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-103-T2-109 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Lam 3:22, Song 2:16, Isa 49:15, 1Cor 13:4 | Primary Hebrew terms and root meanings: **chesed** (H2617A) — root implies binding loyalty, covenant-fidelity; possibly related to the idea of steadfastness or bending toward; the most theologically laden term in the OT love vocabulary. **ahavah** (H0160) — root ahav (to breathe after, to desire) — implying love as a form of directed longing or desire. **racham** (H7355) / **rachamim** (H7356B) — root rechem (womb) — love as womb-like envelopment and tender compassion; the most somatically grounded root. **dod** (H1730G/H) — root unclear; used for lover/beloved in the Song — the erotic-relational vocabulary. Primary Greek terms: **agapē** (G0026) / **agapaō** (G0025) — of uncertain pre-classical etymology; LXX/NT coinage whose meaning is constituted by Scripture rather than importing a prior secular meaning (OBS-103-016). **phileō** (G5368) / **philia** (G5373) / **filos** (G5384) — root from philos (friend); classical Greek for natural affectionate love. Root meanings together reveal: love spans willed-covenant (chesed), longing-desire (ahavah), somatic-compassion (racham), erotic-beloved (dod), self-giving-willed (agapē), and natural-affection (phileō). |

**Findings — via anchor verse (29 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-103-T2-001 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 31:3 | The verse evidence reveals that love is not merely an attribute God exercises but a characterisation of what God is. The divine self-declaration formula at Exo 34:6 (OBS-103-069) names rachum (compassionate) + chesed (steadfast love) as the first two inner attributes God proclaims of himself — love leads the divine self-portrait. Jer 31:3 names God's love as 'everlasting' (ahavat olam) — a qualifier that exceeds any human love-form (OBS-103-093). Song 8:6 names love's flame as 'the very flame of the Lord' (shalhevetyah) — grounding love's intensity in the divine nature itself (OBS-103-096). The data culminates in the NT claim (1Jo 4:8 — God is love), carried in the registry as PH2-103-004 and evidenced structurally by the entire divine love vocabulary across the data. The characteristic being imaged in the human person is therefore not a quality God has alongside others but God's own constitutive being-as-love. |
| OBS-103-T2-010 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 31:3 | The evidence shows both directions operating but with a clear asymmetry. In the OT prophetic tradition: human marital/parental love → divine covenantal love (the human type points toward and is fulfilled in the divine). In the NT: divine love → human love (the divine instance establishes the pattern for the human: 'as I have loved you' — Joh 13:34; 'we love because he first loved us' — 1Jo 4:19). The asymmetry is that in the NT the direction inverts: the human type has served its purpose; the divine original now directly constitutes the human instance. The love-structure of the inner being therefore participates in a typological movement across the canon: created human love → figures divine love → divine love in Christ becomes the source and standard of human love in the new covenant. |
| OBS-103-T2-017 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Pro 10:12 | The structural opposite is hatred (sane — H8130, 139 active verses, OWNER term, OBS-103-030). Sane is embedded in R103 as an OWNER term — the programme treats hate as constitutive of the love vocabulary's boundary, not external to it. Lev 19:17 ('you shall not hate your brother in your heart') places hate as the inner alternative to love of neighbour (OBS-103-114). Pro 10:12 ('hatred stirs up strife, love covers all offences,' OBS-103-097) places them as causal-opposite forces with opposed social effects. The secondary structural opposite at the ultimate level is enmity with God: friendship with the world is enmity (echthra) with God (Jam 4:4, OBS-103-058). But hatred is the primary inner-being opposite — it is the same inner mechanism as love (directed orientation) running in the opposed direction. |
| OBS-103-T2-023 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Zep 3:17 | The data evidences three immediate response patterns, each associated with a primary love-encounter type: (1) **Responsive love** — 1Jo 4:19: the immediate response to receiving divine love is love in return. Love received generates love given — the response is the same characteristic activated in the receiver. (2) **Rest/stillness** — Zep 3:17 (OBS-103-094): God's love toward Israel produces quieting — the immediate inner response to the experience of being loved is rest, not striving. Psa 127:2 (OBS-103-084): being-beloved produces effortless sleep (rest as divine love-gift). (3) **Grief as love-evidence** — Joh 11:36 (OBS-103-149), 2Sa 1:26 (OBS-103-095), Joh 21:17 (OBS-103-153): the immediate response to love's loss or to love's cost is grief — the inner collapse or distress that reveals the depth of the prior love. This is not a response to love per se but to love's absence or cost, and it is consistently love's most visible immediate evidence. |
| OBS-103-T2-024 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Zep 3:17 | The response varies by the type of love-encounter. When divine love is received: the immediate response is love-in-return (1Jo 4:19) or rest (Zep 3:17, Psa 127:2). When love is expressed at cost (forgiveness-love, enemy-love): the immediate response in the giver is not named directly. When love is lost: the immediate response is grief (Song 5:6, OBS-103-062; 2Sa 1:26, OBS-103-095). When the beloved is encountered: the immediate response is delight and longing (Song 2:16, OBS-103-061; Luk 15:20 — the father's compassion-movement at sight of the returning son, OBS-103-057). The variation is systematic, not random: the type of encounter determines the response type. There is no single invariant immediate response to love — the response depends on the direction of the encounter (giving vs receiving vs losing vs encountering). |
| OBS-103-T2-027 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Zep 3:17 | Yes — the distinction is clear. The immediate response to love is primarily experiential: rest/stillness (Zep 3:17), responsive love (1Jo 4:19), or grief (Song 5:6). The sustained effect is primarily constitutional and communal: character formation (Pro 3:3), identity-establishment (agapētos), community legibility (Joh 13:35), and soul-satisfaction (Jer 31:14). The immediate response is felt; the sustained effect is what the person becomes and what the community is constituted as. This distinction maps onto the programme's spirit-soul boundary: the immediate response operates at the soul-affective level; the sustained effect operates at the spirit-character level and the relational-community level. |
| OBS-103-T2-045 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Zep 3:17 | Extensively. Joy (Psa 16:11, OBS-103-112; Zep 3:17, OBS-103-094); grief (Song 5:6, OBS-103-062; Joh 11:36, OBS-103-149; 2Sa 1:26, OBS-103-095); rest/stillness (Zep 3:17, OBS-103-094; Psa 127:2, OBS-103-084); delight (Song 2:16, OBS-103-061; Pro 5:19, OBS-103-099); compassion-movement (Luk 15:20, OBS-103-057; 1Ki 3:26, OBS-103-105); yearning (Jer 31:20, OBS-103-117); anguish at separation (Song 5:6, OBS-103-062). Love's emotional palette is among the richest in the programme. Love does not suppress emotion — it generates, focuses, and intensifies the inner affective life. |
| OBS-103-T2-046 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Zep 3:17, 2Sa 1:26, Pro 5:19 | Produced by love: joy and fullness of pleasure (Psa 16:11, OBS-103-112) — love in the presence of the beloved generates joy. Rest and quieting (Zep 3:17, OBS-103-094) — being loved by God produces inner stillness. Yearning (Jer 31:20, OBS-103-117) — God's love for Ephraim expressed as inner yearning for the wayward. Grief (2Sa 1:26, OBS-103-095; Song 5:6, OBS-103-062) — love under loss produces grief as its evidence. Intoxication/delight (Pro 5:19, OBS-103-099) — erotic love produces a pleasure-state described as intoxication. Required by love: compassion-movement (Luk 15:20, OBS-103-057) — responsive love requires the inner organs to be moved; without the emotional movement, the love-act does not occur. The full range: love produces the most positive (joy, delight, rest) and the most painful (grief, yearning) affective states in the inner-being vocabulary. |
| OBS-103-T2-051 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Zep 3:17 | The pattern from the data: divine love (Spirit-sourced) → enters heart (Rom 5:5) → produces soul-response (rest, joy, grief) → expresses bodily (kiss, embrace, tears, running). The sequence is: reception-at-depth → emergence-in-feeling → expression-in-body. This is the programme's first evidenced downward constitutional movement sequence for any characteristic. The intermediate soul-level is confirmed by OBS-103-041 and OBS-103-062 (Song) — love is experienced at the soul level before it expresses bodily. The body-level expression follows the soul-level experience: grief in the soul produces tears in the body; compassion-movement in the inner organs produces running and embrace. Pattern summary: Spirit origin → heart reception → soul experience → body expression. |
| OBS-103-T2-053 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Zep 3:17 | Love is not primarily emotional in the biblical data, though it has a rich emotional dimension. The evidence positions love as primarily dispositional/constitutional — an inner orientation that produces emotional states rather than consisting in them. Chesed is the clearest evidence: it persists without depending on emotional states (Lam 3:22 — 'the steadfast love of the Lord never ceases' despite Israel's devastating judgment). Agapē of enemies (Mat 5:44) operates where positive feeling is absent or where it must overcome negative feeling. The emotional states (joy, grief, rest, yearning) are love's products and evidence, not its constitutive substance. If love were primarily emotional, its commanded form ('you shall love') would be incoherent — emotions cannot be commanded. The commanded form confirms that love's primary operation is dispositional-volitional; the emotional dimension is real but secondary. |
| OBS-103-T2-059 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Pro 10:12 | Yes — through the judgment of the love-object's worth and the assessment of love's hierarchy. Mat 10:37 (OBS-103-151) requires a judgment: 'whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me' — love operates through a comparative judgment of worth among competing objects. The ranking of loves (OBS-103-022 — love as four-mode structure) requires reason to navigate. Jam 4:4 (OBS-103-058): the reasoning that friendship with the world constitutes enmity with God is a logical judgment. Pro 10:12 (OBS-103-097): 'love covers all offences' — the judgment that covering is better than exposing is a reasoned moral assessment. Love requires and shapes the judgment-faculty at the level of determining what and whom to love, in what order, and how. |
| OBS-103-T2-060 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 2:2 | Yes — and with unusual depth. Jer 2:2 (OBS-103-098): God remembers ('I remember the devotion of your youth') — divine love operates through memory of the prior love-relationship. The remembered love becomes the measure of the present failure ('how you followed me in the wilderness'). Jer 12:7 and Jer 31:20 (OBS-103-086, OBS-103-117): 'I do remember him still' — God's persistent love for Ephraim despite judgment-speech is grounded in divine memory. Lam 3:22 (OBS-103-088): the remembrance of God's chesed ('his mercies never come to an end') is the act of memory that sustains hope in the midst of desolation. Memory functions as love's sustaining faculty: love that cannot remember the beloved cannot maintain itself through absence or failure. |
| OBS-103-T2-061 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 31:3 | Yes. Psa 27:13 (OBS-103-080): 'I believe that I shall look upon the goodness of the Lord in the land of the living' — the psalmist's forward vision of God's love/goodness sustains the present inner life. The love-vocabulary consistently generates forward orientation (OBS-103-006, Q&A-006 — eschatological trajectory of love). Jer 31:3 (OBS-103-093): 'I have loved you with an everlasting love' — the everlasting qualifier generates an imagined future of permanent love. Song 2:16 (OBS-103-061): 'my beloved is mine' — the possession formula is also anticipatory: the belovedness extends into the imagined future. Imagination engages as love's visionary faculty — projecting the love-relationship into the future and sustaining the present through that projection. |
| OBS-103-T2-064 | OBSERVATION | Zep 3:17 | Already fully addressed in T2.6 (Q&A-051, Q&A-052, Q&A-061). Love engages the affective faculty as its primary experiential medium — joy, grief, rest, delight, yearning, compassion-movement are love's affective palette. Love is not reducible to affective state (Q&A-061) but the affective faculty is love's most immediate experiential expression. The distinctive feature: love's affective range spans the widest spectrum in the programme — from the deepest joy (Psa 16:11) to the deepest grief (Song 5:6, 2Sa 1:26). Love is the affective faculty's governing characteristic. |
| OBS-103-T2-065 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Pro 10:12 | Yes — love is the primary shaper of moral sensitivity in the data. Psa 97:10 (OBS-103-115): 'O you who love the Lord, hate evil' — love of God directly produces moral sensitivity to evil (the capacity to hate what is wrong). Lev 19:17 (OBS-103-114): the prohibition on hating the brother in the heart implies that moral sensitivity recognises inner hate as morally significant. 1Cor 13:6 (within OBS-103-127): love 'does not rejoice in wrongdoing' — love shapes the moral sensitivity that recognises wrongdoing as something not to be celebrated. Pro 10:12 (OBS-103-097): love's moral sensitivity distinguishes between hatred-that-stirs-strife and love-that-covers — the faculty that makes this distinction is morally sensitised by love. Love functions as the moral-sensitivity calibrator: it determines what the inner person registers as morally significant and how they register it. |
| OBS-103-T2-069 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 31:3 | The God-to-human direction is the programme's most evidentially dense directional axis for any characteristic. Four primary mechanisms: (a) **Covenantal declaration** — God names the love-relationship: 'I have loved you with an everlasting love' (Jer 31:3, OBS-103-093); 'you are the beloved of my soul' (Jer 12:7, OBS-103-086); 'This is my beloved Son' (Mat 3:17, OBS-103-120). The declaration is performative — it constitutes the relationship. (b) **Infusion** — God's love poured into hearts through the Spirit (Rom 5:5, OBS-103-126) — the Spirit as agent of love's transfer from divine to human inner being. (c) **Acts of love** — God gave his Son (Joh 3:16, OBS-103-141); God restores, forgives, compassionates (Lam 3:22, Isa 49:15, Jer 31:20). (d) **Covenantal covering** — Eze 16:8 (OBS-103-083b): God spreads his garment, makes a vow, enters covenant — the love-gift that constitutes the relationship. The overall pattern: divine love moves toward the human person through declaration, infusion, act, and covenant. |
| OBS-103-T2-070 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 31:3 | Divine love as gift has two structural features in the data: (a) **Priority** — 1Jo 4:19 ('we love because he first loved us') — divine love precedes all human love; it is not responsive to human merit but prior to it. Jer 31:3 ('with an everlasting love I have loved you') — the love predates the present moment and will outlast it. (b) **Sufficiency** — Heb 13:5 (OBS-103-138): the promise 'I will never leave you nor forsake you' is the ground for freedom from love-of-money — divine love is sufficient to displace the need for material security. The gift of divine love reorders the inner economy: receiving it makes other attachments unnecessary. As gift, divine love is both prior (unconditioned by merit) and sufficient (displacing rival loves by its own fullness). |
| OBS-103-T2-071 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Zep 3:17 | The receiver is constituted as agapētos (beloved) — an identity state, not merely a felt experience (OBS-103-040, OBS-103-122). The immediate inner-being state upon receiving divine love: rest/quieting (Zep 3:17, OBS-103-094); fullness and pleasure (Psa 16:11, OBS-103-112); soul-satisfaction (Jer 31:14, OBS-103-081). The sustained inner-being state: security ('I will never leave you' — Heb 13:5), known-ness ('Lord, you know everything — Joh 21:17, OBS-103-153), and free-from-striving (Psa 127:2, OBS-103-084). The person who receives divine love becomes the person who rests in it. The identity-state (beloved) and the experiential state (rest, fullness) are both dimensions of the receiver's inner condition. |
| OBS-103-T2-072 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 2:2 | Human love toward God is the great commandment's content: love with all heart, soul, and mind (Mat 22:37, OBS-103-139). The data shows love toward God expressed through: (a) **Total mobilisation** — 'with all your heart and soul and mind' — no faculty withheld; (b) **Commandment-keeping** — 'if you love me, you will keep my commandments' (Joh 14:15, OBS-103-140) — love produces the obedience-pattern; (c) **Priority-ranking** — love of God above family love (Mat 10:37, OBS-103-151), above love of the present world (2Ti 4:10, OBS-103-148); (d) **Costly following** — Jer 2:2 (OBS-103-098) — Israel's early love expressed through following God into the wilderness; (e) **Praise** — Psa 63:3 (OBS-103-087): 'because your steadfast love is better than life, my lips will praise you' — love toward God expressed as praise. The human-to-God direction is constituted by total inner mobilisation, life-ordering, and expressive acts. |
| OBS-103-T2-076 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Zep 3:17 | Love as received is constitutive of identity (agapētos, OBS-103-040), productive of rest (Zep 3:17, OBS-103-094), and the enabling condition for giving love (1Jo 4:19, OBS-103-146). The reception is: (a) constitutive — being named beloved by God constitutes who the person is; (b) enabling — divine love received is what makes human love possible (1Jo 4:19); (c) experiential — rest, fullness, and joy are the experiential register of received love. Reception of love is not passive — Song 5:2 (OBS-103-108) shows the beloved opening the door; Luk 15:20 (OBS-103-057) shows the son returning. Some movement toward the source appears to be the condition for reception (Q&A-031). |
| OBS-103-T2-085 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 31:3 | The data shows love can be abandoned or displaced, but the covenantal-divine form (chesed, agapē) is presented as permanent. Human love: reversible. 2Ti 4:10 (OBS-103-148): Demas' love of Paul's community abandoned for love of the world — the human love-commitment can be redirected. Mat 10:37 (OBS-103-151) implies love-rankings can be disordered (natural love displacing love of Christ). Joh 21:17: Peter's love was contested but affirmed — the question of love's persistence through failure is the Joh 21 theme. Divine love: irreversible in character. Jer 31:3 (OBS-103-093): 'with an everlasting love' — the qualifier rules out reversibility for the divine instance. Lam 3:22 (OBS-103-088): 'chesed never ceases' — divine chesed is by definition permanent. The distinction: divine love as irreversible; human love as subject to redirection through competing love-allegiances. |
| OBS-103-T2-096 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Zep 3:17 | Yes — love is causally productive in the data at both the inner-being and the social levels: (a) **Love → joy** (Psa 16:11, OBS-103-112): love-in-God's-presence produces fullness of joy; (b) **Love → rest** (Zep 3:17, OBS-103-094): God's love produces inner quieting; (c) **Love → grief** (Song 5:6, OBS-103-062; 2Sa 1:26, OBS-103-095): love under loss produces grief — love constitutes the grief by making the beloved's absence unbearable; (d) **Love → obedience** (Joh 14:15, OBS-103-140): love of Christ produces commandment-keeping; (e) **Love → praise** (Psa 63:3, OBS-103-087): love expressed as response to chesed-above-life produces praise. Love does not merely accompany these states but appears to constitute or cause them. It is the programme's most causally productive characteristic. |
| OBS-103-T2-113 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Pro 10:12 | Yes — embedded as OWNER. H8130 sane (to hate, 139 active / 156 total) is the structural opposite within the registry (OBS-103-030). H8131 sena (hating, 1 active) is a second form. The hate vocabulary is not XREF but OWNER — the programme treats the structural opposite as constituent of the love vocabulary's boundary (OBS-103-019). Additionally: the philo-compound negative forms (afilarguros — not-money-loving; pseudadelfos — false-brother) name the absence of specific love-forms. The disordered love vocabulary names love's distorted presence rather than its absence. True lovelessness (absence without misdirection) is not prominently named — the data suggests the inner mechanism is always present and always directed at something; the question is always the object, not the presence or absence of love-capacity. |
| OBS-103-T2-120 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 31:3 | Love's evidence base spans every major literary form: (a) **Wisdom/Proverb** (Pro 10:12, 27:6): requires attention to the comparative and aphoristic structure — proverbs describe patterns, not absolutes; (b) **Psalm** (Psa 63:3, 16:11, 97:10): requires reading as first-person prayer/meditation — the psalmist's inner orientation, not universal dogma; (c) **Prophetic oracle** (Jer 31:3, Eze 16:8, Isa 49:15): the love-language is covenantal and often metaphorical (marital imagery); responsible reading holds the metaphorical dimension; (d) **Epistle** (1Cor 13, 1Jo 4, Rom 5): the most theologically discursive form — allows propositional claims about love's nature; (e) **Gospel narrative** (Joh 3:16, Luk 7:38, Joh 15:13): love embedded in narrative events requiring attention to the narrative's argument; (f) **Song/Poem** (Song of Songs): erotic love in a canonical wisdom-poem; the allegorical interpretive tradition and the literal reading must both be held. No single form dominates — love's evidence is multi-formal. |
| OBS-103-T2-135 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Pro 10:12 | Yes — and the data makes this explicit. Psa 97:10 (OBS-103-115): 'O you who love the Lord, hate evil' — hatred of evil is commanded of those who love God. Righteous hatred is not love's failure but love's necessary complement: you cannot love God and be indifferent to what opposes him. Lev 19:17 (OBS-103-114): 'you shall not hate your brother in your heart' — hatred of the brother is wrong. The same inner-being mechanism (hatred as directed-aversion) can be rightly directed (at evil) or wrongly directed (at the brother). This is the precise structural parallel to love: love rightly directed (toward God and neighbour) vs love wrongly directed (toward money, pleasure, world). Hatred operates under the same moral logic as love: the mechanism is morally neutral; the object determines the moral quality. The ordered inner life requires both rightly directed love AND rightly directed hatred. |
| OBS-103-T2-141 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 31:3 | The data shows the origin profile varying somewhat by context: in the covenantal-OT context, chesed originates in the covenant bond and is exercised as faithfulness to it — the origin is the covenant relationship itself, not named as Spirit-infusion. In the NT eschatological context (Rom 5:5, Gal 5:22), love's origin is explicitly the Spirit. In the wisdom context (Tit 2:4, Pro 3:3), love's origin is communal formation and heart-inscription — the formative context is primary. In the Song, love's origin is the natural attraction of the beloved — no explicit divine-origin statement. The contexts do not contradict but emphasise different origin-dimensions depending on their theological concern. Gap: a full cross-contextual origin analysis requires comparing the OT covenantal origin account with the NT pneumatological account — this is a Session D synthesis task. |
| OBS-103-T2-146 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 2:2 | Love **deepens and sustains** memory. Jer 31:20 (OBS-103-117): 'I do remember him still' — God's love for Ephraim persists as memory even through judgment-speech. The love-relationship makes the beloved unforgettable: the nephesh cannot forget the one it loves (Jer 12:7 — 'beloved of my soul'). Jer 2:2 (OBS-103-098): God's memory of Israel's early love ('the devotion of your youth') shows love as the content of sustained divine memory. Conversely, the threat to love is forgetting: Hos 2:13 (implied in the prophetic love-vocabulary) — Israel forgot God. Lovelessness leads to forgetting the love-relationship; love sustains memory of the beloved across time and distance. Love deepens memory because the beloved is the content of what is held across time. Love cannot maintain itself without memory — the love that forgets is the love that fails. |
| OBS-103-T2-147 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 2:2 | The memory-engagement pattern reveals that love has a temporal architecture — it is not a momentary state but an orientation that holds the beloved across time. Chesed as covenantal loyalty (Lam 3:22 — 'never ceasing') implies that love must maintain its object in memory through the times when love's felt presence is absent. The theological implication: love's permanence is partly a function of its memory — what God loves, God remembers; what God remembers, God does not abandon. This is the OT's account of covenant faithfulness as a memory-structure: God's love for Israel is expressed through his refusal to forget them even under judgment. Love is therefore constitutively temporal: it is an orientation that persists through time by holding the beloved in memory. |
| OBS-103-T2-148 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 2Sa 1:26 | Love **deepens and intensifies** affect rather than bypassing or impairing it. The love-vocabulary's emotional range is the widest in the programme (OBS-103-051 — from deepest joy to deepest grief). Specific deepening evidence: Song 5:6 (OBS-103-062) — love's absence produces soul-failing, one of the most acute affective states in the corpus. 2Sa 1:26 (OBS-103-095) — love measured by its grief: 'your love was extraordinary, surpassing the love of women.' The intensity of love produces intensity of affect — not merely as a side-effect but as love's evidence and measure. Love also enables affect that would otherwise be inaccessible: love of enemy (Mat 5:44) enables compassion where enmity would produce contempt; love of God enables the reverential joy of Psa 63:3 ('better than life'). Disordered love distorts affect: love-of-money produces 'many pangs' (1Ti 6:10, OBS-103-132) — the wrong love generates the wrong affective outcomes. |

**SD pointers — direct (3)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |

| DIM-103-SD012 | MEDIUM | H0160 | Zep 3:17 — 'He will quiet you by his love.' God's love produces rest/stillness/quietness in the beloved. Cross-registry: love (103), peace/rest (117), surrender (156). Question: is the peace produced by divine love a distinct inner-being state (rest/shalom), or is it the same state as love viewed from the receiving end? The verb 'quiet' (charash — to be still, to silence) suggests love has a stilling effect on inner agitation. |
| DIM-103-SD017 | MEDIUM | H0160 | 2Sa 1:26 — David's lament for Jonathan: 'your love to me was extraordinary, surpassing the love of women.' Expressed in grief at Jonathan's death. The depth of grief reveals the depth of the prior love — grief as love's evidence under loss. Cross-registry: love (103), grief (71). Question: is grief structurally inseparable from love — not its opposite but its expression when love's object is lost? If so, love and grief may be the same inner state viewed from presence and absence. |
| SP-103-034 | HIGH |  | [v1.8 obslog SD pointer] Song 8:6 — (a) does the programme have a qinah registry? (b) does 'flame of Yah' support 1Jo 4:8 ('God is love') from the OT side? Target: R103 (love) / jealousy-zeal registry (qinah) / divine nature Connecting term: H0160 ahavah / H7068 qinah Evidence basis: OBS-103-096 |
