### G1731 endeiknumi — to show

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G1731` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R198 might · Legacy C-cluster: C20
3. Sub-group: `M05-BOUNDARY` (Boundary)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 18
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=1354` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
6. Mounce short-def: to show, display

**Verses:** 7 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3218 | 1354-001 | Term names the act of demonstrating — the inner being engaged as source or recipient of what is shown: love, earnestness, hope, conscience, divine power and wrath made visible through demonstration |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (12 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| 112-F006 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 2:15 | Suneidēsis (conscience) is the NT's distinctive contribution with no direct Hebrew equivalent. The conscience can be weak, clear, seared, or purified (Heb 9:14), and functions as a bridge between inner life and relational/covenantal domains. |
| DIM-160-004 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Rom 2:15 | Registry 160 (thought) contains three instances of the directionally-determined inner faculty pattern: (1) re.a (H7454) — 3453-001 names thoughts known by God and God's thoughts precious to the psalmist, the same relational faculty operating from both sides of the divine-human boundary simultaneously (Sub-pattern C); (2) dialogismos (G1261) — 3409-001 names inner thoughts as site of spiritual warfare and accusation (Moral/Conscience) and 3410-001 names inner reckoning as the ground of faith (Cognitive/Mind) — same root, opposite orientation; (3) fronimos (G5429) pattern carries from C01: prudence and craftiness as the same perceptive faculty (Sub-pattern B). Session B must treat these as analytically distinct uses of the same terms. |
| OBS-067-T2-002 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | Scripture attributes goodness to God extensively and explicitly. Psa 119:68 ("You are good"), Psa 34:8 ("the Lord is good"), Psa 100:5 and Ps 106:1 / 107:1 / 118:1 (doxological refrain "for he is good"), Lam 3:25 ("the Lord is good to those who wait for him"), Rom 11:22 (God's chrēstotēs), Eph 2:7 (God's chrēstotēs in Christ). The attribution reveals that goodness in the human person is not an independent achievement but a participation in and reflection of an attribute that originates in God. Human goodness is derivative by design — it is what being made in the image of the good God looks like in a creature. This has direct significance: the human person who embodies goodness is expressing their created nature; the human person who fails to do so is in a condition of departure from original design. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-006) |
| OBS-067-T2-005 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | The evidence is suggestive but not fully explicit. 2Th 1:11 ("may God fulfil every resolve for good and every work of faith by his power") implies an eschatological completeness — the resolve for good is present now, but its fulfilment is anticipated as God's completing act. Eph 2:7 places God's display of chrēstotēs "in the coming ages" — goodness has a future-facing dimension of fullness. Gal 5:22 as Spirit-fruit connects goodness to the present work of the Spirit that anticipates the fullness of Spirit-transformation. However, the data does not contain a verse that explicitly narrates goodness as moving toward eschatological fullness in the way that glory or resurrection does. The eschatological trajectory is implied by the pneumatological framework (Spirit-production → Spirit-completion) but not explicitly named. This is a partial gap. (OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-016) |
| OBS-067-T2-009 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | The covenantal typological dimension is present and clear: God's good word (884-005, Jos 23:14) and his chrēstotēs displayed supremely in Christ (Eph 2:7) both carry a Christological register — the fullness of divine goodness is displayed in Christ's person and work. Rom 2:4 names chrēstotēs as the mechanism through which God leads to repentance, which is structurally a Christological movement. However, the data does not contain an explicit typological argument (e.g., the good king who typifies the messianic king, or a creation-goodness → new-creation-goodness typology). The christological and eschatological dimensions are present in NT usage but not through formal typological structure. This is a partial finding — the direction is named but the full typological structure is not explicitly drawn in the verse evidence. (OBS-067-OBS-006, OBS-067-OBS-036) |
| OBS-067-T2-085 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | God's extension of goodness reveals a disposition of initiatory, generous, and sustaining goodwill toward the human person. Three dimensions: (1) Initiatory — God acts first in goodness (Gen 1:31; Eph 2:7 — "so that in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus"). The initiative is always divine. (2) Generous — chrēstotēs names not merely adequate goodness but generous goodwill (886-001): God's disposition is one of overflowing benevolence. (3) Sustaining — the covenantal promise (884-005, Jos 23:14: "not one word has failed") names a goodness that holds through time and maintains its commitment. The disposition is not reactive but proactive, generous, and reliable. Lam 3:25 ("the Lord is good to those who wait for him") names waiting as the human posture corresponding to this divine sustaining goodness — the relationship is not passive but involves a human receptive posture. (OBS-067-OBS-006, OBS-067-OBS-033, OBS-067-OBS-034, SBF-067-Q006) |
| OBS-067-T2-109 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | Partial eschatological evidence is present. Eph 2:7 ("so that in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus") explicitly places chrēstotēs in a future-oriented eschatological frame — God's chrēstotēs will be displayed in its fullness in the coming ages. This names an eschatological trajectory: present experience of divine kindness anticipates its future unrestricted display. 2Th 1:11 ("fulfil every resolve for good") implies a completion — God's completing act brings present goodness-resolve to its fullness. However, the specifically eschatological texture of goodness (what it will be in the new creation, what fullness means) is not directly described in the anchor verses beyond these two instances. The trajectory is indicated but not fully developed. (OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-016, SBF-067-Q006) |
| OBS-067-T2-110 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | Eph 2:7 implies that present experience of chrēstotēs is a foretaste of the "immeasurable riches" of its future expression — present goodness is real but partial relative to its coming fullness. The "immeasurable" qualifier suggests that the eschatological form of goodness exceeds present experience in scale and richness. 2Th 1:11's "fulfil every resolve for good" implies that present resolves for goodness, even when genuine, are not yet complete — their completion is a divine eschatological act. The present experience of goodness therefore anticipates: (1) A fullness of divine kindness-display that surpasses present experience; (2) A completion of human goodness-resolve that requires divine power to finish. The gap is that the data does not describe what the experiential content of eschatological goodness will be — only that it will be fuller and completed. (OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-016) |
| OBS-099-T2-005 | OBSERVATION | Eph 2:7 | Yes. Eph 2:7 places divine kindness in an eschatological frame: "in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus" [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Rom 2:4 establishes that divine kindness is purposively aimed at repentance — directed toward a future state not yet present [OBS-099-076]. Kindness has an eschatological trajectory: both in the recipient (transformation) and in the divine economy (immeasurable future display). |
| OBS-099-T2-009 | OBSERVATION | Eph 2:7 | Yes — three typological movements: (a) Covenantal: each individual divine chesed act participates in and points toward the one covenantal faithfulness underlying them all [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]. (b) Christological: Eph 2:7 names Christ as the supreme display of divine kindness — the eschatological revelation of what chesed has always been pointing toward [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. (c) Human-divine mirror: Ruth's kindness, 1Sa 20:14, 2Sa 10:2 — each human chesed act is typological of the divine pattern [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-070/071]. |
| OBS-099-T2-065 | OBSERVATION | Eph 2:7 | Yes — three eschatological markers: (a) Eschatological escalation: Eph 2:7 — "in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus" [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Future form exceeds present expression. (b) Preservation of the chasid: Psa 16:10 — "you will not abandon my soul to Sheol, or let your holy one see corruption" [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-095]. Chesed-shaped person eschatologically preserved; formation survives death. (c) Telos: Rom 2:4 (repentance) and Eph 2:7 (eschatological display) together — divine kindness is purposive from beginning to end. |
| OBS-099-T2-070 | OBSERVATION | Eph 2:7 | Partial evidence for three boundaries: (a) Kindness vs. love: 1Cor 13:4 — kindness is a mode of love's operation, not its definition [OBS-099-072, Q&A-021]. Love is broader; kindness is the specific outward-oriented gracious-action mode. (b) Kindness vs. compassion: Eph 4:32 — kindness and tenderheartedness are distinct qualities [OBS-099-077]. Compassion feels; kindness acts from that feeling. (c) Kindness vs. grace: Eph 2:7 — grace names the favour; kindness names the disposition in which the favour is given [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Gap: boundaries implied by co-occurrence and context but not explicitly theorised. Session D needed for full boundary analysis. |

### G5370 filēma — kiss

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G5370` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R103 love · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-BOUNDARY` (Boundary)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 9
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=1580` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`
6. Mounce short-def: kiss

**Verses:** 7 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1566 | 1580-001 | Term names the kiss as the outward expression of an inner relational reality — betrayal, absent love exposed, or holy community bond |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (5 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-103-T2-022 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Pe 5:14 | Yes — evidenced across several groups. Joh 15:15 (OBS-103-052): love's communicative mode is knowledge-disclosure — Jesus names the content of his friendship with the disciples as 'all that I have heard from my Father I have made known to you.' Love shares the inner world of the lover with the beloved. Lev 19:17 (OBS-103-114): frank correction (hocheach tochi'ach — honest rebuke) is love's communicative act, in contrast to hate's silence. The kiss (filēma, katafileō — OBS-103-054, OBS-103-056) is love's somatic-communicative mode — the body's expression of the inner word. The divine declaration ('this is my beloved Son' — Mat 3:17, OBS-103-120) is love's constitutive speech act: the naming of belovedness is itself the conferral of identity. Love therefore has a speech-mode that functions as: disclosure (sharing the inner world), correction (honest engagement), and declaration (constitutive naming). |
| OBS-103-T2-051 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Pe 5:14 | The pattern from the data: divine love (Spirit-sourced) → enters heart (Rom 5:5) → produces soul-response (rest, joy, grief) → expresses bodily (kiss, embrace, tears, running). The sequence is: reception-at-depth → emergence-in-feeling → expression-in-body. This is the programme's first evidenced downward constitutional movement sequence for any characteristic. The intermediate soul-level is confirmed by OBS-103-041 and OBS-103-062 (Song) — love is experienced at the soul level before it expresses bodily. The body-level expression follows the soul-level experience: grief in the soul produces tears in the body; compassion-movement in the inner organs produces running and embrace. Pattern summary: Spirit origin → heart reception → soul experience → body expression. |
| OBS-103-T2-081 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 22:48 | Breakdowns evidenced: (a) **False brotherhood** — Gal 2:4 (OBS-103-068): using the community-love structure to serve a contrary agenda — the most dangerous breakdown because it uses love's form; (b) **Betrayal-kiss** — Luk 22:48 (OBS-103-054): love's somatic sign (kiss) used to indicate enmity — betrayal as the weaponisation of love's language; (c) **Desertion for world-love** — 2Ti 4:10 (OBS-103-148): abandonment of love-community for love of world; (d) **Poverty-caused dissolution** — Pro 19:7 (OBS-103-073): friendship tested by adversity may fail when it was conditional; (e) **Pride-driven rivalry** — Luk 22:24 (OBS-103-131): love-of-primacy at the table of the Lord. Conditions for maintenance: honest engagement (Lev 19:17, frank rebuke), soul-purification (1Pe 1:22), shared suffering (1Pe 5:9). The most dangerous breakdown is false brotherhood — it operates inside the love-structure while working against it. |
| OBS-103-T2-133 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 22:48 | Yes — the data names two constitutive outward expressions: (a) **Self-giving** — Joh 15:13 (OBS-103-125): 'greater love has no one than this: to lay down one's life for one's friends.' The self-giving is not the evidence of love — it is love's maximum constitutive form. The act constitutes the love at its greatest — without the self-giving there is no greater love, only a lesser form. (b) **Obedience** — Joh 14:15: 'if you love me, you will keep my commandments.' Commandment-keeping is not merely love's fruit — it is love's definitive form toward Christ. The absence of commandment-keeping in the professed lover of Christ constitutes a question about whether the love is genuine. The kiss (filēma, katafileō) is also a constitutive rather than merely evidential act — the Judas-kiss (Luk 22:48) is dangerous precisely because it uses love's constitutive act to signal the opposite. |
| OBS-103-T2-164 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 22:48 | The data provides indirect evidence. The false brother (pseudadelfos — OBS-103-067, OBS-103-068) operates within the community's love-structure using it as a cover for a contrary agenda — this is the weaponisation of love's communal form. The betrayal-kiss (Luk 22:48, OBS-103-054) deploys love's primary somatic sign to accomplish betrayal — weaponising love's expression. Whether these are attributed to adversarial spiritual powers is not stated in the anchor verse evidence. 1Jo 4:18 (via SP-103-029) suggests that fear operates against love in a structural opposition — if adversarial powers work through fear, and perfect love casts out fear, then love is implicitly the counter to adversarial fear-activity. However, direct attribution of attacks on love to spiritual adversarial powers is not evidenced in R103's anchor corpus. Partial. |

### G2705 katafileō — to kiss

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G2705` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R103 love · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-BOUNDARY` (Boundary)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 21
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=1581` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`
6. Mounce short-def: to kiss

**Verses:** 6 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1551 | 1581-001 | Term names the kiss as physical expression of an inner state — love and grief, contrition and gratitude, betrayal, or joyful reunion |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (14 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-103-T2-009 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | Yes — extensively. The dod vocabulary of the Song is the most concentrated typological deployment: human eros/marital love functions as the vehicle for God's love for Israel (Isa 5:1 OBS-103-083; Eze 16:8 OBS-103-083b) and in the prophetic-allegorical tradition as the figure of divine-human covenantal love. God's love for Israel is called 'the time of love' (Eze 16:8) using marital vocabulary. The father-son love (Mat 3:17, OBS-103-120) functions typologically: the Father's love for the Son at baptism is simultaneously the origin-point from which all love flows (SD-016) and the pattern of divine-to-human love in redemption. The parable of the prodigal son (Luk 15:20, OBS-103-057) deploys parental love typologically — the father's running, embracing, and kissing figure God's reception of the returning sinner. Typological use runs in both directions (OT human love → divine love type; NT divine love → human love pattern). |
| OBS-103-T2-024 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | The response varies by the type of love-encounter. When divine love is received: the immediate response is love-in-return (1Jo 4:19) or rest (Zep 3:17, Psa 127:2). When love is expressed at cost (forgiveness-love, enemy-love): the immediate response in the giver is not named directly. When love is lost: the immediate response is grief (Song 5:6, OBS-103-062; 2Sa 1:26, OBS-103-095). When the beloved is encountered: the immediate response is delight and longing (Song 2:16, OBS-103-061; Luk 15:20 — the father's compassion-movement at sight of the returning son, OBS-103-057). The variation is systematic, not random: the type of encounter determines the response type. There is no single invariant immediate response to love — the response depends on the direction of the encounter (giving vs receiving vs losing vs encountering). |
| OBS-103-T2-028 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | From the data: (a) **obedience** — 1Pe 1:22 (OBS-103-124): soul-purification through obedience enables sincere love. The purified soul is the condition for authentic love. (b) **faith** — Jam 2:23 (OBS-103-053): Abraham's faith is what constitutes him as 'friend of God.' Faith as the inner condition through which love-relationship with God becomes possible. (c) **openness / not loving the darkness** — Joh 3:19 (OBS-103-147): those who love darkness cannot receive the light. The inner orientation away from evil is the negative condition for receiving love. (d) **being the returning prodigal** — Luk 15:20 (OBS-103-057): the son's return (movement toward the father) is the condition for the father's embrace. Some movement toward the source of love appears to be a condition for its reception. Gap: the data does not contain an explicit systematic account of reception-conditions. The conditions above are inferred from contexts where love is given and received. The gap is noted. |
| OBS-103-T2-050 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | Yes — the data evidences downward constitutional movement (from spirit-source into soul and body) as the primary direction. Rom 5:5 (OBS-103-126): God's love poured into hearts through the Spirit — movement from divine Spirit into the human heart (spirit → heart). Luk 15:20 (OBS-103-057): compassion (inner movement) → running → embrace → kiss — the inner compassion-love produces bodily movement (soul → body). Lam 3:22 (OBS-103-088) and Pro 3:3 (OBS-103-090): chesed at the character level (spirit) expressed in word and act (soul and body). The movement is consistently from the interior (spirit/heart) outward (soul/affect, then body/act). The inverse — body experience generating love-at-the-spirit-level — is not directly evidenced but may be implied in the somatic kiss-as-love-communication contexts. |
| OBS-103-T2-051 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | The pattern from the data: divine love (Spirit-sourced) → enters heart (Rom 5:5) → produces soul-response (rest, joy, grief) → expresses bodily (kiss, embrace, tears, running). The sequence is: reception-at-depth → emergence-in-feeling → expression-in-body. This is the programme's first evidenced downward constitutional movement sequence for any characteristic. The intermediate soul-level is confirmed by OBS-103-041 and OBS-103-062 (Song) — love is experienced at the soul level before it expresses bodily. The body-level expression follows the soul-level experience: grief in the soul produces tears in the body; compassion-movement in the inner organs produces running and embrace. Pattern summary: Spirit origin → heart reception → soul experience → body expression. |
| OBS-103-T2-062 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 7:38 | Indirectly evidenced. The Song of Songs is love's most concentrated creative expression in Scripture — a poem whose entire form (extended metaphor, nature imagery, dialogue structure) is the creative vehicle of love-expression (OBS-103-041). Isa 5:1 (OBS-103-083): 'let me sing for my beloved my love song' — love generating song as creative expression. The kiss vocabulary (kiss = creative somatic expression of love) and the anointing (Luk 7:38, OBS-103-056) are body-as-creative-medium. However, the explicit creativity-faculty engagement (imagination deployed in artistic creation) is not named in the data — the creative dimension is inferred from the Song's form and from the love-song references. Partial: love generates creative expression in the data; explicit creativity-faculty engagement not directly named. |
| OBS-103-T2-068 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | All three — building, sustaining, and restoring — are evidenced. Building: Joh 13:34 (OBS-103-145) — 'as I have loved you, you also are to love one another' — establishes the community as a new relational structure constituted by love. Sustaining: Lam 3:22 (OBS-103-088) — chesed that never ceases sustains the covenant relationship through judgment and desolation. Pro 3:3 (OBS-103-090) — heart-inscription sustains the disposition through circumstances. Restoring: Luk 15:20 (OBS-103-057) — the father's compassion-love restores the broken relationship with the returning son. Luk 7:47 (via SD-020): 'she loved much because she was forgiven much' — restored relationship (through forgiveness) produces love as its fruit. The restoration function: love does not merely sustain what exists but restores what has been broken — this is one of love's most structurally important capacities. |
| OBS-103-T2-076 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | Love as received is constitutive of identity (agapētos, OBS-103-040), productive of rest (Zep 3:17, OBS-103-094), and the enabling condition for giving love (1Jo 4:19, OBS-103-146). The reception is: (a) constitutive — being named beloved by God constitutes who the person is; (b) enabling — divine love received is what makes human love possible (1Jo 4:19); (c) experiential — rest, fullness, and joy are the experiential register of received love. Reception of love is not passive — Song 5:2 (OBS-103-108) shows the beloved opening the door; Luk 15:20 (OBS-103-057) shows the son returning. Some movement toward the source appears to be the condition for reception (Q&A-031). |
| OBS-103-T2-097 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | From the data: (a) Love may constitute or generate all Spirit-fruits (Gal 5:22, Q&A-105) — if love is the single fruit of which joy, peace, patience, etc. are expressions, then love partially constitutes those characteristics. (b) Forgiveness may constitute the condition from which love emerges (Luk 7:47 via SD-020) — if 'she loved because she was forgiven much,' forgiveness is constitutive of the love-response. (c) Compassion and love appear to be constitutively related — compassion may be love's form under the condition of the other's suffering (OBS-103-042, OBS-103-057). Gap: the precise constitutive relationships between love and compassion, mercy, kindness cannot be fully determined from Stage 2a evidence alone — these require Session D treatment. Partial: love-as-constituent-of-Spirit-fruits and forgiveness-as-constituent-of-love-response are the best-evidenced claims. |
| OBS-103-T2-101 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | The closest adjacent characteristic from co-occurrence data is compassion (R023, 196 shared verses). What distinguishes them from the data: compassion is love's responsive form under the condition of the other's suffering — the rachamim/womb-movement is activated by the beloved's need or distress. Love without suffering context does not require the racham-movement; love in the presence of suffering always produces it. Stated differently: compassion is the subset of love that is suffering-triggered; love is the broader orientation of which compassion is one mode (OBS-103-029, OBS-103-042). However: the data does not fully distinguish chesed from compassion at the verse level — both can be present simultaneously (Lam 3:22). The distinction is partial. Gap: the precise boundary between love and compassion requires dedicated Session D analysis of both registries' verse corpora together. |
| OBS-103-T2-130 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | Yes — the data provides evidence of love operating below the threshold of deliberate conscious attention. Song 5:2 (OBS-103-108): 'I slept, but my heart was awake' — love-awareness persists in sleep, below waking consciousness. 1Ki 3:26 (OBS-103-105): the mother's rachamim for her son is described as physiological movement (nichmeruh — heated, turbulent) — an involuntary somatic response. Jer 31:20 (OBS-103-117): God's inner parts 'hamu' (murmur, are agitated) for Ephraim — the movement of the inner organs is described as spontaneous, not deliberate. The compassion-movement (splanchnizō, Luk 15:20) triggers before deliberate action (running, embracing). These instances suggest love has a pre-reflective dimension — an inner orienting toward the beloved that is already present before the person consciously engages it. Implication: love is not fully within the person's deliberate control at its deepest level; it is constitutive of the inner person at a depth below ordinary conscious access. |
| OBS-103-T2-136 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | The data positions them in a **generative relationship** — disposition generates act. The chesed that is written on the heart (Pro 3:3, OBS-103-090) is the disposition that produces the acts of Ruth's chesed (Rut 3:10, OBS-103-089) and the acts of social kindness in the neighbour-relationships. Love of Christ as inner disposition produces commandment-keeping as outward act (Joh 14:15, OBS-103-140). The compassion-movement (inner) produces running, embracing, kissing (outer) in Luk 15:20 (OBS-103-057). The relationship is not competitive (as if outer act were less genuine than inner disposition) nor merely co-present (as if they were unrelated). Disposition and act are related as inner origin and outer expression — the act is the disposition's actualisation in a specific relational moment. 1Ki 8:18 (OBS-103-103) confirms: God accepts the inner disposition (it was in your heart) even when the act is not performed — the disposition has value prior to and independent of the act. But the disposition that never produces an act is not evidenced as genuine love in the data — love without expression is not thematised as sufficient. |
| OBS-103-T2-151 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 7:38 | The creative engagement pattern — where evidenced — reveals that love's depth produces a surplus that demands expression beyond ordinary speech. The love-song, the poem, the kiss, the anointing (Luk 7:38) are all creative acts that exceed propositional communication. This suggests love has an expressive excess: it overflows the available ordinary forms and generates new ones. However, the pattern of non-engagement is unclear — the data does not indicate love impairing creativity, nor does it name love as the explicit source of creative capacity. The data supports: love is one of the inner-being states most associated with creative expression; whether this constitutes enabling the creative faculty or simply being a frequent companion of it is not determinable from the current evidence. |
| OBS-103-T2-162 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 15:20 | Love **enables, deepens, and restores** relational capacity — it is the primary characteristic that constitutes and sustains the relational faculty. Enabling: Joh 13:34 (OBS-103-145) — love constitutes the new relational community from scratch; the love-commandment creates a relational structure that did not exist before it. Deepening: Joh 15:15 (OBS-103-052) — love deepens the disciple relationship from servant-level to friend-level through knowledge-disclosure; love's deepening of the bond changes its constitutional character. Restoring: Luk 15:20 (OBS-103-057) — the father's love restores the broken son-relationship through compassion and embrace. Joh 21:17 (OBS-103-153) — Jesus' love restores Peter's relational capacity after denial. Disordered love impairs relational capacity: love-of-self (filautos — OBS-103-137) produces the self-enclosed orientation that cannot genuinely connect to another. |

**SD pointers — direct (3)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-103-SD020 | HIGH | G2705 | Luk 7:38-47 — woman's kisses and tears as bodily expression of love; Jesus' verdict: 'she loved much because she was forgiven much.' One of the most explicit causal chains in the NT: forgiveness → love. Cross-registry: love (103), forgiveness (64), grief/tears (71). Question: is love the primary inner response to forgiveness in the biblical data? If so, forgiveness may be the trigger that produces love, and love may be the inner evidence of received forgiveness. |
| DIM-103-SD023 | MEDIUM | G2705 | Act 20:37 — 'they embraced Paul and kissed him' at farewell. Somatic expression of love at anticipated permanent separation. The farewell scene is simultaneously an expression of love (embrace, kiss) and grief (weeping). Cross-registry: love (103), grief (71), fellowship/community (62). Question: is the farewell scene a structural locus where love and grief converge — where love's attachment is revealed by grief at separation? |
| SP-103-030 | MEDIUM |  | [v1.8 obslog SD pointer] Luk 15:20 — is compassion the inner state that activates love's outward expression, or is compassion love itself under the condition of the returning? Target: R023 (compassion) / R103 (love) Connecting term: G2705 katafileō / G4697 splanchnizō Evidence basis: OBS-103-057 |

### G5360 filadelfia — brotherly love

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G5360` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R103 love · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-A` (Love)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 6
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=558` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`
6. Mounce short-def: brotherly love; brotherly kindness

**Verses:** 5 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1556 | 558-001 | Term names brotherly love as the inner-relational disposition that binds the community of faith — to be cultivated, continued, and grounded in purified hearts |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (12 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-103-T2-025 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Pe 1:22 | The verse evidence identifies several sustained effects of love in the inner being: (1) **Character formation** — Pro 3:3 (OBS-103-090): chesed written on the heart over time becomes character. 1Pe 1:22 (OBS-103-124): purified souls move toward sincere brotherly love — love as the goal of sustained inner formation. (2) **Joy and fullness** — Psa 16:11 (OBS-103-112): in God's presence (sustained love-relationship) there is fullness of joy and pleasures forevermore. The sustained love-relationship with God produces an abiding experiential fullness. (3) **Growth** — 1Th 3:12 (OBS-103-130): love can increase and abound over time. Love is not static but grows under the Lord's enabling. (4) **Community cohesion** — Joh 13:35 (OBS-103-129): sustained mutual love becomes the externally legible identity of the community — a sustained social effect rather than a momentary one. (5) **Obedience pattern** — Joh 14:15 (OBS-103-140): sustained love of Christ produces a sustained pattern of commandment-keeping. The inner orientation produces an enduring behavioural pattern. |
| OBS-103-T2-028 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Pe 1:22 | From the data: (a) **obedience** — 1Pe 1:22 (OBS-103-124): soul-purification through obedience enables sincere love. The purified soul is the condition for authentic love. (b) **faith** — Jam 2:23 (OBS-103-053): Abraham's faith is what constitutes him as 'friend of God.' Faith as the inner condition through which love-relationship with God becomes possible. (c) **openness / not loving the darkness** — Joh 3:19 (OBS-103-147): those who love darkness cannot receive the light. The inner orientation away from evil is the negative condition for receiving love. (d) **being the returning prodigal** — Luk 15:20 (OBS-103-057): the son's return (movement toward the father) is the condition for the father's embrace. Some movement toward the source of love appears to be a condition for its reception. Gap: the data does not contain an explicit systematic account of reception-conditions. The conditions above are inferred from contexts where love is given and received. The gap is noted. |
| OBS-103-T2-073 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Pe 1:22 | The condition for human love toward God is prior reception of divine love (1Jo 4:19 — 'we love because he first loved us'). Human love toward God is always responsive, not originating. The capacity required: soul-purification (1Pe 1:22, OBS-103-124) — the inner person must be cleared of the disordering loves that block genuine love toward God. The great commandment ('with all your soul') implies that the soul must be free from divided ultimate attachment to function as love-toward-God. Love of money (1Ti 6:10, OBS-103-132) and love of the world (2Ti 4:10, OBS-103-148) represent the prior conditions that prevent the human-to-God love-flow. The capacity for human love toward God is therefore: received divine love (enabling condition) + soul-purification from rival loves (clearing condition). |
| OBS-103-T2-080 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 12:10 | Human-to-human love creates a social architecture with three features: (a) **Restructured family** — Mat 12:50 (OBS-103-063): 'whoever does the will of my Father is my brother and sister and mother' — love redraws the family boundary around shared orientation toward God rather than natural kinship. (b) **Public community mark** — Joh 13:35 (OBS-103-129): mutual love is externally legible as the community's identity. The social architecture of love is publicly readable — it creates a community whose internal reality is visible to outsiders. (c) **Competitive generosity** — Rom 12:10 (OBS-103-119): 'outdo one another in showing honor' — love creates a social order where the competition is in giving rather than taking, in honour-showing rather than honour-claiming. These three features constitute a social architecture that is structurally counter to the natural social patterns of kinship, concealment, and rivalry. |
| OBS-103-T2-081 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Pe 1:22 | Breakdowns evidenced: (a) **False brotherhood** — Gal 2:4 (OBS-103-068): using the community-love structure to serve a contrary agenda — the most dangerous breakdown because it uses love's form; (b) **Betrayal-kiss** — Luk 22:48 (OBS-103-054): love's somatic sign (kiss) used to indicate enmity — betrayal as the weaponisation of love's language; (c) **Desertion for world-love** — 2Ti 4:10 (OBS-103-148): abandonment of love-community for love of world; (d) **Poverty-caused dissolution** — Pro 19:7 (OBS-103-073): friendship tested by adversity may fail when it was conditional; (e) **Pride-driven rivalry** — Luk 22:24 (OBS-103-131): love-of-primacy at the table of the Lord. Conditions for maintenance: honest engagement (Lev 19:17, frank rebuke), soul-purification (1Pe 1:22), shared suffering (1Pe 5:9). The most dangerous breakdown is false brotherhood — it operates inside the love-structure while working against it. |
| OBS-103-T2-082 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Pe 1:22 | Yes — multiple acquisition-pathways: (a) **Reception of divine love** — 1Jo 4:19 (OBS-103-146): human love is acquired by receiving divine love first. The primary acquisition pathway is reception, not self-generation. (b) **Spirit infusion** — Rom 5:5 (OBS-103-126): God's love poured into hearts through the Spirit — the Spirit is the agent of love's acquisition at the heart level. Gal 5:22 — love as the Spirit's fruit — love grows in the person as the Spirit produces it. (c) **Training** — Tit 2:4 (OBS-103-124 context): young women trained to love husbands and children — love as a domestically trainable virtue. (d) **Soul purification through obedience** — 1Pe 1:22 (OBS-103-124): obedience purifies the soul for sincere love. The obedience-pathway cleanses the inner person of what blocks love, enabling authentic love to emerge. (e) **Covenant-formation** — Pro 3:3: chesed written on the heart — love is inscribed through the practices of the covenant life. |
| OBS-103-T2-084 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Pe 1:22 | Yes. Joh 15:15 (OBS-103-052): the disciples are transformed from servants to friends through Christ's love — a fundamental identity and relational reclassification. 1Pe 1:22 (OBS-103-124): soul-purification as the outcome of the love-formation process — the inner person is changed by the pathway that leads to sincere love. The 1Cor 13 trajectory (OBS-103-127): love's sustained exercise produces a character that does not envy, boast, or seek its own — the moral character is transformed by the practice of love. Luk 7:47 (via SD-020): the woman who loves much has been transformed by forgiveness — the transformation in her produced the love-expression. Love both requires and produces transformation. |
| OBS-103-T2-086 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Pe 1:22 | The primary mechanism from the data: divine love received → responsive love generated (1Jo 4:19). The Spirit is the operating agent of this mechanism (Rom 5:5, Gal 5:22) — the Spirit infuses divine love into the heart, which produces the inner reorientation toward love. The soul-purification mechanism (1Pe 1:22): obedience → purification → sincere love — a preparatory clearing that enables love to emerge. The training mechanism (Tit 2:4): repeated practice within the community forms love as a stable disposition. The forgiveness-trigger mechanism (Luk 7:47 — via SD-020): received forgiveness generates love as its fruit. Together: love's mechanism operates at multiple levels — the Spirit as the primary agent, obedience as the clearing condition, practice as the formation pathway, and received forgiveness as a specific love-trigger. |
| OBS-103-T2-088 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Pe 1:22 | Yes — fundamentally. Love cannot be formed in isolation: it requires a beloved to be directed toward. The community formation of love (Joh 13:34 — 'love one another'; filadelfia cultivated together; training in Tit 2:4 within a community context) is inherently relational. The primary formation-by-others: reception of divine love as the enabling condition (1Jo 4:19) — God's action toward the person is the formative ground. The community of mutual love (1Pe 1:22 — 'sincere brotherly love' developed through shared obedience) is a communal-formation context. Love cannot be a solo formation project — it requires the other's presence, action, and response. The relational structure of love (always toward another) makes community the necessary formation context. |
| OBS-103-T2-127 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Pe 1:22 | Both patterns appear in the data. Simultaneous: Exo 34:6 (OBS-103-069) — God is simultaneously rachum AND channun AND erek-appayim AND rab-chesed AND emet — five love-modes co-present as one divine character. Lam 3:22 (OBS-103-088) — chesed AND rachamim named together. Song 5:2 (OBS-103-108) — the beloved is simultaneously sister, love, dove, perfect one — multiple love-names applied simultaneously. Sequential: 1Jo 4:19 — divine love first, then human love in response (sequential). 1Pe 1:22 — obedience → purification → sincere love (sequential formation). Rom 5:5 → Gal 5:22 — Spirit infusion → fruit production (sequential). The data does not resolve to a single pattern — love's modes cohere simultaneously in their full expression (the divine portrait) and arise sequentially in their human formation. |
| OBS-103-T2-132 | OBSERVATION | 1Pe 1:22 | Already addressed at T5.1 (Q&A-091) and T5.1.2 (Q&A-092). Yes — 1Th 3:12 ('may the Lord make you increase and abound in love'); Rut 3:10 (this chesed greater than the first). Means: divine action (Lord makes it increase), Spirit's work (Gal 5:22), training (Tit 2:4), obedience-pathway (1Pe 1:22). No additional findings beyond Q&A-091 and Q&A-092. |
| OBS-103-T2-139 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 12:10 | Yes — and the data names the direction explicitly. The reorganisation operates in three directions: (a) **Restructuring of kinship** — Mat 12:50 (OBS-103-063): the family is restructured around shared orientation toward God rather than natural kinship. Love's community replaces the natural family as the primary social unit. (b) **Inversion of competitive hierarchy** — Rom 12:10 (OBS-103-119): 'outdo one another in showing honor' — the social competition is reversed from honour-claiming to honour-giving. Love reorganises from rivalry to generosity. (c) **Inclusion of the excluded** — Mat 5:44 (OBS-103-143): enemy-love includes those whom natural social dynamics exclude. The direction of love's social reorganisation is consistently: from exclusion toward inclusion, from taking toward giving, from natural kinship toward chosen community. The community constituted by love (Joh 13:35) is a social body organised on principles that invert the natural social order. |
