### G2155 eusplanchnos — compassionate

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G2155` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R183 heart · Legacy C-cluster: C01
3. Sub-group: `M05-B` (Compassion)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 2
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=593` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
6. Mounce short-def: compassionate, tenderhearted

**Verses:** 2 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2717 | 593-001 | Term names the inner quality of compassion — the tender-hearted disposition marked by kindness and mercy |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (24 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-064-T2-232 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Yes — commanded horizontal forgiveness is richly evidenced. Mat 18:35 (forgive from the heart), Luk 17:3-4 (if your brother sins, rebuke him; if he repents, forgive him — seven times a day), Mat 18:22 (seventy times seven). Eph 4:32 ("forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you"). OBS-064-031. |
| OBS-064-T2-252 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Yes — the commanded forgiveness ("seventy times seven" — Mat 18:22) positions forgiveness as a formation discipline. Habitual forgiveness-giving forms the character of the forgiver toward generosity of relational posture. The Eph 4:32 command ("forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you") places horizontal forgiveness within the sanctification framework of corporate Christian life. OBS-064-031. |
| OBS-064-T2-253 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Forgiveness functions in sanctification as both a discipline and a formation vehicle: the habitual act of releasing claims shapes the person toward the divine model (Eph 4:32 — "as God in Christ forgave you"). The person who habitually forgives is being progressively formed into the image of the divine forgiver. OBS-064-031. |
| OBS-099-T2-003 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Two purposes: (a) Relational — kindness equips the person to inhabit covenant; Mic 6:8 frames it as the inner orientation through which the covenant partner relates to God and others [OBS-099-052]. (b) Missional — kindness in the human person propagates divine character into the relational world; Eph 4:32 makes this explicit [OBS-099-077]. Created purpose: to make the human being a bearer and transmitter of divine relational character. |
| OBS-099-T2-006 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Kindness instantiates the outward-oriented relational disposition of God toward others. Mic 6:8 and Eph 4:32 together reveal that the relational-moral complex (justice, kindness, humility; kindness, tenderheartedness, forgiveness) is what the image-bearer embodies [OBS-099-052, OBS-099-077]. The specific divine likeness instantiated: the freedom and generosity to act in favour of another without compulsion — Ruth's kindness to Naomi (Rut 3:10 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-086]) is the clearest human illustration. |
| OBS-099-T2-010 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Primary direction: divine-to-human. Eph 4:32 — "be kind... as God in Christ forgave you" [OBS-099-077]. The divine instance establishes the pattern; human kindness is the derived expression. Secondary direction: human-pointing-to-divine — each human chesed act points toward the divine kindness it participates in. Both directions present; primary movement is from divine instance to human response. |
| OBS-099-T2-015 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Composite — four consistent internal constituents: (a) faithfulness/truth (emet) — chesed is persistently paired with emet [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]; (b) justice (mishpat) — Mic 6:8 pairs kindness with justice and humility as a single moral-relational complex [OBS-099-052]; (c) tenderheartedness (compassion) — Eph 4:32 [OBS-099-077]; (d) forgiveness — same verse [OBS-099-077, SP-099-009]. Internal structure: loyalty element + justice element + compassion element + forgiveness element. Structurally composite. |
| OBS-099-T2-016 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Working description: Kindness is a stable inner disposition of loyal goodwill toward another, rooted in the heart, that orients the whole person toward the practical benefit and relational flourishing of its object, regardless of desert. Constituted by faithfulness (holds across time and through failure), compassion (feels with the other), and absence of self-serving motivation. In divine form: inexhaustible, unconditional in ultimate terms, purposively aimed at transformation. In human form: creaturely, finite, Spirit-formed, called to mirror the divine pattern. Primary constitutional location: heart. Primary expression: deliberate relational action. Counterfeit: pleasant speech covering self-serving motivation (chrēstologia). |
| OBS-099-T2-019 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | (a) Not the same as love — 1Cor 13:4 places kindness as a mode of love's operation; love is the parent characteristic [OBS-099-072]. (b) Not compassion — compassion is visceral (womb-feeling); chesed is dispositional and covenantal [OBS-099-009]. (c) Not grace — grace names the favour; kindness names the disposition that extends it. (d) Not mere pleasantness — chrēstos includes ease but chesed's covenantal depth exceeds pleasant affect. Gap: precise kindness-love and kindness-mercy/compassion boundaries require Session D. SP-099-006 covers kindness-love boundary. |
| OBS-099-T2-031 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Primary dimension: Relational Disposition (06). Kindness is fundamentally an orientation-toward-another — stable inner stance directed outward toward a relational partner. The chesed vocabulary is constitutively relational: it exists within and sustains covenant. Eight of 27 OWNER groups carry Relational Disposition — largest single dimension. H2617A group 536-001 (180 relevant verses, dominant subject GOD [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-083]) confirms at scale. |
| OBS-099-T2-033 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Yes — two strong secondary dimensions and one weaker: (a) Moral Character (05): seven OWNER groups carry this; H2617A group 536-002 (human chesed as moral character quality) and H2623 (chasid as moral character identity) confirm it [XREG: H2617A/H2623, OBS-099-083/094]. (b) Divine-Human Correspondence (11): 2Sa 22:26 / Eph 4:32 mirror structures are strong evidence [XREG: H2616A, G5544 group 886-001, OBS-099-096/090]. (c) Formative/Transformation: Gal 5:22 and Rom 2:4 show kindness operating formatively — thinner evidence, may properly belong to T5 rather than as dimension. |
| OBS-099-T2-039 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Yes — explicitly. Pro 3:3 — "write them on the tablet of your heart" (chesed and faithfulness) [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]. The heart is the constitutional site where kindness as disposition is inscribed. Eph 4:32 — "tenderhearted" paired with kindness [OBS-099-077]. Gen 6:5 — the heart as generative centre: kind acts from kind heart; evil acts from corrupted heart [OBS-099-046]. |
| OBS-099-T2-040 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Heart's moral-volitional-affective function — spanning: (a) moral formation (inscribed character — Pro 3:3 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]); (b) affective response (tenderheartedness — Eph 4:32 [OBS-099-077]); (c) covenantal orientation (heart turning toward or away — Jer 17:5 [OBS-099-047]). Kindness engages the whole integrating function of the heart, not a single facet. |
| OBS-099-T2-041 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Belly/viscera (splanchna) referenced as adjacent — Eph 4:32 (eusplanchnoi — compassionate hearts) in direct combination with kindness [OBS-099-077]. Kindness has an affective-visceral accompaniment (tenderheartedness) but is not itself a visceral phenomenon the way compassion (racham) is. |
| OBS-099-T2-054 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Kindness is one of the primary characteristics that builds and sustains relational connection — it constitutes relationships in their quality, not merely accompanies them. Chesed is the inner substance of covenant relationship [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-083]. The im.ma.di groups frame kindness as the quality making relational presence real rather than nominal [OBS-099-065–071]. 1Sa 20:14 — request for relational faithfulness to be maintained into the future [OBS-099-070]. The chasid's relational quality is his defining identity before God and others [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. The person without kindness has relationships in form but not in substance. |
| OBS-099-T2-057 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Well-evidenced. (a) How: specific deliberate acts — Ruth to Naomi (Rut 3:10 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-086]); David to Hanun (2Sa 10:2 [OBS-099-071]); Jonathan/David (1Sa 20:14 [OBS-099-070]); Judah's surety-pledge (Gen 44:32 [OBS-099-063]). Each concrete, costly, relational. (b) Inner conditions in giver: the chasid — settled inner character [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]; heart-inscription prior to the act (Pro 3:3 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]). (c) What the person must have received: Eph 4:32 — "as God in Christ forgave you, be kind" [OBS-099-077]. Grounded in having received divine kindness/forgiveness first. |
| OBS-099-T2-067 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Two partial sequences: (a) Eph 4:32 cluster — kindness → tenderheartedness → forgiveness [OBS-099-077, SP-099-009]. Whether strictly sequential or co-present is not resolved by the verse — listed together without specified order. (b) Rom 2:4 — encounter with divine kindness → recognition → repentance — explicitly sequential [OBS-099-076, Q&A-072]. Kindness precedes repentance; it is the mechanism leading to it. Gap: explicit sequential relationships (A causes B, enables C) are partially implied but not extensively narrated. |
| OBS-099-T2-070 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Partial evidence for three boundaries: (a) Kindness vs. love: 1Cor 13:4 — kindness is a mode of love's operation, not its definition [OBS-099-072, Q&A-021]. Love is broader; kindness is the specific outward-oriented gracious-action mode. (b) Kindness vs. compassion: Eph 4:32 — kindness and tenderheartedness are distinct qualities [OBS-099-077]. Compassion feels; kindness acts from that feeling. (c) Kindness vs. grace: Eph 2:7 — grace names the favour; kindness names the disposition in which the favour is given [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Gap: boundaries implied by co-occurrence and context but not explicitly theorised. Session D needed for full boundary analysis. |
| OBS-099-T2-071 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Three sets with structural significance: (a) Mic 6:8 — shared with Regs 23, 24, 67, 80, 103, 173, 177 [OBS-099-038, SP-099-003]. Justice, kindness, humility in a single normative statement. Density of shared registry anchors signals this is the C17 cluster's most central verse. (b) 1Cor 13:4 — shared with Regs 103, 116, 123 [OBS-099-039, SP-099-006]. NT equivalent of the Mic 6:8 compression. (c) Eph 4:32 — shared with Regs 68, 183 [OBS-099-041]. Kindness-tenderheartedness-forgiveness cluster grounded in divine grace. |
| OBS-099-T2-073 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | The lexical foundation is now strong following cross-registry supplementation. Primary terms: H2617A (chesed — 228 relevant verses, 3 groups covering divine and human chesed and its absence); H2623 (chasid — 31 relevant verses); H2616A (chasad verbal — 2 verses); G5543/G5544 (chrēstos/chrēstotēs — 12 relevant verses combined). Secondary OWNER terms: G5541 (chrēsteuomai — 1 verse), G5542 (chrēstologia — 1 verse). The lexical foundation spans both Testaments, action and noun forms, the person embodying the characteristic, and its counterfeit. Following cross-registry supplementation: comprehensive. One remaining gap: LXX background for Greek terms — LSJ data sparse for OWNER Greek terms [OBS-099-021]. |
| OBS-099-T2-075 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | (P1 — contextual settings) Psalmic lament and praise (dominant for chesed); narrative of covenant relationships (Ruth, Genesis, Samuel-Kings); prophetic indictment (Hos 6:6, Mic 6:8); NT epistolary instruction (Eph 4:32, Col 3:12, Rom 2:4). (P2 — literary forms) Lament, praise, narrative, instruction, argument — kindness appears across all major literary forms; not genre-specific. (P5 — primary anchor verse) Lam 3:22: "The steadfast love of the Lord never ceases; his mercies never come to an end." This verse asserts the permanence and inexhaustibility of divine kindness in the context of maximum suffering and loss. It reveals what no other verse reveals: chesed is not contingent on circumstances or human faithfulness — it persists when everything else has failed. Every other kindness statement in the evidence is a specific expression of this; Lam 3:22 is the structural foundation. |
| OBS-099-T2-081 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Yes — and substantively. The evidence shows kindness engaging the affective faculty in multiple directions. (a) Receiving divine chesed produces affective states: Psa 63:3 — "your steadfast love is better than life; my lips will praise you" — the receipt of divine chesed generates an affective orientation of praise and joy [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-084]. Psa 23:4 — "I will fear no evil, for you are with me" — the displacement of fear by security is an affective change produced by the experience of divine kindness-as-presence [OBS-099-066]. (b) Kindness involves an affective orientation in the giver: Eph 4:32 — "tenderhearted" (eusplanchnoi — literally "good-bowelled," an affective visceral term) is placed immediately alongside kindness, suggesting that genuine kindness carries an affective warmth component — it does not operate as cold duty [OBS-099-077]. (c) The absence of kindness produces grief: Gen 44:29 — Jacob's anticipatory grief at losing Benjamin — the possibility of losing the object of kindness generates profound affective distress [OBS-099-062]. The affective faculty is engaged at all three interfaces: receiving kindness (produces joy/security), giving kindness (accompanied by tenderheartedness), and threatened absence of kindness (produces grief/distress). |
| OBS-099-T2-082 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | Kindness primarily enables and deepens affect — it does not bypass or impair it. Three patterns are evidenced. (a) Enables affect: the person who has received divine chesed acquires an affective capacity that exceeds what they would otherwise have — Lam 3:22–23 situates hope (an affective orientation) in the context of Lamentations (total devastation) specifically because chesed is inexhaustible [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-085]. The receipt of kindness opens an affective register that devastation alone would close. (b) Deepens affect: the chasid formation arc [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094] — the person shaped by chesed over a lifetime — develops deeper affective sensitivity to the relational world. The paired term in Eph 4:32 (eusplanchnoi — compassionate hearts) suggests that the formation of kindness goes alongside deepening affective resonance with others. (c) The counterfeit (chrēstologia — Rom 16:18 [OBS-099-074]) reveals what bypassed affect looks like: pleasant words without inner disposition is the pathology of kindness-form without affective substance. Genuine kindness cannot be bypassed from affect — the counterfeit that does so is named and condemned. |
| OBS-099-T2-083 | OBSERVATION | Eph 4:32 | The consistent affective engagement of kindness in the evidence reveals that kindness is not a cold duty-virtue but a warm relational disposition. The tenderheartedness (eusplanchnoi) co-occurrence in Eph 4:32, the praise-response to received chesed (Psa 63:3), and the grief at anticipated loss of kindness (Gen 44:29) together show that affect is intrinsic to kindness's character, not merely an accompaniment. This is analytically significant for the working definition established at Q&A-018: the definition named "compassion (feels with the other)" as a constituent element of kindness — the T3.4 finding confirms this element. A kindness that operates without affective engagement is not the full biblical phenomenon — it may be correctness or duty, but chesed/chrēstotēs carries feeling as well as faithfulness. The pattern also distinguishes kindness from justice: justice can be rendered without personal feeling; chesed in the evidence is always accompanied by some form of affective resonance with the other. |

### G4834 sumpatheō — to sympathize

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G4834` · Lang: Greek · Owner: —
2. Registry: R023 compassion · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-B` (Compassion)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 2
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=734` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`
6. Mounce short-def: to sympathize with

**Verses:** 2 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 334 | 734-001 | Term names the inner capacity for fellow-feeling — the disposition to enter into and share the experience of another's suffering |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (1)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-023-T2-133 | OBSERVATION |  | The primary terms and their root meanings, drawn from the term inventory and Pass 1 sense analyses: **Hebrew primary terms:** - **H7355 ra.cham / H7356B ra.cha.mim** (XREF, 43/39 occurrences): verbal and plural noun forms from the RACHAM root. Root meaning: the womb (re.chem, H7358). Compassion named from the body's most interior generative space. The plural form (ra.cha.mim) signals a multiple, accumulated, inexhaustible quality — grammatically plural in the most common form. - **H7349 ra.chum** (XREF, 13 occurrences): adjectival form — "compassionate" as character quality. Used almost exclusively as a divine epithet in the Exod 34:6 formula. - **H2347 chus** (OWNER, 24 active occurrences): verbal — "to pity, spare." Root connection to darkened (H2345 chum) through phonological similarity rather than semantic affinity. Appears predominantly in judicial-prohibition contexts. - **H2617B chesed / H2616A chasad** (OWNER, 169/2 occurrences): steadfast love / be kind. Root: CHASAD — spanning covenantal loyalty and shame/disgrace. The most theologically weighted vocabulary in the OT; foundational to covenantal relationship. - **H2587 channum** (OWNER, 13 occurrences): gracious — adjective from the CHANAN root (favour/grace). Divine epithet in Exod 34:6 paired with rachum. **Greek primary terms:** - **G4697 splanchnizō** (OWNER, 12 active occurrences): verbal — "to be moved with compassion." Root: splanchnon (G4698, entrails/affections). The gut-movement verb — the most precise single term for compassion as inner somatic event in the NT. - **G4698 splanchnon** (XREF, 11 occurrences): noun — entrails/affections/compassion. The anatomical root of the splanchnizō family. - **G4184 polusplanchnos** (OWNER, 1 occurrence): compound hapax — "very/deeply compassionate" (polus + splanchnon). Coined for Jas 5:11 to name divine compassion beyond the range of existing vocabulary. - **G4834/4835 sumpatheō/sumpathēs** (OWNER, 2/1 occurrences): verbal and adjectival — "to sympathise / sympathetic." Root: sum (with) + pathos (suffering/experience). Names compassion as participatory solidarity — suffering-with. **Root meanings summary:** Both primary root families (RACHAM/womb; SPLANCHN/entrails) ground compassion in the body's interior. Both move from concrete anatomical reality to abstract inner-being concept. The convergence of two independent linguistic traditions on the same anatomical grounding is the defining lexical feature of this characteristic. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (0 unique)**

(none)

**SD pointers — direct (2)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-023-SD010 | HIGH |  | Luke 10:33 — the Good Samaritan is splanchnizō toward the wounded man. This is the only unambiguous human use of the term outside parables. The verse connects compassion directly to the question "who is my neighbour?" — making compassion the defining quality of the one who correctly answers that question. This connects to Love (Reg 103 — "love your neighbour") and potentially Mercy (Reg 111 — "go and do likewise" in v.37 uses eleos). The structural observation: compassion (splanchnizō) generates mercy (eleos) in Luke 10:33-37 — the inward movement produces the outward act. This sequence matters for Session D. --- #### Term 14: G4834 sumpatheō — to sympathise (occ=2, extracted) *Sense structure: Two occurrences: Heb 4:15 (Christ sympathising with our weaknesses) and Heb 10:34 ("you had compassion on those in prison"). The verb names entering into another's experience — literally "suffering-with." LSJ first meaning: "to be sympathetically affected, to have the same thing happen to one"; notes the classical philosophical use of the soul and body sympathising with each other. Semantic observation:* The classical philosophical usage (soul-body sympatheia) is significant for the spirit-soul-body classification work in Pass 4. The term originated in Stoic philosophy to describe the interconnection of parts of a whole — the way the wellbeing of one part affects all others. The NT reappropriates this concept for the community of faith and for Christology. |
| DIM-023-SD020 | MEDIUM |  | Mark 1:41 — compassion (splanchnizō) produces touch across the ritual-purity boundary. This connects compassion to purity/defilement vocabulary (Reg 125, Reg 41) in a structurally significant way: compassion overrides the inner orientation toward self-protection and social boundary-maintenance. Cross-registry: Purity (Reg 125), Defilement (Reg 41). *H5150 ni.chum — Hos 11:8 (group 733-001) Session C annotation confirmed accurate and complete. Additional observation: the verse opens with four rhetorical questions — "How can I give you up? How can I hand you over? How can I make you like Admah? How can I treat you like Zeboiim?" — before naming the inner movement. The questions record God's inner deliberation between judgment and compassion. The ni.chum is not presented as the suppression of judgment but as the winning of an inner debate. This has significant implications for theological anthropology: the inner life of God (as the text presents it) is not static but deliberative. FRAMEWORK SIGNAL noted: H5150 ni.chum / Hos 11:8 — the divine inner deliberation between judgment and compassion represents a unique category in the programme data: the dynamic quality of divine compassion, where it contends with and overcomes competing inner dispositions. This is relevant to the spirit-soul-body classification and to the session B finding DIM-23-001. G4834 sumpatheō — Heb 10:34 (non-anchor) "You had compassion (sumpatheō) on those in prison, and you joyfully accepted the plundering of your property, since you knew that you yourselves had a better possession and an abiding one." This is the only NT verse where sumpatheō clearly has a human-to-human subject (the readers showing compassion to imprisoned believers). The compassion is expressed through material sacrifice and joy — not grief. |

### H2616A cha.sad — be kind

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H2616A` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R023 compassion · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-A` (Love)
4. Term status: extracted_thin · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 2
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=1635` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`

**Verses:** 2 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 343 | 1635-001 | Term names the responsive quality of inner covenantal mercy — the faithfulness of character that draws out corresponding faithfulness from God |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (9)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-023-T2-006 | OBSERVATION |  | The evidence strongly suggests genuine sharing, not merely analogical correspondence. Three lines of evidence support this: **1. The same vocabulary applies to both.** Splanchnizō (G4697) is used of Jesus (Synoptic Gospels, 11 occurrences) and of the human person (Luke 10:33 — the Good Samaritan; Luke 15:20 — the father of the prodigal; Matt 18:27 — the king in the parable). The vocabulary does not bifurcate into a divine term and a human term — the same term governs both. This implies the inner-being reality being named is the same, not merely analogous. **2. The sumpatheō vocabulary grounds the sharing Christologically.** Heb 4:15 names Christ's capacity to sympathise (sumpatheō) with human weaknesses, grounded in his own experience of temptation. The priestly solidarity is genuine: Christ shares in the human condition in order to share in the human inner experience of suffering. This is not analogy — it is participation (SD-002). **3. The mirroring principle.** 2 Sam 22:26 / Psa 18:25 (H2616A cha.sad): "With the merciful you show yourself merciful." This establishes that God's compassionate disposition toward the human person is itself responsive to the person's inner character. The sharing runs in both directions: not only does God's compassion establish the pattern for human compassion, but human compassionate character draws out corresponding divine response (SD-007). The data does not support a reading in which compassion is exclusively a creaturely analogue — the vocabulary, the Christological grounding, and the mirroring principle all point toward genuine sharing across the Creator-creature boundary. |
| OBS-023-T2-123 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — the data identifies three root families that cross registry boundaries at root level (from Pass 2 and the XREF analysis): **RACHAM root:** H7356A ra.cham (womb/compassion, OWNER in R23) and H7356B ra.cha.mim (compassion, XREF in R23) share the same root as the love vocabulary in R103. SD-001 raises: does the womb-root function as a unifying somatic image across both compassion and love, or does it serve different semantic purposes in each? The root-level connection is structural: the same root generates both the compassion vocabulary and a significant portion of the love vocabulary. **CHASAD root:** H2616A cha.sad (be kind, OWNER in R23) and H2617B chesed/shame (OWNER in R23) share the CHASAD root with H2616B cha.sad (to shame, OWNER in R146) and H2617A chesed (kindness, XREF in R23/R99/R104). This root crosses two clusters — C17 (compassion, love, kindness) and the shame cluster (C06 or equivalent). SD-008 raises the most theologically acute question: does the root-level connection between chesed and shame reflect a genuine semantic relationship — covenantal violation producing shame as its consequence — or is this purely lexicographic? **ATAR root:** The data notes a shared root between mercy (R111) and compassion (R23) through the ATAR vocabulary (Pass 2 root families table). Both remain within C17. **CHIN/CHEN root collision:** The data notes a Stage 1 flag on the root collision between channum (graciousness, OWNER in R23) and chen (favour/grace, XREF in R23, OWNER in R68). The shared root raises the SD-006 boundary question between graciousness (character quality) and grace (relational gift). |
| OBS-023-T2-133 | OBSERVATION |  | The primary terms and their root meanings, drawn from the term inventory and Pass 1 sense analyses: **Hebrew primary terms:** - **H7355 ra.cham / H7356B ra.cha.mim** (XREF, 43/39 occurrences): verbal and plural noun forms from the RACHAM root. Root meaning: the womb (re.chem, H7358). Compassion named from the body's most interior generative space. The plural form (ra.cha.mim) signals a multiple, accumulated, inexhaustible quality — grammatically plural in the most common form. - **H7349 ra.chum** (XREF, 13 occurrences): adjectival form — "compassionate" as character quality. Used almost exclusively as a divine epithet in the Exod 34:6 formula. - **H2347 chus** (OWNER, 24 active occurrences): verbal — "to pity, spare." Root connection to darkened (H2345 chum) through phonological similarity rather than semantic affinity. Appears predominantly in judicial-prohibition contexts. - **H2617B chesed / H2616A chasad** (OWNER, 169/2 occurrences): steadfast love / be kind. Root: CHASAD — spanning covenantal loyalty and shame/disgrace. The most theologically weighted vocabulary in the OT; foundational to covenantal relationship. - **H2587 channum** (OWNER, 13 occurrences): gracious — adjective from the CHANAN root (favour/grace). Divine epithet in Exod 34:6 paired with rachum. **Greek primary terms:** - **G4697 splanchnizō** (OWNER, 12 active occurrences): verbal — "to be moved with compassion." Root: splanchnon (G4698, entrails/affections). The gut-movement verb — the most precise single term for compassion as inner somatic event in the NT. - **G4698 splanchnon** (XREF, 11 occurrences): noun — entrails/affections/compassion. The anatomical root of the splanchnizō family. - **G4184 polusplanchnos** (OWNER, 1 occurrence): compound hapax — "very/deeply compassionate" (polus + splanchnon). Coined for Jas 5:11 to name divine compassion beyond the range of existing vocabulary. - **G4834/4835 sumpatheō/sumpathēs** (OWNER, 2/1 occurrences): verbal and adjectival — "to sympathise / sympathetic." Root: sum (with) + pathos (suffering/experience). Names compassion as participatory solidarity — suffering-with. **Root meanings summary:** Both primary root families (RACHAM/womb; SPLANCHN/entrails) ground compassion in the body's interior. Both move from concrete anatomical reality to abstract inner-being concept. The convergence of two independent linguistic traditions on the same anatomical grounding is the defining lexical feature of this characteristic. |
| OBS-099-T2-007 | OBSERVATION | 2Sa 22:26 · Rut 3:10 · Hos 6:6 | Genuinely shared, with structural asymmetry. The same terms (chesed, chrēstos/chrēstotēs) are predicated of both without distinction of kind. 2Sa 22:26 makes the sharing explicit: "With the merciful you show yourself merciful" [XREG: H2616A, OBS-099-096]. Asymmetry: God's chesed is inexhaustible and eschatologically guaranteed; human chesed is creaturely and can be withdrawn (Hos 6:6 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]). The sharing is real; the asymmetry is structural. |
| OBS-099-T2-033 | OBSERVATION | Psa 4:3 · 2Sa 22:26 · Eph 4:32 | Yes — two strong secondary dimensions and one weaker: (a) Moral Character (05): seven OWNER groups carry this; H2617A group 536-002 (human chesed as moral character quality) and H2623 (chasid as moral character identity) confirm it [XREG: H2617A/H2623, OBS-099-083/094]. (b) Divine-Human Correspondence (11): 2Sa 22:26 / Eph 4:32 mirror structures are strong evidence [XREG: H2616A, G5544 group 886-001, OBS-099-096/090]. (c) Formative/Transformation: Gal 5:22 and Rom 2:4 show kindness operating formatively — thinner evidence, may properly belong to T5 rather than as dimension. |
| OBS-099-T2-056 | OBSERVATION | Psa 86:2 · 2Sa 22:26 · Psa 69:13 | Evidenced primarily in appeal/petition mode and chasid identity. (a) Appeal mode: psalmist appeals to God's chesed as ground for being heard — "in the abundance of your steadfast love answer me" (Psa 69:13 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]). Inner posture: dependence and trust. (b) Chasid orientation: Psa 86:2 — "preserve my life, for I am godly (chasid)" — character appealing, not merit-claiming [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. (c) Mirror dynamic: 2Sa 22:26 — human chesed character draws out divine chesed response [XREG: H2616A, OBS-099-096]. Human-to-God kindness is not benevolence toward God but the orientation that mirrors God's own character. |
| OBS-099-T2-069 | OBSERVATION | Pro 3:3 · 2Sa 22:26 · 1Cor 13:4 | Four root-sharing relationships: (a) CHESED root family: H2617A (chesed), H2616A (chasad — be kind), H2617B (chesed — shame), H2623 (chasid — pious/kind person). Shame-semantic of same root analytically significant: loyalty/faithfulness and shame are two faces of the same covenantal reality [OBS-099-008]. (b) CHRĒS root family (Greek): G5541 (chrēsteuomai), G5542 (chrēstologia), G5543 (chrēstos), G5544 (chrēstotēs) — kindness, action form, counterfeit, abstract noun [OBS-099-021]. (c) AM/IM root family: etymological connection without semantic kindness content [OBS-099-018]. |
| OBS-099-T2-073 | OBSERVATION | Lam 3:22 · Psa 63:3 · Gal 5:22 · Eph 4:32 | The lexical foundation is now strong following cross-registry supplementation. Primary terms: H2617A (chesed — 228 relevant verses, 3 groups covering divine and human chesed and its absence); H2623 (chasid — 31 relevant verses); H2616A (chasad verbal — 2 verses); G5543/G5544 (chrēstos/chrēstotēs — 12 relevant verses combined). Secondary OWNER terms: G5541 (chrēsteuomai — 1 verse), G5542 (chrēstologia — 1 verse). The lexical foundation spans both Testaments, action and noun forms, the person embodying the characteristic, and its counterfeit. Following cross-registry supplementation: comprehensive. One remaining gap: LXX background for Greek terms — LSJ data sparse for OWNER Greek terms [OBS-099-021]. |
| OBS-099-T2-089 | OBSERVATION | Lam 3:22 · Psa 4:3 · 1Cor 13:4 · Rom 16:18 | The full vocabulary arc for kindness spans Hebrew and Greek, noun and verb, abstract quality and embodied person, genuine expression and named counterfeit, positive attribute and negative consequence — and this breadth is itself analytically significant. Reading the arc as a whole: (a) The Hebrew root family (H2617A chesed — loyal kindness as noun; H2616A chasad — to be kind as verb; H2623 chasid — the kind person as identity; H2617B — shame/reproach as the negative consequence of its failure) covers the complete semantic circuit: what kindness is, how it is enacted, who embodies it, and what happens when it is absent or fails. The inclusion of shame as the negative of chesed reveals that chesed operates within an honour-covenant framework — its failure brings reproach, not merely disappointment [OBS-099-008]. (b) The Greek root family (G5543 chrēstos — kind as quality; G5544 chrēstotēs — kindness as abstract noun; G5541 chrēsteuomai — to act kindly as verb; G5542 chrēstologia — pleasant speech as counterfeit) similarly covers quality, abstraction, action, and counterfeit. The coinage of chrēstologia as a specific term for the counterfeit reveals that the Greek-speaking community around Paul had encountered false-kindness frequently enough to need a named category for it [OBS-099-021/023]. (c) Taken together, the vocabulary arc reveals that kindness is semantically rich in both Testaments — it is not a thin peripheral term but a conceptually dense one with its own vocabulary of enactment (verb forms), personification (chasid), negative (shame, severity), and falsification (chrēstologia). The complete semantic range encompasses: loyal covenant love (chesed), benign quality of character (chrēstos), the Spirit-produced virtue (chrēstotēs), the active exercise of love in the kind mode (chrēsteuomai), the person whose identity is shaped by this loyalty (chasid), and the imitative counterfeit (chrēstologia). This arc from concept to embodiment to action to falsification reveals kindness as one of the programme's most structurally complete vocabulary fields. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (4 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-099-T2-007 | OBSERVATION | 2Sa 22:26 | Genuinely shared, with structural asymmetry. The same terms (chesed, chrēstos/chrēstotēs) are predicated of both without distinction of kind. 2Sa 22:26 makes the sharing explicit: "With the merciful you show yourself merciful" [XREG: H2616A, OBS-099-096]. Asymmetry: God's chesed is inexhaustible and eschatologically guaranteed; human chesed is creaturely and can be withdrawn (Hos 6:6 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]). The sharing is real; the asymmetry is structural. |
| OBS-099-T2-033 | OBSERVATION | 2Sa 22:26 | Yes — two strong secondary dimensions and one weaker: (a) Moral Character (05): seven OWNER groups carry this; H2617A group 536-002 (human chesed as moral character quality) and H2623 (chasid as moral character identity) confirm it [XREG: H2617A/H2623, OBS-099-083/094]. (b) Divine-Human Correspondence (11): 2Sa 22:26 / Eph 4:32 mirror structures are strong evidence [XREG: H2616A, G5544 group 886-001, OBS-099-096/090]. (c) Formative/Transformation: Gal 5:22 and Rom 2:4 show kindness operating formatively — thinner evidence, may properly belong to T5 rather than as dimension. |
| OBS-099-T2-056 | OBSERVATION | 2Sa 22:26 | Evidenced primarily in appeal/petition mode and chasid identity. (a) Appeal mode: psalmist appeals to God's chesed as ground for being heard — "in the abundance of your steadfast love answer me" (Psa 69:13 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]). Inner posture: dependence and trust. (b) Chasid orientation: Psa 86:2 — "preserve my life, for I am godly (chasid)" — character appealing, not merit-claiming [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. (c) Mirror dynamic: 2Sa 22:26 — human chesed character draws out divine chesed response [XREG: H2616A, OBS-099-096]. Human-to-God kindness is not benevolence toward God but the orientation that mirrors God's own character. |
| OBS-099-T2-069 | OBSERVATION | 2Sa 22:26 | Four root-sharing relationships: (a) CHESED root family: H2617A (chesed), H2616A (chasad — be kind), H2617B (chesed — shame), H2623 (chasid — pious/kind person). Shame-semantic of same root analytically significant: loyalty/faithfulness and shame are two faces of the same covenantal reality [OBS-099-008]. (b) CHRĒS root family (Greek): G5541 (chrēsteuomai), G5542 (chrēstologia), G5543 (chrēstos), G5544 (chrēstotēs) — kindness, action form, counterfeit, abstract noun [OBS-099-021]. (c) AM/IM root family: etymological connection without semantic kindness content [OBS-099-018]. |

**SD pointers — direct (2)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-023-SD006 | HIGH |  | Chan.nun (gracious, Reg 23) and chen (favour/grace, XREF in Reg 23, OWNER in Reg 68 Grace) share the same Hebrew root (חנן — the CHIN/CHEN root code collision noted in Stage 1). Exod 34:6 is a shared anchor verse for Reg 23 and Reg 68. The question: is graciousness (the character quality, channum) distinct from grace (the relational gift, chen), and if so, where does the inner-being boundary lie between them? This is the most direct compassion/grace boundary question in the programme. --- #### Term 7: H2594 cha.ni.nah — favour (occ=1, extracted_thin) *Sense structure: Single occurrence: Jer 16:13. The verse is a divine judgment speech — "I will hurl you out of this land into a land that neither you nor your fathers have known, and there you shall serve other gods day and night, for I will show you no favour (cha.ni.nah)." The term names the withdrawing of divine favour as judicial consequence. Same root as chan.nun and chen. The term occurs once in a context of its absence — like H2617B che.sed (shame), which is another register of chesed absence. Semantic observation: This is the noun form of the gracious-favour vocabulary. Its single occurrence in a withdrawal-of-favour context mirrors the pattern seen in H2347 chus (pity mostly appearing in prohibition contexts) and H2617B che.sed (shame as the negative pole of chesed). The compassion vocabulary defines itself in part through naming its own absence and its judicial withdrawal. --- #### Term 8: H2616A cha.sad — be kind (occ=2, extracted_thin) Sense structure: The verbal form of the chesed root. Two occurrences, both the identical verse: 2 Sam 22:26 = Ps 18:25 ("With the merciful you show yourself merciful"). The hitpael form (hitchasad) means "to act kindly/show kindness toward." The verse establishes the mirror principle: God responds in kind to the inner disposition the person brings. |
| DIM-023-SD008 | HIGH |  | The CHASAD root (Reg 23 H2617B shame vs Reg 146 shame) and the CHASAD root (Reg 23 H2616A be kind) cross two clusters — C17 (compassion, love, mercy) and C06 (shame, contempt). The question for Session D: does the root-level connection between chesed (steadfast love) and shame reflect a genuine semantic relationship — that the violation of covenantal faithfulness produces shame as its consequence — or is this purely a lexicographic artefact? If genuine, it suggests the inner experience of shame is structurally connected to the failure of chesed in a way the programme should explore. --- #### Term 10: H5150 ni.chum — comfort (occ=3, extracted) *Sense structure: Noun from the na.cham root (to comfort/relent). Three verses: Isa 57:18 (divine comfort restored to the straying); Hos 11:8 (divine compassion "grows warm and tender" — ni.chumay nikmeru, "my compassions are kindled"); Zec 1:13 ("gracious and comforting words"). The term names the compassionate warmth that produces comfort — the inner stirring that precedes and motivates the comforting act. Semantic observation: Ni.chum sits at the intersection of compassion and comfort. It is not comfort as consolation-received but compassion as the motivating warmth from which comfort flows. Hos 11:8 is the key verse: the divine inner life stirs with ni.chum in response to the prospect of judgment — this stirring overrides the judicial decision. The term names the felt, warm, visceral quality of compassion at the moment it becomes action. Session B finding DIM-23-001 engaged: The Dimension Review finding asked whether ni.chum, splanchnizō, and ra.cham share a sub-pattern of "visceral inner movement" terms. Pass 1 confirms this: all three name compassion as a bodily movement from within — warm kindling (ni.chum), gut-stirring (splanchnizō), womb-love (ra.cham). This sub-pattern spans the divine-human boundary: God's compassion and human compassion are described with the same visceral language. |

### H2551 chem.lah — compassion

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H2551` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R023 compassion · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-B` (Compassion)
4. Term status: extracted_thin · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 2
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=2192` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`

**Verses:** 2 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 339 | 2192-001 | Term names divine compassion as the inward movement of mercy that acts on behalf of those in need, despite their unworthiness |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (2)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-023-T2-002 | OBSERVATION |  | Scripture attributes compassion to God with unusual directness, frequency, and vocabulary density. The GOD_AS_SUBJECT flag is warranted for H2587 chan.nun (13 occurrences, almost exclusively divine epithet), H2617B che.sed (predominant divine referent across Psalter, prophets, and historical books), H5150 ni.chum (2/3 occurrences divine), H7358 re.chem (God as actor over the womb in ~9/25 occurrences), H2551 chem.lah (Gen 19:16), G3627 oikteirō (Rom 9:15, exclusively divine sovereignty), G4697 splanchnizō (Jesus as primary subject in Gospels), and G4184 polusplanchnos (Jas 5:11) — as confirmed in the Pass 2 God-as-Subject table. What does this attribution reveal about the significance of compassion in the human person? Two implications follow from the data: First, compassion in the human person is a reflection of something that belongs primarily and essentially to God. The attribution of compassion to God is not metaphorical embellishment — it is the primary usage, and human compassion is derivative. The human person's capacity for compassion is therefore not merely a natural emotional response but a participation in the character of the one in whose image the person is made. This is reinforced by the fact that the RACHAM root is anatomical (re.chem, womb) — the human person's body is the ground from which the vocabulary of divine compassion is drawn. The direction of meaning flows from the human body to the divine inner life, but the direction of significance flows the other way: what the human experiences somatically, God possesses constitutively. Second, the divine character formula of Exod 34:6 (rachum ve-channum) functions as the standard of inner being that human compassion aspires to reflect. Joel 2:13 explicitly deploys this formula as the ground for human repentance and heart-rending ("return to the LORD your God, for he is gracious and merciful" — SD-029). The divine character is not merely the source of compassion but the norm against which human compassion is measured. |
| OBS-023-T2-084 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — the God-to-human direction is the most extensively evidenced direction in the entire compassion data. It is the dominant structural direction of the vocabulary. The Pass 2 God-as-Subject table confirms that the GOD_AS_SUBJECT flag is warranted for the majority of the primary terms: H2587 channum (almost exclusively divine epithet), H2617B chesed (predominant divine referent across Psalter, prophets, and historical books), H5150 ni.chum (2/3 occurrences divine), H2551 chem.lah (Gen 19:16 — divine), G3627 oikteirō (Rom 9:15 — exclusively divine sovereignty), G4697 splanchnizō (Jesus as primary Synoptic subject), G4184 polusplanchnos (Jas 5:11 — divine). **How it operates:** The God-to-human direction operates across several distinct modes: - **Constitutional declaration:** Exod 34:6 — God declares his own compassion-character as a defining attribute. This is not a response to a specific human situation but a self-revelation of inner character. God's compassion operates toward the human person as a prior fact of his nature. - **Responsive mercy in individual need:** Gen 19:16 (chem.lah — Lot), Jon 4:11 (chus — Nineveh), Isa 57:18 (ni.chum — the straying healed). God perceives the condition and moves toward the person in mercy. - **Sustained covenantal presence:** The chesed vocabulary in the Psalter (Psa 25:7; 48:9; Lam 3:22) names God's compassion as the continuous covenantal orientation toward the human person — not episodic but ongoing, "never ceasing." - **Incarnate movement:** Jesus's splanchnizō-movements (Matt 9:36; Luke 7:13; Mark 1:41) are the God-to-human direction of compassion in its most embodied, immediate form. The Incarnation does not merely describe divine compassion — it enacts it in the space of direct human encounter. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (0 unique)**

(none)

**SD pointers — direct (2)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-023-SD004 | HIGH |  | The high frequency of chus in judicial-prohibition contexts (Deut, Ezek) reveals a structural relationship between compassion and justice in the inner life: the default orientation is toward sparing; justice requires the deliberate overriding of that default. This is a programme-level observation about the architecture of the moral inner life. Connects to Justice (Reg 98), Guilt (Reg 73), and potentially Conscience (Reg 26). Question for Session D: does the inner life have a default orientation toward compassion that other moral demands must actively override, and if so, what does this reveal about the programme's understanding of the moral constitution of the human person? --- #### Term 5: H2551 chem.lah — compassion (occ=2, extracted_thin) *Sense structure: The related-words are to spare (H2550 cha.mal) and compassion — confirming this term sits in the sparing/mercy semantic space. Two verses: Gen 19:16 (God's mercy toward Lot despite his lingering) and Isa 63:9 ("in his love and in his pity he redeemed them"). Isa 63:9 is significant — chem.lah appears alongside divine love (ahavah) as the motivating disposition of God's redemptive act. Semantic observation: Chem.lah (compassion/pity) and cha.mal (to spare) share a root that emphasises restraint on behalf of the other* — the withholding of force or judgment in response to need. This is compassion expressed not as emotional movement but as mercy-in-action. |
| DIM-023-SD018 | MEDIUM |  | God's judicial withholding of compassion (H2347 chus in Ezek 7:4; H2594 cha.ni.nah in Jer 16:13) appears structurally alongside his active exercise of it (Jon 4:11; Gen 19:16). The inner-being question: what does the deliberate suspension of compassion reveal about its nature as a characteristic? Does it indicate that compassion is a disposition subject to governance by other attributes (justice, righteousness), or that it is a capacity that can be directed or withheld? Cross-registry: Justice (Reg 98), Righteousness (Reg 139), Anger/Wrath (Reg 4/178). --- ### PASS 2 — Divine Dimension *Date: 2026-04-11 #### God-as-Subject pattern by term \| Term \| Divine verses / Total \| Pattern \| \|---\|---\|---\| \| H2347 chus \| ~14/24 \| God primarily as the one who withholds pity in judgment (Ezek); also shows pity (Jon 4:11). Dominant: divine judicial withdrawal \| \| H2587 chan.nun \| ~11/13 \| Almost exclusively divine epithet. God as the gracious one. GOD_AS_SUBJECT flag warranted. \| \| H2617B che.sed \| ~87/169 \| Predominantly divine. God's steadfast love is primary referent across the Psalter, prophets, historical books. GOD_AS_SUBJECT flag warranted. \| \| H5150 ni.chum \| 2/3 \| Divine. God's compassionate comfort-movement. GOD_AS_SUBJECT flag warranted. \| \| H7356A ra.cham \| 1/5 \| The womb-metaphor verse (Isa 46:3) is divine. Anatomical uses are human. Partial. \| \| H7358 re.chem \| ~9/25 \| God as actor over the womb (opening, closing, consecrating). GOD_AS_SUBJECT flag warranted for these verses. \| \| H2551 chem.lah \| 1/2 \| Gen 19:16 — divine. GOD_AS_SUBJECT flag warranted. \| \| G3627 oikteirō \| 1/1 \| Rom 9:15 — exclusively divine sovereignty. GOD_AS_SUBJECT flag warranted. \| \| G4697 splanchnizō \| ~3/12 \| Jesus (God incarnate) as primary subject in Gospels. Human in one parable context (Lk 10:33). GOD_AS_SUBJECT flag warranted for Christological uses. \| \| G4184 polusplanchnos \| 1/1 \| Jas 5:11 — divine. GOD_AS_SUBJECT flag warranted. |

### G3629 oiktirmōn — compassionate

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G3629` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R111 mercy · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-B` (Compassion)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 15
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=3158` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`
6. Mounce short-def: merciful, compassionate

**Verses:** 2 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1639 | 3158-001 | Term names compassion as the inner character of God and the standard for human inner disposition — to be merciful as God is merciful |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (1)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-111-T2-139 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7, Heb 2:17, Jam 5:11 | Yes — *eleēmōn* (G1655 — the merciful one, Mat 5:7; Heb 2:17) and *oiktirmōn* (G3629 — the compassionate one, Luk 6:36; Jam 5:11). Hebrew person-type: *rachum* (compassionate — used of God as person-type adjective, e.g. Exo 34:6 — "a God merciful and gracious"). OBS-111-013. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (16 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-111-T2-001 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 6:36, Jam 5:11 | The verse evidence names divine mercy as the character of the Father (Luk 6:36 — "Be merciful as your Father is merciful"), as the inner compassion of the Lord (*polysplanchnos/oiktirmōn*, Jam 5:11), as the defining inner disposition ("Father of mercies," 2Cor 1:3), and as the ground of divine action (Psa 78:38 — "being compassionate, he atoned"). God's mercy is not incidental to his character — it is named as his defining disposition and as the motivation for his most significant acts (atonement, forgiveness, comfort). OBS-111-030, OBS-111-031, OBS-111-052, OBS-111-068. |
| OBS-111-T2-002 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 6:36 | Yes — Scripture explicitly and repeatedly attributes mercy to God. The attributions cluster around three patterns: (1) God as merciful in character (*rachum*, *oiktirmōn*, *eleēmōn*); (2) God's mercy as motivated and purposive (Psa 78:38 — compassion motivates atonement; 1Jo 4:10 — love motivates propitiation); (3) God's mercy as the ground of human identity (1Pe 2:10 — mercy reception constitutes God's people). That God possesses and exercises mercy reveals that mercy is a perfection — a quality of the divine nature that is good without qualification. In the human person, mercy thus reflects the divine image most directly when it is shown toward those who have no claim on it. OBS-111-030, OBS-111-031, OBS-111-052, OBS-111-053. |
| OBS-111-T2-006 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 6:36 | Mercy expresses the divine image through the correspondence structure: "Be merciful as your Father is merciful" (Luk 6:36) names the human-divine alignment of inner disposition. The human person who shows mercy toward those who have no claim on their help is imaging the divine character as the data package description states. Col 3:12 ("compassionate hearts... as God's chosen ones, holy and beloved") grounds the character command in election and divine love — the image is expressed because the person has received mercy and now reflects it. OBS-111-030, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-007 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Luk 6:36 | The evidence presents mercy as genuinely shared between God and the human person — not merely analogous. The same vocabulary (*eleeō/eleēmōn*) applies to both God (Rom 9:15 — God having mercy) and the human person (Mat 5:7 — the merciful). Christ's incarnation (Heb 2:17) takes on humanity specifically to become *eleēmōn* in the priestly sense — the divine mercy acquires human experiential content through solidarity. This suggests that mercy is not merely a creaturely analogue to a divine quality but a genuinely shared characteristic in which the divine and human poles are structurally interconnected. The image-sharing in mercy is constitutive: received mercy produces the capacity to extend mercy. OBS-111-030, OBS-111-033, OBS-111-052, OBS-111-055. *New finding: mercy is a genuinely shared characteristic — not merely analogical — in which the divine and human poles are interconnected through reception and extension.* |
| OBS-111-T2-031 | OBSERVATION | Luk 6:36 | The registry's dimension assignment is "Relational/Social" and the cluster assignment is C17 (relational-grace cluster). The primary dimension is Relational Disposition — mercy is fundamentally a disposition toward another person (or toward God, or from God toward the person). The group landscape (OBS-111-015, OBS-111-016) confirms that the largest clusters (mercy disposition and character groups — Theme B) are Relational Disposition/GOD and Relational Disposition/HUMAN. OBS-111-016, OBS-111-075. |
| OBS-111-T2-032 | OBSERVATION | Luk 6:36 | Multiple evidence streams: (1) The divine-human correspondence structure (Luk 6:36 — "Be merciful as your Father is merciful") defines mercy as a relational orientation. (2) The person-type vocabulary (*eleēmōn*, *oiktirmōn*, *rachum*) designates mercy as a stable inner character quality directed toward others. (3) The Beatitude structure (Mat 5:7) and the new humanity character list (Col 3:12) place mercy in the relational character domain. OBS-111-030, OBS-111-032, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-035 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Luk 6:36 | The womb-root etymology of *racham* (OBS-111-015) suggests mercy originates from the deepest inner centre. The divine-image correspondence (Luk 6:36 — OBS-111-030) suggests human mercy mirrors divine mercy, and divine mercy is not merely relational but ontological — closer to spirit than soul in depth. However, the data does not explicitly confirm spirit-level origin in the verse evidence. The spirit-level classification is defensible but not directly named. OBS-111-015, OBS-111-030. |
| OBS-111-T2-085 | OBSERVATION | Luk 6:36 | Yes — mercy is constitutively relational. All three directions of mercy's operation (God→human, human→God, human→human) are relational. The divine-human correspondence structure (Luk 6:36 — OBS-111-030), the mercy-face encounter (Gen 32:20 — OBS-111-040), and the mercy-community constitution (1Pe 2:10 — OBS-111-053) are all relational at their core. OBS-111-030, OBS-111-040, OBS-111-053. |
| OBS-111-T2-090 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 5:11 | The data reveals that God's disposition toward the human person is mercy-constituted: he is "Father of mercies" (2Cor 1:3 — OBS-111-068), "compassionate and merciful" (Jam 5:11 — OBS-111-031), and his mercy is "from generation to generation" (Luk 1:50). The restraint of wrath (Psa 78:38 — OBS-111-038) shows that mercy is the active principle that holds back the alternative disposition — divine mercy is not passive but actively modulates the relationship. OBS-111-031, OBS-111-038, OBS-111-068. |
| OBS-111-T2-097 | OBSERVATION | Jam 5:11 | The data is rich on receiving mercy from God and on extending mercy to others, but receiving mercy from a human person (human-to-human giving to me as receiver) is less directly addressed. The Bartimaeus cry (Mar 10:48 — OBS-111-054) is directed toward Jesus who is human-divine. The unforgiving servant received mercy from his master/king (Mat 18:27). The evidence addresses the receiving of human mercy only in parabolic and illustrative form — not as a systematic category. Jam 5:11 (God as compassionate and merciful toward Job's suffering) addresses divine mercy received by a human. OBS-111-054, OBS-111-055. |
| OBS-111-T2-111 | OBSERVATION | Jam 5:11 | Yes — substantially. The cry for mercy (Mar 10:48 — OBS-111-054; Luk 18:13 — OBS-111-047) arises from affliction (blindness, guilt). Jam 5:11 (OBS-111-031) explicitly couples Job's suffering and the Lord's mercy as the telos of that suffering. 2Cor 1:3–4 (OBS-111-068) names the Father of mercies as the God of comfort in affliction — mercy is comfort's source in the suffering context. Dan 2:18 (OBS-111-061) — mercy sought under life-threatening pressure. OBS-111-031, OBS-111-047, OBS-111-054, OBS-111-068. |
| OBS-111-T2-112 | VERSE_PATTERN | Jam 5:11 | Two effects of suffering on mercy: (1) Suffering reveals the inner disposition toward mercy (the mercy-cry under suffering: Mar 10:48, Luk 18:13 — genuine need strips away pretence and produces authentic mercy-seeking). (2) Suffering as telos-display context: Jam 5:11 places the Lord's mercy as the revealed purpose behind Job's suffering — mercy is displayed through suffering, not merely despite it. Heb 2:17 (OBS-111-033) — Christ's mercy-capacity was formed through suffering ("made like his brothers in every respect"). *New finding: suffering functions in the mercy-economy as both the context that generates authentic mercy-seeking and the formation-medium through which mercy-capacity is deepened.* OBS-111-031, OBS-111-033, OBS-111-047, OBS-111-054. |
| OBS-111-T2-127 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Jam 5:11 | The two closest adjacent characteristics are compassion (R023, 76 shared verses) and kindness/lovingkindness (R099, shared *chesed* vocabulary). The distinction between mercy and compassion: compassion (*racham*, *oiktirmos*) is the inner felt response to suffering; mercy (*eleos*, *chanan*) includes the acting-upon that felt response toward the guilty or needy. Compassion is the feeling; mercy is feeling-moved-to-act. The distinction is not sharp in all verse evidence — both terms appear in parallel structures (Jam 5:11: *polysplanchnos* and *oiktirmōn*). The precise boundary between mercy and lovingkindness (*chesed*) is a Session D question (SD003 touches this area). OBS-111-021, OBS-111-031. |
| OBS-111-T2-128 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Jam 5:11 | Mercy-compassion overlap: the same verse can use both terms in parallel (Jam 5:11, Psa 78:38 — "being compassionate [rachum], he atoned [kipper]"). In Psa 78:38 the compassion (*rachum*) is the motivating inner state; the atoning act (*kipper*) is the expression. This suggests compassion is the inner state, mercy the expressed act — but this is interpretive, not definitively stated. The precise boundary between mercy and grace (*chen/chanan*) is similarly elusive — grace connotes unmerited favour more broadly; mercy specifically addresses need or guilt. OBS-111-038. |
| OBS-111-T2-129 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Jam 5:11 | Mercy vs. compassion: the distinction appears to be of kind (compassion = inner feeling; mercy = inner feeling expressed in action) rather than merely degree. Mercy vs. grace: the distinction appears to be of direction (grace = favour extended to the lesser; mercy = response specifically to need/guilt — guilt-directedness is mercy's specificity). But these distinctions are not fully confirmed from verse evidence alone — they would require systematic comparison across R023 and R099 verse sets. OBS-111-031, OBS-111-038. |
| OBS-111-T2-139 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Jam 5:11 | Yes — *eleēmōn* (G1655 — the merciful one, Mat 5:7; Heb 2:17) and *oiktirmōn* (G3629 — the compassionate one, Luk 6:36; Jam 5:11). Hebrew person-type: *rachum* (compassionate — used of God as person-type adjective, e.g. Exo 34:6 — "a God merciful and gracious"). OBS-111-013. |

### G1655 eleēmōn — merciful

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G1655` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R111 mercy · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-C` (Mercy)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 20
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=3164` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`
6. Mounce short-def: merciful, including feelings of pity, with a focus of showing compassion to those in serious need

**Verses:** 2 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1631 | 3164-001 | Term names mercifulness as an inner character quality — the blessed disposition of those who show mercy and the defining character of Christ as high priest |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (2)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-111-T2-135 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7, Mat 18:33, Rom 9:15 | For *eleos* (G1656, the dominant Greek noun, 26 active): functions as noun — mercy as a state or quality. For *eleeō* (G1653, 28 active): verb — mercy as act. For *eleēmōn* (G1655, 2 active): adjective — mercy as character quality of a person. For *chanan* (H2603A, 72 active): verb (Qal and Piel) — to be gracious, to show grace; also forms person-type nouns. The full grammatical range (noun, verb, adjective, person-type noun) confirms mercy operates as condition (noun), action (verb), character (adjective), and identity (person-type). OBS-111-008, OBS-111-013. |
| OBS-111-T2-139 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7, Heb 2:17, Jam 5:11 | Yes — *eleēmōn* (G1655 — the merciful one, Mat 5:7; Heb 2:17) and *oiktirmōn* (G3629 — the compassionate one, Luk 6:36; Jam 5:11). Hebrew person-type: *rachum* (compassionate — used of God as person-type adjective, e.g. Exo 34:6 — "a God merciful and gracious"). OBS-111-013. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (21 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-111-T2-003 | OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7 | The verse evidence indicates that human mercy equips the person for relational participation in the mercy-logic that structures the divine-human relationship: Mat 5:7 (the merciful receive mercy — mercy enables participation in the mercy-reality); Mat 18:33 (received mercy is meant to flow outward — mercy equips the person for generous relational extension); Col 3:12 (mercy as first inner garment of the new humanity — mercy is constitutive of the character set that makes community life possible). What the evidence does not address is whether mercy is part of the original pre-fall design or whether it is specifically a post-fall response. The atonement vocabulary presupposes the fallen condition — it has no function where there is no guilt. This is a gap: OBS-111-075 and the atonement cluster evidence do not speak to mercy's pre-fall status. OBS-111-032, OBS-111-055, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-005 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Mat 5:7 | The propitiation vocabulary points forward: Rom 3:25 (*hilastērios* received by faith) and the typological fulfillment of the mercy seat in Christ (OBS-111-066, OBS-111-067) establish mercy's eschatological trajectory through Christ. The Beatitude in Mat 5:7 ("they shall receive mercy") carries a future-tense promise that points toward the completion of mercy's logic. Heb 8:12's new covenant citation ("I will remember their sins no more") is eschatological in character — it names the final state of divine-human relationship under mercy. The evidence does not name a specific future fullness of mercy beyond these. OBS-111-032, OBS-111-035, OBS-111-066. |
| OBS-111-T2-007 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 2:17, Mat 5:7 | The evidence presents mercy as genuinely shared between God and the human person — not merely analogous. The same vocabulary (*eleeō/eleēmōn*) applies to both God (Rom 9:15 — God having mercy) and the human person (Mat 5:7 — the merciful). Christ's incarnation (Heb 2:17) takes on humanity specifically to become *eleēmōn* in the priestly sense — the divine mercy acquires human experiential content through solidarity. This suggests that mercy is not merely a creaturely analogue to a divine quality but a genuinely shared characteristic in which the divine and human poles are structurally interconnected. The image-sharing in mercy is constitutive: received mercy produces the capacity to extend mercy. OBS-111-030, OBS-111-033, OBS-111-052, OBS-111-055. *New finding: mercy is a genuinely shared characteristic — not merely analogical — in which the divine and human poles are interconnected through reception and extension.* |
| OBS-111-T2-008 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Mat 5:7 | Mercilessness (*aneleēmōn*, *anileōs*) appears in vice catalogues marking constitutional inner collapse (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034; Jam 2:13 — OBS-111-070). The absence of mercy is not merely virtue-failure but image-defacement — the inner person has lost the capacity that most visibly reflects the divine character. Conversely, the Beatitude (Mat 5:7) and the new humanity character list (Col 3:12) present mercy as the marker of those in whom the image is being restored. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-032, OBS-111-069, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-014 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7 | Mercy appears as both disposition and act — and the data's structure reflects this distinction. The disposition vocabulary (*eleēmōn* — merciful as person-type; *oiktirmōn* — compassionate as character quality; *rachum* — compassionate as God's inner character) names mercy as a stable inner orientation. The act vocabulary (*eleeō* — to have mercy; *kipper* — to atone; *hilaskomai* — to propitiate) names mercy as expressed in specific directed events. The distinction matters: the Beatitude (Mat 5:7) designates the disposition (*eleēmōn*); the parable (Mat 18:33) names the expected act (*eleeō*). OBS-111-008, OBS-111-013, OBS-111-032, OBS-111-055. |
| OBS-111-T2-032 | OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7 | Multiple evidence streams: (1) The divine-human correspondence structure (Luk 6:36 — "Be merciful as your Father is merciful") defines mercy as a relational orientation. (2) The person-type vocabulary (*eleēmōn*, *oiktirmōn*, *rachum*) designates mercy as a stable inner character quality directed toward others. (3) The Beatitude structure (Mat 5:7) and the new humanity character list (Col 3:12) place mercy in the relational character domain. OBS-111-030, OBS-111-032, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-082 | OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7 | Yes — Dan 4:27 (break off sins by practicing righteousness, show mercy to the oppressed — OBS-111-062) makes mercy a conscientious act: it requires moral awareness (recognising the oppressed), volition (choosing to show mercy), and concrete action (the practice of righteousness/mercy). The Beatitude (Mat 5:7 — OBS-111-032) designates the merciful as those whose integrated inner character is conscientiousness-directed — they show mercy because they are merciful. OBS-111-062, OBS-111-032. |
| OBS-111-T2-086 | OBSERVATION | Heb 2:17 | Mercy enables and constitutes relational capacity in several distinct ways: (1) Mercy enables face-to-face access (Gen 32:20 — the appeasement-mercy enables Jacob to see Esau's face). (2) Mercy constitutes community (1Pe 2:10 — mercy reception constitutes "God's people"). (3) Heb 2:17 (OBS-111-033) — Christ's mercy-capacity was enabled by incarnational solidarity; mercy-capacity requires experiential solidarity. *New finding: mercy is the foundational enabler of relational capacity — it creates the conditions under which genuine face-to-face encounter, community membership, and compassionate extension are possible.* OBS-111-033, OBS-111-040, OBS-111-053. |
| OBS-111-T2-087 | OBSERVATION | Heb 2:17 | The relational pattern reveals mercy as the characteristic that makes relationship possible after failure — not the normal maintenance of good relationships but the restoration and constitution of relationships that would otherwise be broken or non-existent. Mercy is the relational repair and foundation mechanism. OBS-111-033, OBS-111-040, OBS-111-053, OBS-111-055. |
| OBS-111-T2-095 | OBSERVATION | Heb 2:17 | The enabling inner conditions for human mercy-giving: (1) Prior reception of divine mercy (Mat 18:33 — the servant received mercy first; failure to extend was inexcusable because of this). (2) Solidarity with the condition of the needy — Heb 2:17 establishes the principle that mercy-extension requires experiential solidarity (OBS-111-033); Christ's incarnational solidarity with humanity is the mechanism for his priestly mercy. (3) The inner character quality of mercifulness (*eleēmōn*, *oiktirmōn*) — which Col 3:12 frames as a garment to be deliberately appropriated. OBS-111-033, OBS-111-055, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-096 | OBSERVATION | Heb 2:17 | The data consistently requires prior reception before genuine extension. Mat 18:33 makes this explicit — the failure to extend mercy is inexcusable specifically because of what was received. The Heb 2:17 principle extends this: the capacity to show mercy requires solidarity-knowledge of the condition mercy addresses. The person who can genuinely give mercy is one who knows themselves to be under mercy (Luk 18:13 — self-knowledge as sinner), has received mercy (Mat 18:28 — the sequence in the parable), and has had inner orientation reshaped (the expectation of Mat 18:33). OBS-111-033, OBS-111-047, OBS-111-055. |
| OBS-111-T2-109 | OBSERVATION | Heb 2:17 | The evidence names several distinct mechanisms: (1) Blood-mediated covering (Lev 17:11 — OBS-111-037): the bodily sacrifice mechanism through which guilt is covered and the soul addressed. (2) Divine speech-act (Heb 8:12 — OBS-111-035): covenantal declaration ("I will be merciful... I will remember no more") as the speech-mechanism of mercy. (3) Answered prayer (Gen 25:21, 2Ch 33:13 — OBS-111-059, OBS-111-060): the petition-response dynamic as mechanism. (4) Incarnational solidarity (Heb 2:17 — OBS-111-033): the mechanism of mercy-formation through experiential participation. OBS-111-033, OBS-111-035, OBS-111-037, OBS-111-059. |
| OBS-111-T2-110 | OBSERVATION | Heb 2:17 | Yes — mechanism varies by context: OT ritual context uses blood-sacrifice; new covenant context uses faith-reception of the living mercy seat (Rom 3:25 — OBS-111-066); prayer context uses petition-response; Christ's priestly context uses incarnational solidarity (Heb 2:17). The mechanisms are contextually differentiated but share a common logic: the mercy mechanism is always specific, embodied, and costs something (blood, incarnation, divine speech, answered petition). OBS-111-033, OBS-111-037, OBS-111-060, OBS-111-066. |
| OBS-111-T2-112 | VERSE_PATTERN | Heb 2:17 | Two effects of suffering on mercy: (1) Suffering reveals the inner disposition toward mercy (the mercy-cry under suffering: Mar 10:48, Luk 18:13 — genuine need strips away pretence and produces authentic mercy-seeking). (2) Suffering as telos-display context: Jam 5:11 places the Lord's mercy as the revealed purpose behind Job's suffering — mercy is displayed through suffering, not merely despite it. Heb 2:17 (OBS-111-033) — Christ's mercy-capacity was formed through suffering ("made like his brothers in every respect"). *New finding: suffering functions in the mercy-economy as both the context that generates authentic mercy-seeking and the formation-medium through which mercy-capacity is deepened.* OBS-111-031, OBS-111-033, OBS-111-047, OBS-111-054. |
| OBS-111-T2-113 | OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7 | Yes — Col 3:12 (OBS-111-069) places mercy ("compassionate hearts") as the first item in a deliberate character formation list — the new humanity's inner character complex is to be cultivated progressively. The "put on" (*endysasthe*) metaphor names it as ongoing formation, not one-time reception. The Beatitude (Mat 5:7 — OBS-111-032) designates the merciful as those whose character has been formed toward mercy. The daily prayer practice (Dan 6:11 — OBS-111-063) shows mercy-seeking as sanctifying practice, not isolated event. OBS-111-032, OBS-111-063, OBS-111-069. |
| OBS-111-T2-115 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Mat 5:7 | Yes — three eschatological pointers: (1) Heb 8:12 (new covenant mercy — "I will remember their sins no more") is the eschatological state of the divine-human relationship as mercy-constituted. (2) Mat 5:7 ("they shall receive mercy") points forward — the promise is future-tense. (3) The typological fulfillment structure (mercy seat → Christ as living mercy seat, OBS-111-066) points to the completed mercy-reality in Christ that the OT types anticipated. OBS-111-032, OBS-111-035, OBS-111-066. |
| OBS-111-T2-135 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7 | For *eleos* (G1656, the dominant Greek noun, 26 active): functions as noun — mercy as a state or quality. For *eleeō* (G1653, 28 active): verb — mercy as act. For *eleēmōn* (G1655, 2 active): adjective — mercy as character quality of a person. For *chanan* (H2603A, 72 active): verb (Qal and Piel) — to be gracious, to show grace; also forms person-type nouns. The full grammatical range (noun, verb, adjective, person-type noun) confirms mercy operates as condition (noun), action (verb), character (adjective), and identity (person-type). OBS-111-008, OBS-111-013. |
| OBS-111-T2-136 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7 | *Eleos* (mercy noun) ranges across: divine compassion (Rom 9:15), human mercy extension (Mat 5:7), the mercy-greeting formula (epistolary — "grace, mercy, peace"), covenantal mercy memory (Luk 1:54), and the telos of suffering (Jam 5:11). *Chanan* (to be gracious) ranges across: divine grace-extension, prayer-petition (in supplication compounds), human gracious speech, and interpersonal favour-seeking (Gen 32:20). The range is wide: from divine sovereign mercy to human interpersonal mercy-seeking, from atoning act to character description. OBS-111-008, OBS-111-010. |
| OBS-111-T2-137 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7 | Yes — the vocabulary makes clear lexical distinctions: (1) Disposition vs act: *eleēmōn/oiktirmōn/rachum* (character adjectives — disposition) vs. *eleeō/chanan/kipper* (verbs — act). (2) Received vs given: *techinah/atar* (supplication vocabulary — seeking mercy to be received) vs. *eleeō/chanan/oiktirmos* (mercy extended). (3) Condition vs quality: *lo ruchamah* (condition of mercy-withdrawal — negative condition) vs. *eleēmōn* (quality of the merciful person). OBS-111-008, OBS-111-013, OBS-111-014. |
| OBS-111-T2-139 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7, Heb 2:17 | Yes — *eleēmōn* (G1655 — the merciful one, Mat 5:7; Heb 2:17) and *oiktirmōn* (G3629 — the compassionate one, Luk 6:36; Jam 5:11). Hebrew person-type: *rachum* (compassionate — used of God as person-type adjective, e.g. Exo 34:6 — "a God merciful and gracious"). OBS-111-013. |
| OBS-111-T2-150 | OBSERVATION | Heb 2:17 | Key divergence: moral psychology and philosophy typically treat mercy as arising from within the human agent (the merciful person decides to extend mercy as a moral act). The biblical data consistently grounds mercy in prior reception — the one who extends mercy does so because they have received it (Heb 2:17 — incarnational solidarity; Mat 18:33 — received mercy creates extension obligation). This is a structural divergence: the human-science model locates mercy's source in the autonomous moral agent; the biblical model locates it in the received mercy that reshapes the agent. The divergence reveals mercy's constitutively received character in the biblical frame — it is not primarily a virtue the person develops independently but a grace that is passed on. OBS-111-033, OBS-111-055. |

**SD pointers — direct (1)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| SP-111-018 | HIGH |  | [v1.8 obslog SD pointer] Does Heb 2:17 establish that the capacity to extend mercy requires prior experiential solidarity with the recipient's condition — and is this a universal principle or specific to Christ's mediatorial role?
