### G0448 anileōs — merciless

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G0448` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R111 mercy · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-C` (Mercy)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 1
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=993` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`
6. Mounce short-def: merciless

**Verses:** 1 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1626 | 993-001 | Term names mercilessness as the inner orientation that forfeits mercy — and mercy as the disposition that triumphs over judgment |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (2)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-111-T2-017 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31, Jam 2:13, Hos 1:6 | Two lexically explicit structural opposites: (1) *aneleēmōn* (G0415 — merciless/ruthless) in the moral-collapse catalogue (Rom 1:31, OBS-111-034); (2) *anileōs* (G0448 — without mercy) in the mercy-judgment contrast (Jam 2:13, OBS-111-070); (3) H3819 *lo ruchamah* (No Mercy — the prophetic name embodying mercy's withdrawal, Hos 1:6, OBS-111-058). The structural opposite operates at three levels: the inner character failure (mercilessness as constitution), the relational disposition (without mercy toward another), and the divine withdrawal (No Mercy as prophetic verdict). OBS-111-034, OBS-111-058, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-138 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31, Jam 2:13, Hos 1:6 | Yes — three terms: *aneleēmōn* (G0415 — merciless, Rom 1:31), *anileōs* (G0448 — without mercy, Jam 2:13), H3819 *lo ruchamah* (No Mercy — prophetic name, Hos 1:6). The structural opposite is lexically explicit at the character, relational, and prophetic-identity levels. OBS-111-014, OBS-111-034, OBS-111-058, OBS-111-070. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (14 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-111-T2-008 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 2:13 | Mercilessness (*aneleēmōn*, *anileōs*) appears in vice catalogues marking constitutional inner collapse (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034; Jam 2:13 — OBS-111-070). The absence of mercy is not merely virtue-failure but image-defacement — the inner person has lost the capacity that most visibly reflects the divine character. Conversely, the Beatitude (Mat 5:7) and the new humanity character list (Col 3:12) present mercy as the marker of those in whom the image is being restored. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-032, OBS-111-069, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-017 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Jam 2:13 | Two lexically explicit structural opposites: (1) *aneleēmōn* (G0415 — merciless/ruthless) in the moral-collapse catalogue (Rom 1:31, OBS-111-034); (2) *anileōs* (G0448 — without mercy) in the mercy-judgment contrast (Jam 2:13, OBS-111-070); (3) H3819 *lo ruchamah* (No Mercy — the prophetic name embodying mercy's withdrawal, Hos 1:6, OBS-111-058). The structural opposite operates at three levels: the inner character failure (mercilessness as constitution), the relational disposition (without mercy toward another), and the divine withdrawal (No Mercy as prophetic verdict). OBS-111-034, OBS-111-058, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-018 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Jam 2:13 | Mercy explicitly resists and triumphs over judgment (Jam 2:13 — "mercy triumphs over judgment," OBS-111-070). It resists the logic of strict retributive accountability — the ransom/atonement vocabulary is precisely the mechanism that addresses what strict justice would require. Mercy also resists hardness of heart (the failure in Mat 18:33 is not cruelty but failure to let received mercy reshape the inner disposition, OBS-111-055). OBS-111-070, OBS-111-055. |
| OBS-111-T2-019 | OBSERVATION | Jam 2:13 | The evidence draws some boundaries: mercy is not the same as justice (they are in structural tension, Jam 2:13; Mat 23:23 — SD003). Mercy is not the same as grace (though they share vocabulary — *chanan* generates both mercy-extension and grace-language). Mercy is not the same as forgiveness (Lev 4:20 couples atonement and forgiveness — they are sequential, not synonymous; forgiveness/R64 shares this anchor). The precise boundaries with compassion (R023 — 76 shared verses, highest co-occurrence) and lovingkindness/chesed are not fully drawn in the current data. SD003 and existing SD pointers acknowledge these boundary questions belong to Session D. OBS-111-021, OBS-111-036. |
| OBS-111-T2-029 | OBSERVATION | Jam 2:13 | The data addresses blocking of mercy's extension rather than its reception more directly. Mercilessness blocks the receipt of mercy (Jam 2:13 — "judgment without mercy to one who has shown no mercy," OBS-111-070). Taking a bribe (*kopher* as bribe, 1Sa 12:3) corrupts the mercy-mechanism by redirecting it toward injustice (OBS-111-044). What blocks reception of mercy in a willing God remains less clearly addressed — the data is richer on the posture of the seeking person than on what prevents them from receiving. The gap is whether pride, self-sufficiency, or unrepentance is named as blocking reception. OBS-111-044, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-076 | OBSERVATION | Jam 2:13 | Yes — mercy engages moral evaluation in a complex way. Jam 2:13 (mercy triumphs over judgment — OBS-111-070) places mercy in explicit relation to the moral-evaluative process: judgment is the conclusion of moral evaluation; mercy overrides it. Mat 23:23 (justice, mercy, faithfulness as weightier matters of the law — SD003) places mercy as a component of the moral law itself. Dan 4:27 (practicing righteousness and showing mercy as parallel — OBS-111-062) makes mercy a moral obligation assessed against a standard. *New finding: mercy is not opposed to moral evaluation but is itself a moral evaluation — specifically, the evaluation that sets the claims of the other's need above the calculus of strict retributive accountability.* OBS-111-070, OBS-111-062. |
| OBS-111-T2-077 | OBSERVATION | Jam 2:13 | Mercy enables moral evaluation by expanding what is evaluated: justice considers the act; mercy considers the person behind the act. The *kopher*-bribe perversion (1Sa 12:3 — OBS-111-044) shows that corrupt mercy (bribery) impairs moral evaluation — it literally blinds the evaluator. Genuine mercy sharpens rather than bypasses moral evaluation — Jam 2:13 does not deny judgment but asserts mercy's triumph over it. OBS-111-044, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-078 | OBSERVATION | Jam 2:13 | Mercy does not bypass moral evaluation — it exceeds it. The pattern reveals mercy as a higher-order moral evaluation that operates after primary moral judgment has assessed wrongdoing. Mercy is not moral blindness but moral overflow — it sees the wrongdoing clearly (hence the need for atonement, not the denial of it) and then exceeds strict accountability. OBS-111-070, OBS-111-036. |
| OBS-111-T2-094 | OBSERVATION | Jam 2:13 | Yes — human-to-human mercy extension is evidenced in: Mat 18:33 (mercy-obligation on the one who received mercy, OBS-111-055); Dan 4:27 (showing mercy to the oppressed, OBS-111-062); Jam 2:13 (one who has shown mercy, OBS-111-070); Mat 5:7 (the merciful — those whose inner character extends mercy, OBS-111-032). The extension is toward those in a weaker or guilty position — the oppressed (Dan 4:27), the debtor (Mat 18:33). OBS-111-032, OBS-111-055, OBS-111-062. |
| OBS-111-T2-138 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Jam 2:13 | Yes — three terms: *aneleēmōn* (G0415 — merciless, Rom 1:31), *anileōs* (G0448 — without mercy, Jam 2:13), H3819 *lo ruchamah* (No Mercy — prophetic name, Hos 1:6). The structural opposite is lexically explicit at the character, relational, and prophetic-identity levels. OBS-111-014, OBS-111-034, OBS-111-058, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-144 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | Jam 2:13 | Three key argument structures: (1) Jam 2:13 — bipartite argument: premise (judgment without mercy to those who show no mercy) → conclusion (mercy triumphs over judgment). Internal logic: mercy-extension determines mercy-reception; therefore mercy > judgment. (2) 1Jo 4:10 — chain argument: divine love (ground) → sending of Son (act) → propitiation (mechanism) → sin covered (result). Love is the motive; mercy/propitiation is the instrument. (3) Mat 18:23–34 — analogical argument: the king's cancellation of debt proportional to the servant's refusal to cancel a smaller debt; the conclusion ("should you not have had mercy?") is argued by proportion. OBS-111-049, OBS-111-055, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-145 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | Jam 2:13 | Primary contextual settings: (1) Liturgical/cultic — Lev 4:20, 17:11, 23:27 (priestly atonement ritual); Heb 9:5 (tabernacle description). (2) Covenantal — Heb 8:12 (new covenant citation); Psa 78:38 (covenantal mercy toward Israel). (3) Judicial — Jam 2:13 (mercy vs. judgment); Mat 18:23ff (parabolic legal frame). (4) Petition/prayer — Luk 18:13 (prayer parable); Dan 2:18 (crisis petition). (5) Christological — Heb 2:17 (incarnation); Rom 3:25 (propitiation). The liturgical and covenantal settings carry the heaviest weight of mercy-content. OBS-111-037, OBS-111-038. |
| OBS-111-T2-148 | OBSERVATION | Jam 2:13 | Three frameworks have relevance: (1) Moral psychology — mercy as moral evaluation that exceeds strict accountability (Q&A-089; Q&A-091) maps to debates about retributive vs restorative justice and the moral psychology of forgiveness. (2) Attachment theory — the womb-etymology of *racham* and the mother-child imagery underlying compassion vocabulary aligns with attachment theory's account of foundational relational security and its disruption. (3) Shame theory — the shame-response to received mercy (Eze 16:63 — OBS-111-039; Q&A-025) connects to contemporary work on healthy vs. toxic shame, and the role of relational mercy in transforming shame from paralysis to humility. The most directly relevant is moral psychology (mercy as a moral virtue that exceeds justice). OBS-111-039, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-149 | OBSERVATION | Jam 2:13 | Moral psychology illuminates Jam 2:13 (mercy triumphing over judgment as moral virtue) and the unforgiving servant parable — the inability to extend received mercy maps to the moral-psychological phenomenon of unprocessed shame that prevents generous treatment of others. Attachment theory illuminates the womb-rooted compassion vocabulary — mercy as the inner relational bond that generates security and care. Shame theory illuminates the Eze 16:63 sequence — received mercy transforming shame from condemnation into humility. These are framework-level identifications; fuller engagement is beyond the current data. OBS-111-039, OBS-111-055, OBS-111-070. |

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1567 | 1588-001 | Term names friendship with the world as a disordered inner orientation constituting enmity with God — the inward allegiance that cannot serve two masters |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (1)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-103-T2-109 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Lam 3:22, Song 2:16, Isa 49:15, 1Cor 13:4 | Primary Hebrew terms and root meanings: **chesed** (H2617A) — root implies binding loyalty, covenant-fidelity; possibly related to the idea of steadfastness or bending toward; the most theologically laden term in the OT love vocabulary. **ahavah** (H0160) — root ahav (to breathe after, to desire) — implying love as a form of directed longing or desire. **racham** (H7355) / **rachamim** (H7356B) — root rechem (womb) — love as womb-like envelopment and tender compassion; the most somatically grounded root. **dod** (H1730G/H) — root unclear; used for lover/beloved in the Song — the erotic-relational vocabulary. Primary Greek terms: **agapē** (G0026) / **agapaō** (G0025) — of uncertain pre-classical etymology; LXX/NT coinage whose meaning is constituted by Scripture rather than importing a prior secular meaning (OBS-103-016). **phileō** (G5368) / **philia** (G5373) / **filos** (G5384) — root from philos (friend); classical Greek for natural affectionate love. Root meanings together reveal: love spans willed-covenant (chesed), longing-desire (ahavah), somatic-compassion (racham), erotic-beloved (dod), self-giving-willed (agapē), and natural-affection (phileō). |

**Findings — via anchor verse (6 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-103-T2-013 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | All three. Directional: love always has a direction — toward God, toward neighbour, toward the beloved, or toward disordered objects (money, pleasure, world). The moral question of love is always the question of its direction (OBS-103-017). Relational: love cannot exist in isolation — it is constitutively relational, requiring at minimum a lover and a beloved. The relational structure is not incidental but essential (OBS-103-035). Constitutional: love operates at the deepest level of the inner person — the great commandment (Mat 22:37) requires 'all your heart and soul and mind,' indicating that love engages the entire constitutional depth of the inner being (OBS-103-139). The enquiry is oriented from the outset toward a characteristic that is simultaneously moral (choice of direction), relational (requires another), and constitutional (engages the whole person). |
| OBS-103-T2-017 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | The structural opposite is hatred (sane — H8130, 139 active verses, OWNER term, OBS-103-030). Sane is embedded in R103 as an OWNER term — the programme treats hate as constitutive of the love vocabulary's boundary, not external to it. Lev 19:17 ('you shall not hate your brother in your heart') places hate as the inner alternative to love of neighbour (OBS-103-114). Pro 10:12 ('hatred stirs up strife, love covers all offences,' OBS-103-097) places them as causal-opposite forces with opposed social effects. The secondary structural opposite at the ultimate level is enmity with God: friendship with the world is enmity (echthra) with God (Jam 4:4, OBS-103-058). But hatred is the primary inner-being opposite — it is the same inner mechanism as love (directed orientation) running in the opposed direction. |
| OBS-103-T2-018 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | 1Cor 13:4-7 provides the programme's most explicit catalogue of what love excludes: envy, boasting, arrogance, rudeness, self-seeking, irritability, resentfulness, rejoicing in wrongdoing (OBS-103-127). The list names love's resistances across moral, relational, and emotional registers. At the structural level, love resists division of ultimate allegiance (Jam 4:4 — cannot love God and world simultaneously, OBS-103-058), resists self-assertion as the primary orientation (love-of-self as the first item in the last-days catalogue, OBS-103-137), and resists the withholding of self (Joh 15:13 — love's maximum is the giving of the soul/life, OBS-103-125). Love also explicitly resists concealment of inner truth from the beloved: Lev 19:17 contrasts hate-as-silence with love-as-frank-correction (OBS-103-114). |
| OBS-103-T2-059 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | Yes — through the judgment of the love-object's worth and the assessment of love's hierarchy. Mat 10:37 (OBS-103-151) requires a judgment: 'whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me' — love operates through a comparative judgment of worth among competing objects. The ranking of loves (OBS-103-022 — love as four-mode structure) requires reason to navigate. Jam 4:4 (OBS-103-058): the reasoning that friendship with the world constitutes enmity with God is a logical judgment. Pro 10:12 (OBS-103-097): 'love covers all offences' — the judgment that covering is better than exposing is a reasoned moral assessment. Love requires and shapes the judgment-faculty at the level of determining what and whom to love, in what order, and how. |
| OBS-103-T2-128 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | Yes — love is always directed. The grammar confirms this: agapaō always takes a direct object; the philo-compound system is entirely object-defining (love-of-X where X determines the compound). No instance of undirected love appears in the data — love is constitutively directional (OBS-103-017, OBS-103-009). The object determines love's moral character entirely: the inner mechanism is identical whether the object is God (filotheos — morally ordered) or pleasure (filēdonos — morally disordered) or money (filarguros). This is the data's most consistently evidenced structural feature: the same inner act of attachment produces radically different moral outcomes based solely on the object. The moral life in the biblical data is substantially a question of love's direction — not whether one loves but what one loves. This confirms L-004's anticipated finding (below). |
| OBS-103-T2-131 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | Yes — the philo-compound disordered forms constitute a taxonomy (OBS-103-036, OBS-103-017): filarguria/filarguros (love of money — 3 groups), filēdonos (love of pleasure), filoneikia/filoneikos (love of dispute), filoprōteuō (love of being first), filia tou kosmou (friendship with world — Jam 4:4), agapaō-darkness/world (Joh 3:19, 2Ti 4:10), agavah (lust — H5691), filautos (love of self — 2Ti 3:2). The structural logic organising the taxonomy: all disordered loves are the same inner mechanism (directed attachment) applied to one of three wrong object-classes: (a) **Material objects** — money, pleasure; (b) **Social-positional objects** — primacy, dispute-victory; (c) **The self** — self-love, friendship with the world as self-assertion. The taxonomy is not arbitrary — it maps the full range of objects that compete with God and the neighbour as the proper objects of love. The disordered loves are a systematic counter-ordering of the inner person's relational architecture. |

### H7362 ra.cha.ma.ni — compassionate

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H7362` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R023 compassion · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-B` (Compassion)
4. Term status: extracted_thin · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 1
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=1616` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`

**Verses:** 1 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 349 | 1616-001 | Term names the inner quality of compassion as a defining human characteristic — its violation by circumstances of extreme suffering marking the depth of the catastrophe |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (1)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-023-T2-138 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — the data contains multiple person-type terms: **H2623 chasid** (XREF, 33 occurrences): "devout/pious/loyal person" — the one whose inner life is constituted by chesed. The chasid is the person-type whose character is defined by covenantal compassion-loyalty. This is the most developed person-type term in the vocabulary. **H7349 rachum** (XREF, 13 occurrences): as a divine epithet, this names God as the paradigmatic compassionate person-type. When applied to humans, it names the one who is constitutively compassionate. **H7362 ra.cha.ma.ni** (OWNER, 1 occurrence, Lam 4:10): adjectival — "compassionate (women)." Names the person-type by character quality. Used at the moment of its catastrophic violation — which reveals that the term was understood as a stable character-type identifier. **G4835 sumpathēs** (OWNER, 1 occurrence, 1 Pet 3:8): "sympathetic" as a character quality of the community member. The person who is sumpathēs is constitutively open to the inner experience of others. **G3356 metriopatheō context (Heb 5:2):** The high priest who "deals gently" is implicitly named as a person-type: the one who exercises calibrated, wise compassion arising from shared vulnerability. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (0 unique)**

(none)

**SD pointers — direct (1)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-023-SD002 | MEDIUM |  | H7358 re.chem (womb, 26 occ) is primarily a fertility and creation-order term. Its 26 occurrences include divine sovereignty over conception and birth (Gen 20:18; 29:31; 30:22) — language closely associated with the Love (Reg 103) and potentially Calling (Reg 19) registries. The question: does divine sovereignty over the womb constitute a cross-registry connection between Compassion (the response to suffering), Love (the relational disposition), and Calling (divine purpose from before birth)? Isa 49:1 ("The Lord called me from the womb") and Jer 1:5 ("before you were born I consecrated you") belong to other registries but are semantically adjacent. --- #### Term 3: H7362 ra.cha.ma.ni — compassionate (occ=1, extracted_thin) *Sense structure: Adjective from the RACHAM root. Single occurrence: Lam 4:10 — the compassionate (ra.cha.ma.ni) women who boiled their children. The adjective names the settled character quality of compassion. The verse's force depends entirely on the characterisation: these are compassionate women — yet this is what they did. The term names the inner quality at the moment of its most catastrophic inversion. Semantic observation: This is the only adjectival form in the OWNER vocabulary for this registry. It establishes that compassion is a character quality (the adjective names what kind of person one is, not what one does in a moment). The tragedy of Lam 4:10 is that character qualities are not indestructible — extreme circumstance can force violations of the deepest human dispositions. Session C check:* Section 3 annotation confirmed accurate and complete. |

### G0017 agathopoios — doing good

1. Strong's: `G0017` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R065 generosity · Legacy C-cluster: C08

| OBS-067-T2-118 | ROOT_FINDING | Mic 6:8, Gal 5:22 | Yes — vocabulary sharing is extensive. From the XREF structure: R103 (love) shares H2896A and G0019; R042 (delight) shares H2896A; R099 (kindness) shares G5544; R065 (generosity) shares G0019 (via agathos root family — G0015, G0016, G0017, G0018 all in R65 XREF). The 42 XREF terms in R67's inventory flow to multiple registries, confirming that the tov and agatho/chresto root families are shared across a wide range of programme registries. (OBS-067-OBS-018, OBS-067-OBS-019, SBF-067-Q019, SBF-067-Q124) |
| OBS-067-T2-119 | ROOT_FINDING | Gal 5:22 | Yes — root-level architecture is confirmed for both root families: TOV root family: H2896A (tov adjective, R67 OWNER) / H2895 (tov verbal — be pleasing, R103 XREF) / H2896B (tov adjective, various) / H2896C (to.vah — welfare, various) / H2898 (tuv — goodness nominal, R103). The TOV root generates terms distributed across R67, R103, and adjacent registries. AGATHO root family: G0019 (agathōsunē, R67 OWNER) / G0018 (agathos, R65 OWNER) / G0015 (agathopoieo, R65 XREF) / G0016 (agathopoiia, R65 XREF) / G0017 (agathopoios, R65 XREF) / G0014 (agathoergeō, R65 XREF) — all agatho- compounds distributed across R67 and R65. CHRESTO root family: G5544 (chrēstotēs, R67 OWNER) / G5543 (chrēstos, adjacent) / G5541 (chrēsteuomai, adjacent) / G5542 (chrēstologia, distortion form). (OBS-067-OBS-018, SBF-067-Q019, SP-067-001, SP-067-011) |
| OBS-067-T2-134 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | N/A | G0017 (agathopoios — doing good, "the one who does good") is in the XREF inventory — this is a person-type term for the one who does good (1 Pet 2:14 / 2:15 context). However, it is assigned to R65 (generosity) rather than R67. No direct OWNER-term person-type exists in R67 for "the good person" as a named inner-being category. H2896A in its adjectival form functions descriptively ("a good person") but is not a fixed person-type label. G0017 is the closest — but it is an action-person type (one who does good) rather than a character-person type (one who is good). The distinction is analytically significant — the vocabulary distinguishes the person who does good from the person who is good. Partial gap: action-person type present (in R65 XREF); character-person type absent as a fixed lexical label. (OBS-067-OBS-007, OBS-067-OBS-090, SBF-067-Q090) |

### G0415 aneleēmōn — merciless

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G0415` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R111 mercy · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-C` (Mercy)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 7
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=3165` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`
6. Mounce short-def: ruthless, merciless

**Verses:** 1 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1625 | 3165-001 | Term names mercilessness as an inner character failure — one of the marks of those whose inner moral fabric has collapsed |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (2)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-111-T2-017 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31, Jam 2:13, Hos 1:6 | Two lexically explicit structural opposites: (1) *aneleēmōn* (G0415 — merciless/ruthless) in the moral-collapse catalogue (Rom 1:31, OBS-111-034); (2) *anileōs* (G0448 — without mercy) in the mercy-judgment contrast (Jam 2:13, OBS-111-070); (3) H3819 *lo ruchamah* (No Mercy — the prophetic name embodying mercy's withdrawal, Hos 1:6, OBS-111-058). The structural opposite operates at three levels: the inner character failure (mercilessness as constitution), the relational disposition (without mercy toward another), and the divine withdrawal (No Mercy as prophetic verdict). OBS-111-034, OBS-111-058, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-138 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31, Jam 2:13, Hos 1:6 | Yes — three terms: *aneleēmōn* (G0415 — merciless, Rom 1:31), *anileōs* (G0448 — without mercy, Jam 2:13), H3819 *lo ruchamah* (No Mercy — prophetic name, Hos 1:6). The structural opposite is lexically explicit at the character, relational, and prophetic-identity levels. OBS-111-014, OBS-111-034, OBS-111-058, OBS-111-070. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (20 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-111-T2-008 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:31 | Mercilessness (*aneleēmōn*, *anileōs*) appears in vice catalogues marking constitutional inner collapse (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034; Jam 2:13 — OBS-111-070). The absence of mercy is not merely virtue-failure but image-defacement — the inner person has lost the capacity that most visibly reflects the divine character. Conversely, the Beatitude (Mat 5:7) and the new humanity character list (Col 3:12) present mercy as the marker of those in whom the image is being restored. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-032, OBS-111-069, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-017 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Two lexically explicit structural opposites: (1) *aneleēmōn* (G0415 — merciless/ruthless) in the moral-collapse catalogue (Rom 1:31, OBS-111-034); (2) *anileōs* (G0448 — without mercy) in the mercy-judgment contrast (Jam 2:13, OBS-111-070); (3) H3819 *lo ruchamah* (No Mercy — the prophetic name embodying mercy's withdrawal, Hos 1:6, OBS-111-058). The structural opposite operates at three levels: the inner character failure (mercilessness as constitution), the relational disposition (without mercy toward another), and the divine withdrawal (No Mercy as prophetic verdict). OBS-111-034, OBS-111-058, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-036 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Rom 1:31 | If the spirit-soul interface classification is correct, mercy sits at the deepest level of the person — the interface between the created spirit (the capacity for relationship with God) and the soul (the personal self). This would explain mercy's dual orientation: toward God (spirit-level connection with the divine mercy that is its source) and toward others (soul-level expression in relational life). The classification also explains why mercilessness appears as constitutional collapse (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034) rather than merely behavioral failure — it names a spirit-level deficiency. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-051. |
| OBS-111-T2-067 | OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Mercy deepens affect in both the seeking and receiving directions: seeking mercy is accompanied by intensified emotional expression (breast-beating, persistent cry against opposition); received mercy produces affective depth (joy, shame, wonder, silence). The mercilessness catalogue (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034) pairs mercilessness with *astorgos* (heartless/affectionless) — the absence of mercy is associated with affective incapacity. Mercy thus enables affect; its absence impairs it. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-047, OBS-111-054. |
| OBS-111-T2-068 | OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Affect is mercy's native register — mercy is felt before it is expressed, and received mercy produces felt response. The affective engagement is constitutive of mercy: a mercy that leaves no affective trace is not fully mercy in the biblical sense. Mercilessness as affective incapacity (Rom 1:31) confirms this — the loss of mercy is inseparable from the loss of felt inner connection to the other. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-039, OBS-111-047. |
| OBS-111-T2-071 | OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Mercy enables volition in the receiver: received mercy does not constrain future choice but expands the moral capacity to choose mercy toward others. Mat 18:33 implies that received mercy should have generated the volitional capacity to show mercy — the failure is in not exercising this enabled volition. Conversely, mercilessness (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034) appears alongside faithlessness — the merciless person's volitional capacity is compromised. *New finding: mercy is a volitional enabler — received mercy expands the person's capacity to make merciful choices; mercilessness marks a contracted volitional capacity for compassionate action.* OBS-111-034, OBS-111-055. |
| OBS-111-T2-083 | OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Mercy deepens conscientiousness: the person who has received mercy and understands what it cost (atonement through blood, Lev 17:11 — OBS-111-037) has a deeper moral seriousness about the wrongdoing that required mercy. Mercilessness impairs conscientiousness — the merciless catalogue (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034) pairs mercilessness with moral recklessness. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-037, OBS-111-055. |
| OBS-111-T2-138 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Yes — three terms: *aneleēmōn* (G0415 — merciless, Rom 1:31), *anileōs* (G0448 — without mercy, Jam 2:13), H3819 *lo ruchamah* (No Mercy — prophetic name, Hos 1:6). The structural opposite is lexically explicit at the character, relational, and prophetic-identity levels. OBS-111-014, OBS-111-034, OBS-111-058, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-034-T2-008 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:31 | The verse evidence provides clear indicators. Presence of covenant-faithfulness: in the human, keeping covenant is an expression of moral integrity (3308-002 — OBS-034-053), alignment with God's own character (Deu 7:9 — OBS-034-039), and is associated with knowing God and living under his blessing. Absence or corruption: G0802 (asunthetos — covenant-breaking as inner character defect — OBS-034-018, OBS-034-057) appears in Paul's catalogue of the debased mind (Rom 1:31) — the morally degraded human who has rejected God becomes incapable of covenant faithfulness. Dan 9:11 (OBS-034-036) frames collective covenant breach as the inner moral failure of a people. The pattern: covenant faithfulness indexes the health of the divine image in the person; covenant-breaking indexes its degradation. The person who cannot keep covenant is described in terms of inner moral collapse. |
| OBS-034-T2-017 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | The structural opposite of covenant is covenant-breaking — the inner disposition and act of faithlessness that violates and severs the covenant bond. The data names this explicitly: G0802 asunthetos (untrustworthy, covenant-breaking — OBS-034-018, OBS-034-057) is Paul's term for the morally debased person who cannot hold covenants together. H0423 alah (oath/curse — OBS-034-007, OBS-034-036) shows that breach activates the self-invoked curse embedded in the oath — covenant-breaking is the self-destruction of the bond one has made. H3748 keritut (divorce as cutting of the marriage covenant — OBS-034-012, OBS-034-043) names the formal opposite: the "cut" that severs the covenant bond. Dan 9:11 (OBS-034-036) frames collective covenant breach as inner moral failure — transgression, turning aside, refusing to obey, sinning. The opposite is therefore: faithlessness (the character quality), covenant-breaking (the act), and divorce/cutting-off (the formal result). At the deepest level: the structural opposite of covenant is the severing of self from the relational bond through infidelity. |
| OBS-034-T2-018 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | From the verse evidence: (1) Covenant explicitly excludes false covenants — making covenants with Canaanite inhabitants (3304-003 — OBS-034-048) is prohibited as a binding to false loyalties that misdirects inner allegiance away from God. (2) Covenant resists unfaithfulness — the curse embedded in the oath (H0423 — OBS-034-007) is the built-in mechanism resisting breach. (3) Covenant excludes the hardened-mind/veiled cognitive condition (2 Cor 3:14 — OBS-034-072) — the old covenant produces a veil; the new covenant resists and removes cognitive hardening. (4) Covenant resists the inner condition of alienation and hopelessness (Eph 2:12 — OBS-034-060) — covenant inclusion is the antidote to covenantal exclusion/desolation. In summary: covenant excludes false loyalty, resists infidelity, opposes cognitive hardening, and displaces inner alienation. |
| OBS-034-T2-029 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:31 | The verse evidence identifies blocking conditions: (1) Hardened mind — 2 Cor 3:14: "their minds were hardened. For to this day, when they read the old covenant, that same veil remains unlifted" (OBS-034-072). Cognitive hardening blocks the reception of the covenant's transformative content — the veil prevents seeing through the old covenant to Christ. (2) Stubborn/evil heart — Jer 11:8: "everyone walked in the stubbornness of his evil heart... they did not obey or incline their ear" (group 765-002 context). Stubbornness and inclination of the will away from God blocks reception. (3) Covenant-breaking character — G0802 asunthetos: the person whose inner character is faithlessness is already disposed against covenant-reception (OBS-034-057). (4) False covenantal allegiance — making covenants with the wrong parties (3304-003 — OBS-034-048) misdirects the will and blocks genuine covenantal reception with God. (5) The veil of the old covenant (2 Cor 3:14-16 — OBS-034-072) — the old covenant itself becomes a blocking mechanism when read without Christ. |
| OBS-034-T2-030 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:31 | The data provides a clear picture: (1) Desolation — Eph 2:12: "having no hope and without God in the world" (OBS-034-060). The person who encounters the covenants of promise and remains a stranger to them lives in inner desolation — hopelessness, alienation, godlessness. (2) Bondage — Gal 4:24-25 (OBS-034-071): remaining under the Sinai covenant without entering the new covenant freedom is described as bearing children for slavery — the inner condition is one of bondage. (3) Moral collapse — Rom 1:31: the covenant-breaker (asunthetos) appears in the catalogue of moral and cognitive failure that characterises those who have suppressed the truth (OBS-034-057). Not receiving the covenantal bond produces inner moral dissolution. (4) Curse activation — Dan 9:11 (OBS-034-036): the person who encounters the covenant, receives its obligations, but breaks it experiences the activated curse — the inner consequence of breach. The common thread: not receiving covenant (or receiving it falsely/partially) produces inner conditions of desolation, bondage, moral dissolution, or judgment. |
| OBS-034-T2-074 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Yes — moral evaluation is deeply engaged by covenant. (1) Covenant defines the moral standard against which inner fidelity is assessed: the covenant's terms (law, commandments, statutes) provide the content of moral evaluation (Deu 7:9 — OBS-034-039; 2Ki 23:3 — OBS-034-047). Living within covenant means living under a defined moral evaluative framework. (2) Covenant breach is a moral failure assessed by the moral evaluation faculty: Dan 9:11 — "we have sinned against him" (OBS-034-036) — Daniel's prayer of confession is a moral self-assessment in covenantal categories. (3) The oath/curse structure (H0423 — OBS-034-007) embeds a moral assessment mechanism in the covenant itself: the covenant includes its own evaluation criterion and sanction for breach. (4) G0802 (asunthetos — OBS-034-057): Paul's taxonomy in Rom 1:31 names covenant-breaking as a moral defect — the moral evaluation faculty of the apostle and of Scripture judges covenant-breaking as character vice. |
| OBS-034-T2-076 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:31 | The pattern reveals that covenant is intrinsically moral — it cannot be separated from moral evaluation because its structure embeds evaluation within it. The covenant oath (with its curse for breach — OBS-034-007) is not merely a relational bond but a morally evaluative structure: the person under covenant is always already under moral assessment relative to the covenant's terms. This means covenant is not a merely relational or emotional reality but a morally accountable one — being in covenant means being accountable to a standard. The new covenant's internalisation of the standard (law on the heart — Heb 8:10) means the moral evaluation faculty itself is transformed: the person evaluates from within a renewed inner standard rather than against an external one. The pattern confirms that the Moral Character dimension (05) is correctly assigned to multiple covenant groups — covenant-keeping is constitutively a moral-character reality. |
| OBS-034-T2-130 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Rom 1:31 | Yes — confirmed from §G.3 (OBS-034-032, OBS-034-033): Covenant shares 11 anchor verses with other registries. Key shared anchors: (1) Heb 8:10 — shared with R001 (abomination) and R112 (mind). (2) Neh 10:29 — shared with R019 (calling). (3) Eph 4:24 — shared with R076 (holiness). (4) Eph 2:12 — shared with R078 (hope) and R197 (authority). (5) Rom 1:31 — shared with R111 (mercy). (6) Dan 9:24 — shared with R147 (sin). (7) 2Ki 23:3 — shared with R159 (testimony). (8) 2Sa 23:5 — shared with R177 (worth). (9) Rev 2:17 — shared with R204 (name). (10) Isa 28:15 — shared with R078 (hope). These 11 shared-anchor relationships across 10 distinct registries confirm that covenant's anchor verses are structurally important to a wide range of programme registries — covenant is not isolated but serves as an anchor point for multiple inner-being characteristics. |
| OBS-034-T2-131 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Rom 1:31 | Three most analytically significant shared anchors: (1) Heb 8:10 shared with R001 (abomination) and R112 (mind): the new covenant's constitutional claim (law written on mind and heart) is an anchor for both the abomination and mind registries — revealing that the new covenant's inner inscription touches the cognitive inner-being territory that both registries engage. The fact that this verse anchors the abomination registry is unexpected and suggests that the abomination registry's engagement with this verse may be through the contrast between the abomination of old-covenant violation and the new-covenant remedy. Requires Session D investigation (SP-034-001 direction). (2) Eph 2:12 shared with R078 (hope) and R197 (authority): covenant and hope share this anchor, revealing that covenantal standing is the ground of hope (OBS-034-060) — and that authority-structures also anchor on this verse, suggesting that covenant intersects with authority in the domain of inclusion/exclusion from divine authority-structures. (3) Rom 1:31 shared with R111 (mercy): the covenantal defect (asunthetos) and mercy share an anchor — suggesting that the mercy registry engages the covenant-breaking context of Rom 1:31 as a site where mercy is absent, and that the covenant-mercy relationship requires cross-registry synthesis (SP-034-005). |
| OBS-034-T2-139 | ETYMOLOGY | Rom 1:31 | Yes — explicitly. (1) H3748 keritut (divorce — the cutting of the marriage covenant — OBS-034-012): the formal opposite of covenant-union is named by a term from the same cutting root. Divorce is the covenant-opposite explicitly named by covenant's own vocabulary. (2) G0802 asunthetos (untrustworthy/covenant-breaker — OBS-034-018): the NT names the covenant-lacking character type with a specific term — asunthetos = not-placed-together = unable to hold covenants. The structural opposite (covenant-breaking as character defect) has its own lexical item in the NT. (3) H3772J karat (to cut: lack — OBS-034-015): the negative of covenantal continuity — being cut off is the covenant-opposite of being in continuous covenant relationship. (4) H0423 alah (the curse embedded in the oath — OBS-034-007): the sanction for covenant-absence is named within the covenant vocabulary itself. The vocabulary is internally comprehensive — it names both the positive reality and its negation. |
| OBS-034-T2-140 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | The data contains one explicit person-type term adjacent to the covenant vocabulary. G0802 asunthetos (OBS-034-018) — the covenant-breaker as a character type — is a person-type term for the habitual absence of covenant faithfulness. However, a positive person-type term for the covenant-keeper (the habitually faithful covenant person) is not directly evidenced in the programme's vocabulary with a single dedicated term. The data does contain person-descriptions that function as person-type indicators: "those who love him and keep his commandments" (Deu 7:9), "those who keep his covenant and his testimonies" (Psa 25:10) — but these are phrases, not dedicated lexical items. Gap: a dedicated positive person-type term for the covenant-faithful person is not identified in the programme's term inventory. The negative person-type (asunthetos) is named; the positive is described but not lexically fixed. |
| OBS-034-T2-142 | ETYMOLOGY | Rom 1:31 | G0802 (asunthetos — untrustworthy/covenant-breaker — OBS-034-018) appears to be a distinctive NT-period term for the covenant-breaking character quality. While the word's components are classical Greek, its appearance in Paul's vice-catalogue (Rom 1:31) as a moral character term within a NT theological context is the word's most prominent biblical use. It represents the NT's naming of covenant-faithlessness as a specific moral character defect — giving an explicit lexical label to what the OT described through narrative and prophetic denunciation. The other NT-specific development is not a newly coined term but a newly deployed term: diathēkē in its testamentary sense (Heb 9:16-17) exploits an existing Greek legal term for theological purposes — not a coinage but a semantic deployment that generates a new theological meaning-range for an existing word. |

### G4835 sumpathēs — sympathetic

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G4835` · Lang: Greek · Owner: —
2. Registry: R023 compassion · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-B` (Compassion)
4. Term status: extracted_thin · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 1
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=3980` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=to_revise`
6. Mounce short-def: sympathetic

**Verses:** 1 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 335 | 3980-001 | Term names sympathy as a character disposition of the inner person — the settled quality of being responsive to the suffering of others |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (5)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-023-T2-013 | OBSERVATION |  | The data reveals that compassion is not a single kind of inner-being phenomenon — it operates across multiple kinds simultaneously, and the vocabulary differentiates them precisely. **Compassion as constitutive character quality (disposition):** H7349 ra.chum (compassionate) and G4835 sumpathēs (sympathetic) are adjectival forms naming the settled character quality — what kind of person one is. Lam 4:10 uses ra.cha.ma.ni (compassionate women) to name a character type. The person who is ra.chum or sumpathēs is constitutively compassionate — it describes their inner being, not a single act. **Compassion as inner movement (event/act):** G4697 splanchnizō and H5150 ni.chum both name the moment of compassion as a movement — a stirring, a kindling, a being-moved. Hos 11:8 ("my compassion grows warm and tender") captures the event quality: something happens inside at a specific moment. Splanchnizō in the Gospels consistently names a discrete event that precedes action. **Compassion as condition of the one who needs it:** G1652 eleeinos (pitiful) names the condition that calls forth compassion — the pitiable state. This is not compassion itself but its proper object, and its presence in the vocabulary reveals that compassion's operation is constitutionally linked to a condition of need. **Compassion as externalised act:** G1654 eleēmosunē (almsgiving) names the normalised outer form of compassion — the concrete act into which the inner movement arrives. The semantic development from inner pity to outer giving is encoded in this term's history (Pass 1). **Summary:** Compassion in this registry is a layered inner-being phenomenon: a character disposition that generates event-movements when triggered by need, which produce outward acts. The vocabulary differentiates all three layers, and the distinction between them is analytically essential. |
| OBS-023-T2-034 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — the evidence consistently locates compassion at the soul level, primarily through the chesed and channum vocabulary and the character-quality (adjectival) terms. H7349 ra.chum (compassionate) is an adjectival form naming the settled soul-level disposition — what kind of person one is constitutively. H2587 channum (gracious) is similarly a soul-level character descriptor, used almost exclusively as a divine attribute in the epithet formula. G4835 sumpathēs (sympathetic, 1 Pet 3:8) is placed in a list of soul-level character qualities alongside unity of mind, brotherly love, tender heart, and humble mind — all soul-level orientations. The chesed vocabulary (H2617B, H2617A as XREF) names compassion-love as a settled inner disposition of covenantal loyalty — a soul-level character reality, not a momentary event. The group descriptions for 1633-001 (God's steadfast love), 1633-002 (human disposition of covenantal loyalty), and 1633-003 (trust and hope grounded in chesed) all operate at the soul level. |
| OBS-023-T2-136 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — the vocabulary makes all three distinctions explicitly: **Disposition vs act:** Ra.chum / channum / sumpathēs (adjectival — disposition) vs ra.cham / splanchnizō / sumpatheō (verbal — act). The adjectival terms name what the person is; the verbal terms name what the person does or what happens within them. The vocabulary holds both and distinguishes them grammatically. **Received vs given:** The supplication vocabulary (techinah, tahanun — terms for seeking/receiving compassion) vs the splanchnizō/chesed vocabulary (terms for extending/giving compassion). These are distinct vocabulary families with distinct grammatical positions: the supplication terms name the human act of receiving-oriented appeal; the character terms name the giver's inner state. **Condition vs quality:** G1652 eleeinos (pitiful — the condition of the one who needs compassion) vs G4835 sumpathēs / H7349 rachum (sympathetic/compassionate — the quality of the one who gives it). The vocabulary explicitly names both the condition that calls for compassion and the quality that responds to it, which is unusual in the programme data. Most characteristics name only the quality, not the condition of its proper object. |
| OBS-023-T2-138 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — the data contains multiple person-type terms: **H2623 chasid** (XREF, 33 occurrences): "devout/pious/loyal person" — the one whose inner life is constituted by chesed. The chasid is the person-type whose character is defined by covenantal compassion-loyalty. This is the most developed person-type term in the vocabulary. **H7349 rachum** (XREF, 13 occurrences): as a divine epithet, this names God as the paradigmatic compassionate person-type. When applied to humans, it names the one who is constitutively compassionate. **H7362 ra.cha.ma.ni** (OWNER, 1 occurrence, Lam 4:10): adjectival — "compassionate (women)." Names the person-type by character quality. Used at the moment of its catastrophic violation — which reveals that the term was understood as a stable character-type identifier. **G4835 sumpathēs** (OWNER, 1 occurrence, 1 Pet 3:8): "sympathetic" as a character quality of the community member. The person who is sumpathēs is constitutively open to the inner experience of others. **G3356 metriopatheō context (Heb 5:2):** The high priest who "deals gently" is implicitly named as a person-type: the one who exercises calibrated, wise compassion arising from shared vulnerability. |
| SYN-INTER-023-013 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER |  | T1's four-kind structure is encoded in T7's vocabulary architecture. The character-quality kind (ra.chum) is carried by the adjectival forms (H7349, G4835); the inner-event kind (splanchnizō) by the verbal forms (G4697, H7355); the externalised-act kind by the noun forms and compound forms (G1656, H2617B). T7's vocabulary arc (womb to almsgiving) is the lexical expression of T1's range from the most interior character quality to the most exterior externalised act — the same spectrum named by the definition is traversed by the vocabulary. The dual primary anchor (T7) maps onto T1's dimensional analysis: Hos 11:8 provides the evidence for the divine relational disposition; Luke 10:33 provides the evidence for the human relational disposition as moral norm. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (0 unique)**

(none)

**SD pointers — direct (1)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-023-SD011 | MEDIUM |  | The classical sumpatheō (soul and body sympathise with each other, Aristotle) is imported into NT anthropology as the ground of Christian community. This raises a programme-level question: does the NT use of sym- compounds (sumpatheō, sumpaschō) draw on Greek philosophical anthropology about the unity of the person, and does this inform the programme's spirit-soul-body framework? Cross-registry: potentially Soul (Reg 182), Spirit (Reg 184), the whole C01 cluster. --- #### Term 15: G4835 sumpathēs — sympathetic (occ=1, extracted_thin) *Sense structure: Adjective form. Single occurrence: 1 Pet 3:8. LSJ notes: "affected by like feelings, sympathetic" — with classical examples citing the greater sympathy of mothers for their children. The term describes the settled character quality of being capable of fellow-feeling, not a momentary act of it. Semantic observation: The adjectival form establishes sympatheia as a character trait — the person who is sumpathēs is consistently responsive to others' suffering. 1 Pet 3:8 places it in a list of community virtues: unity of mind, sympathy, brotherly love, tender heart, humble mind. This is the community portrait — sympathy as one thread in the fabric of the new humanity. --- #### Term 16: G4841 sumpaschō — to suffer with (occ=3, extracted_thin) Sense structure: 1 Cor 12:26 (the body suffers together), Rom 8:17 ("if children, then heirs — heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ, provided we suffer with him"), and one other occurrence. LSJ: "have the same thing happen to one; to be affected in common with." Semantic observation: Sumpaschō* has two distinct usages: (a) mutual suffering within the body of Christ (1 Cor 12:26 — the ontological suffering-together that is a property of membership in the body); (b) sharing in Christ's sufferings as the condition of sharing in his glory (Rom 8:17 — the eschatological frame). |

### G5541 chrēsteuomai — be kind

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G5541` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R099 kindness · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: `M05-D` (Kindness and Goodness)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 1
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=5729` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
6. Mounce short-def: to be kind

**Verses:** 1 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1467 | 5729-001 | Kindness as inner quality of love — outward expression of love's inner character toward others |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (6)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-067-T2-119 | ROOT_FINDING | Gal 5:22 | Yes — root-level architecture is confirmed for both root families: TOV root family: H2896A (tov adjective, R67 OWNER) / H2895 (tov verbal — be pleasing, R103 XREF) / H2896B (tov adjective, various) / H2896C (to.vah — welfare, various) / H2898 (tuv — goodness nominal, R103). The TOV root generates terms distributed across R67, R103, and adjacent registries. AGATHO root family: G0019 (agathōsunē, R67 OWNER) / G0018 (agathos, R65 OWNER) / G0015 (agathopoieo, R65 XREF) / G0016 (agathopoiia, R65 XREF) / G0017 (agathopoios, R65 XREF) / G0014 (agathoergeō, R65 XREF) — all agatho- compounds distributed across R67 and R65. CHRESTO root family: G5544 (chrēstotēs, R67 OWNER) / G5543 (chrēstos, adjacent) / G5541 (chrēsteuomai, adjacent) / G5542 (chrēstologia, distortion form). (OBS-067-OBS-018, SBF-067-Q019, SP-067-001, SP-067-011) |
| OBS-067-T2-136 | ETYMOLOGY | N/A | The LXX connection is not directly addressed in the anchor verses, but inferrable evidence is present (SBF-067-Q097). Rom 3:12 cites Ps 14:3 LXX — confirming that chrēstotēs was used in the LXX to render a Hebrew moral-quality term in the goodness domain. This establishes lexical continuity: the LXX chrēstotēs bridges H2896A's moral register (OT) to the NT's chrēstotēs usage. G5543 (chrēstos — good/kind) and G5541 (chrēsteuomai — be kind) in the related-word set suggest a LXX-rooted word family inherited by the NT. For H2896A → Greek: the LXX renders tov primarily as agathos and kalos, providing the lexical bridge for the NT's agathos vocabulary. However, LSJ data was absent for both G0019 and G5544 (NO_WORD_ANALYSIS flags cover both — flagged in quality flags §7). The LXX analysis is therefore only partially supported by the available data. Genuine gap — NO_WORD_ANALYSIS flags on both Greek OWNER terms. (OBS-067-OBS-009, SBF-067-Q097) |
| OBS-099-T2-012 | OBSERVATION | 1Cor 13:4 · Gal 5:22 · Lam 3:22 | Three definitional disclosures: (a) Chesed (H2617A) is loyal-kindness — its semantic range (goodness, kindness, faithfulness) encompasses what English divides between kindness and faithfulness [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-083]. (b) Chrēstos/chrēstotēs (G5543/G5544) — benign goodness: agreeable, gentle, gracious; range from "easy" (yoke, Matt 11:30) to "kind in character" [OBS-099-024, XREG: G5544, OBS-099-090]. (c) Chrēsteuomai (G5541) — active form: love acting kindly, showing kindness is a mode of action [OBS-099-022]. Together: kindness is a loyal relational disposition (chesed) expressed in benign agreeable action (chrēstos) through deliberate acts of care (chrēsteuomai). |
| OBS-099-T2-069 | OBSERVATION | Pro 3:3 · 2Sa 22:26 · 1Cor 13:4 | Four root-sharing relationships: (a) CHESED root family: H2617A (chesed), H2616A (chasad — be kind), H2617B (chesed — shame), H2623 (chasid — pious/kind person). Shame-semantic of same root analytically significant: loyalty/faithfulness and shame are two faces of the same covenantal reality [OBS-099-008]. (b) CHRĒS root family (Greek): G5541 (chrēsteuomai), G5542 (chrēstologia), G5543 (chrēstos), G5544 (chrēstotēs) — kindness, action form, counterfeit, abstract noun [OBS-099-021]. (c) AM/IM root family: etymological connection without semantic kindness content [OBS-099-018]. |
| OBS-099-T2-073 | OBSERVATION | Lam 3:22 · Psa 63:3 · Gal 5:22 · Eph 4:32 | The lexical foundation is now strong following cross-registry supplementation. Primary terms: H2617A (chesed — 228 relevant verses, 3 groups covering divine and human chesed and its absence); H2623 (chasid — 31 relevant verses); H2616A (chasad verbal — 2 verses); G5543/G5544 (chrēstos/chrēstotēs — 12 relevant verses combined). Secondary OWNER terms: G5541 (chrēsteuomai — 1 verse), G5542 (chrēstologia — 1 verse). The lexical foundation spans both Testaments, action and noun forms, the person embodying the characteristic, and its counterfeit. Following cross-registry supplementation: comprehensive. One remaining gap: LXX background for Greek terms — LSJ data sparse for OWNER Greek terms [OBS-099-021]. |
| OBS-099-T2-089 | OBSERVATION | Lam 3:22 · Psa 4:3 · 1Cor 13:4 · Rom 16:18 | The full vocabulary arc for kindness spans Hebrew and Greek, noun and verb, abstract quality and embodied person, genuine expression and named counterfeit, positive attribute and negative consequence — and this breadth is itself analytically significant. Reading the arc as a whole: (a) The Hebrew root family (H2617A chesed — loyal kindness as noun; H2616A chasad — to be kind as verb; H2623 chasid — the kind person as identity; H2617B — shame/reproach as the negative consequence of its failure) covers the complete semantic circuit: what kindness is, how it is enacted, who embodies it, and what happens when it is absent or fails. The inclusion of shame as the negative of chesed reveals that chesed operates within an honour-covenant framework — its failure brings reproach, not merely disappointment [OBS-099-008]. (b) The Greek root family (G5543 chrēstos — kind as quality; G5544 chrēstotēs — kindness as abstract noun; G5541 chrēsteuomai — to act kindly as verb; G5542 chrēstologia — pleasant speech as counterfeit) similarly covers quality, abstraction, action, and counterfeit. The coinage of chrēstologia as a specific term for the counterfeit reveals that the Greek-speaking community around Paul had encountered false-kindness frequently enough to need a named category for it [OBS-099-021/023]. (c) Taken together, the vocabulary arc reveals that kindness is semantically rich in both Testaments — it is not a thin peripheral term but a conceptually dense one with its own vocabulary of enactment (verb forms), personification (chasid), negative (shame, severity), and falsification (chrēstologia). The complete semantic range encompasses: loyal covenant love (chesed), benign quality of character (chrēstos), the Spirit-produced virtue (chrēstotēs), the active exercise of love in the kind mode (chrēsteuomai), the person whose identity is shaped by this loyalty (chasid), and the imitative counterfeit (chrēstologia). This arc from concept to embodiment to action to falsification reveals kindness as one of the programme's most structurally complete vocabulary fields. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (21 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-103-T2-015 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:4 | Structurally complex from the first encounter. The four-mode taxonomy (OBS-103-033) — willed covenantal, compassionate responsive, relational-social, intense erotic — is the first-level complexity. Beneath that, the common structure: directed attachment toward an object, whose moral quality is entirely determined by the object (OBS-103-009, OBS-103-017). The disordered forms (OBS-103-036) share the same structure as the righteous forms. The three-axis directional structure (OBS-103-035) adds spatial complexity: love operates in three directions simultaneously. The tripartite kind (Q&A-016) adds ontological complexity: disposition, act, and condition. Love appears to be the programme's most structurally composite inner-being characteristic — not simple but architecturally layered. |
| OBS-103-T2-018 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:4 | 1Cor 13:4-7 provides the programme's most explicit catalogue of what love excludes: envy, boasting, arrogance, rudeness, self-seeking, irritability, resentfulness, rejoicing in wrongdoing (OBS-103-127). The list names love's resistances across moral, relational, and emotional registers. At the structural level, love resists division of ultimate allegiance (Jam 4:4 — cannot love God and world simultaneously, OBS-103-058), resists self-assertion as the primary orientation (love-of-self as the first item in the last-days catalogue, OBS-103-137), and resists the withholding of self (Joh 15:13 — love's maximum is the giving of the soul/life, OBS-103-125). Love also explicitly resists concealment of inner truth from the beloved: Lev 19:17 contrasts hate-as-silence with love-as-frank-correction (OBS-103-114). |
| OBS-103-T2-029 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:4 | The data names several inner blockers: (a) **love of darkness** — Joh 3:19 (OBS-103-147): the inner orientation toward darkness is the condition that prevents reception of love-as-light. The person who loves the darkness does not receive the light; the blocker is the prior love-commitment to a wrong object. (b) **love of money** — 1Ti 6:10 (OBS-103-132): love of money causes departure from faith — the love of a wrong object displaces the reception of divine love. (c) **pride/self-seeking** — 1Cor 13:4-7 (OBS-103-127): love of self expressed as arrogance and self-seeking is explicitly named as what love is not — the self-orientation blocks the other-orientation that genuine love requires. (d) **false-brotherhood orientation** — Gal 2:4 (OBS-103-068): the concealed inner agenda of the false brother prevents genuine love-reception within the community. The pattern: all inner blockers are disordered loves — prior commitments to wrong objects that displace the capacity for rightly directed love. |
| OBS-103-T2-084 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:4 | Yes. Joh 15:15 (OBS-103-052): the disciples are transformed from servants to friends through Christ's love — a fundamental identity and relational reclassification. 1Pe 1:22 (OBS-103-124): soul-purification as the outcome of the love-formation process — the inner person is changed by the pathway that leads to sincere love. The 1Cor 13 trajectory (OBS-103-127): love's sustained exercise produces a character that does not envy, boast, or seek its own — the moral character is transformed by the practice of love. Luk 7:47 (via SD-020): the woman who loves much has been transformed by forgiveness — the transformation in her produced the love-expression. Love both requires and produces transformation. |
| OBS-103-T2-104 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:4 | Yes — 48 shared anchor verses identified (§6, OBS-103-050). Most significantly: Exo 34:6 (5 registries: anger, patience, faith, compassion, calling), Gal 5:22 (5 registries: patience, joy, goodness, peace, faith), 1Cor 13:4 (3 registries: patience, pride, kindness), Mic 6:8 (6 registries: goodness, kindness, will, humility, compassion, condemnation), Jer 31:34 (3 registries: guilt, thought, forgiveness). The 48-anchor shared map is the richest in the programme — love's evidential base is the most cross-registered in the corpus. |
| OBS-103-T2-109 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:4 | Primary Hebrew terms and root meanings: **chesed** (H2617A) — root implies binding loyalty, covenant-fidelity; possibly related to the idea of steadfastness or bending toward; the most theologically laden term in the OT love vocabulary. **ahavah** (H0160) — root ahav (to breathe after, to desire) — implying love as a form of directed longing or desire. **racham** (H7355) / **rachamim** (H7356B) — root rechem (womb) — love as womb-like envelopment and tender compassion; the most somatically grounded root. **dod** (H1730G/H) — root unclear; used for lover/beloved in the Song — the erotic-relational vocabulary. Primary Greek terms: **agapē** (G0026) / **agapaō** (G0025) — of uncertain pre-classical etymology; LXX/NT coinage whose meaning is constituted by Scripture rather than importing a prior secular meaning (OBS-103-016). **phileō** (G5368) / **philia** (G5373) / **filos** (G5384) — root from philos (friend); classical Greek for natural affectionate love. Root meanings together reveal: love spans willed-covenant (chesed), longing-desire (ahavah), somatic-compassion (racham), erotic-beloved (dod), self-giving-willed (agapē), and natural-affection (phileō). |
| OBS-103-T2-117 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:4 | Yes — agapē itself is effectively a NT-period coinage in its theological-central usage. The word exists rarely in classical Greek but carries no comparable weight; its meaning is constituted by its LXX/NT usage (OBS-103-016). The coinage-significance: the NT needed a word for love that was not pre-loaded with the Platonic eros tradition or the friendship-philia tradition — agapē fills this gap, its meaning entirely constituted by Scripture. This is analytically significant: agapē is the programme's strongest example of a characteristic whose vocabulary definition is wholly internal to the scriptural corpus. What agapē means cannot be determined by reference to classical Greek usage — it must be read from the LXX and NT contexts themselves. |
| OBS-103-T2-119 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:4 | Identifying 1Jo 4:8 ('God is love') as the primary theological claim and Joh 15:13 as the primary ethical-definitional verse: In 1Jo 4:8: the predicate nominative construction ('God is love') makes love not merely an attribute of God but an identification — love as God's being. The verb 'is' (estin) establishes identity, not activity. In Joh 15:13: 'greater love has no one than this: that someone lay down his life for his friends' — love is the subject of a comparative superlative; the function is to name the outer limit of love by identifying its maximum expression (soul-giving). The primary term in both cases functions as the frame of reference against which all other love-instances are measured: Joh 15:13 defines love's maximum; 1Jo 4:8 defines love's origin. Together they establish the top and bottom of love's definitional range: what love ultimately is (God's being) and what love ultimately does (gives itself to death for the beloved). |
| OBS-103-T2-120 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:4 | Love's evidence base spans every major literary form: (a) **Wisdom/Proverb** (Pro 10:12, 27:6): requires attention to the comparative and aphoristic structure — proverbs describe patterns, not absolutes; (b) **Psalm** (Psa 63:3, 16:11, 97:10): requires reading as first-person prayer/meditation — the psalmist's inner orientation, not universal dogma; (c) **Prophetic oracle** (Jer 31:3, Eze 16:8, Isa 49:15): the love-language is covenantal and often metaphorical (marital imagery); responsible reading holds the metaphorical dimension; (d) **Epistle** (1Cor 13, 1Jo 4, Rom 5): the most theologically discursive form — allows propositional claims about love's nature; (e) **Gospel narrative** (Joh 3:16, Luk 7:38, Joh 15:13): love embedded in narrative events requiring attention to the narrative's argument; (f) **Song/Poem** (Song of Songs): erotic love in a canonical wisdom-poem; the allegorical interpretive tradition and the literal reading must both be held. No single form dominates — love's evidence is multi-formal. |
| OBS-103-T2-122 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:4 | Three dominant contextual settings: (a) **Covenantal** — the OT love vocabulary (chesed, ahavah, racham) is carried primarily in covenantal contexts (Sinai formula, Deuteronomy love-command, prophetic covenant-marriage imagery). This reveals love as the inner disposition of covenant — it is what the covenant relationship requires and produces at the inner-being level. (b) **Communal/ecclesial** — the NT love vocabulary (agapē in Paul and John) is carried in the context of the believing community (1Cor 13 in worship-chaos context; Joh 13:34 at the Last Supper; 1Th 3:12 in community growth prayer). This reveals love as the community's constitutive practice. (c) **Lament/petition** — Lamentations, Psalms of lament: love's permanence is specifically evidenced under conditions of suffering and judgment. This reveals that love's most definitive testimony is given precisely when circumstances deny it — chesed is most powerfully stated when experience is most desolate (Lam 3:22). |
| OBS-103-T2-167 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:4 | Love plays a dual role in the sanctification arc: it is both the **process** and the **goal**. As process: love operating through the life — obedience, community, training — is itself sanctifying. Pro 3:3's heart-inscription implies that sustained exercise of chesed changes the person over time into the kind of person whose heart has it written into it. Joh 14:15 (OBS-103-140): sustained love of Christ produces the commandment-keeping pattern that constitutes sanctification's behavioural dimension. As goal: the 1Cor 13 portrait is the telos of sanctification — a person who loves with no envy, arrogance, or self-seeking is the sanctification's outcome. 1Jo 4:18 (via SP-103-029): 'perfect love casts out fear' — perfect love is the eschatological form of sanctification's completion. Love is not one virtue among the list of sanctification's outputs; it is the organising content from which all other virtue flows and toward which sanctification moves. |
| OBS-099-T2-012 | OBSERVATION | 1Cor 13:4 | Three definitional disclosures: (a) Chesed (H2617A) is loyal-kindness — its semantic range (goodness, kindness, faithfulness) encompasses what English divides between kindness and faithfulness [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-083]. (b) Chrēstos/chrēstotēs (G5543/G5544) — benign goodness: agreeable, gentle, gracious; range from "easy" (yoke, Matt 11:30) to "kind in character" [OBS-099-024, XREG: G5544, OBS-099-090]. (c) Chrēsteuomai (G5541) — active form: love acting kindly, showing kindness is a mode of action [OBS-099-022]. Together: kindness is a loyal relational disposition (chesed) expressed in benign agreeable action (chrēstos) through deliberate acts of care (chrēsteuomai). |
| OBS-099-T2-014 | OBSERVATION | 1Cor 13:4 | Primarily a disposition — a stable inner orientation toward another — that generates states and acts. Three levels: (a) God's chesed "never ceases" — a permanent attribute, not episodic act [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-085]; (b) Pro 3:3 — chesed written on the heart — heart-inscription is the language of stable inner character [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]; (c) H2623 — the chasid names the person characterised by chesed — a dispositional identity, not an occasional actor [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. Acts of kindness (Ruth, David, chrēsteuomai) are expressions of the underlying disposition. |
| OBS-099-T2-018 | OBSERVATION | 1Cor 13:4 | Four explicit resistances: (a) Self-serving motivation — chrēstologia fails because inner motivation is self-serving [OBS-099-074]. (b) Envy, boasting, arrogance — 1Cor 13:4 places kindness within love against these [OBS-099-072]. (c) Ritual without inner disposition — Hos 6:6 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]. (d) Withdrawal from the other — Gen 44:29/32 show that the shadow of kindness is the burden accepted in remaining present rather than withdrawing [OBS-099-062/063]. |
| OBS-099-T2-019 | OBSERVATION | 1Cor 13:4 | (a) Not the same as love — 1Cor 13:4 places kindness as a mode of love's operation; love is the parent characteristic [OBS-099-072]. (b) Not compassion — compassion is visceral (womb-feeling); chesed is dispositional and covenantal [OBS-099-009]. (c) Not grace — grace names the favour; kindness names the disposition that extends it. (d) Not mere pleasantness — chrēstos includes ease but chesed's covenantal depth exceeds pleasant affect. Gap: precise kindness-love and kindness-mercy/compassion boundaries require Session D. SP-099-006 covers kindness-love boundary. |
| OBS-099-T2-032 | OBSERVATION | 1Cor 13:4 | Multi-stranded: (a) Chesed appears overwhelmingly in covenant contexts [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]. (b) All acts of kindness in the data are directed toward specific others [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-063, OBS-099-071, OBS-099-060]. (c) The structural opposite (pride, self-enclosure) is a relational failure [OBS-099-054]. (d) The counterfeit (chrēstologia) fails precisely in the relational direction [OBS-099-074]. Relational Disposition is the correct primary dimension. |
| OBS-099-T2-050 | OBSERVATION | 1Cor 13:4 | Yes — agency is constitutive of kindness. Chrēsteuomai (1Cor 13:4) — love acts kindly — names active exercise of agency in service of another [OBS-099-072]. Ruth, David, Judah — all agentive initiatives. The chasid is the active agent of kindness in the community. The contrast with chrēstologia (smooth talk without action [OBS-099-074]) reinforces that genuine kindness is enacted agency, not speech alone. |
| OBS-099-T2-069 | OBSERVATION | 1Cor 13:4 | Four root-sharing relationships: (a) CHESED root family: H2617A (chesed), H2616A (chasad — be kind), H2617B (chesed — shame), H2623 (chasid — pious/kind person). Shame-semantic of same root analytically significant: loyalty/faithfulness and shame are two faces of the same covenantal reality [OBS-099-008]. (b) CHRĒS root family (Greek): G5541 (chrēsteuomai), G5542 (chrēstologia), G5543 (chrēstos), G5544 (chrēstotēs) — kindness, action form, counterfeit, abstract noun [OBS-099-021]. (c) AM/IM root family: etymological connection without semantic kindness content [OBS-099-018]. |
| OBS-099-T2-070 | OBSERVATION | 1Cor 13:4 | Partial evidence for three boundaries: (a) Kindness vs. love: 1Cor 13:4 — kindness is a mode of love's operation, not its definition [OBS-099-072, Q&A-021]. Love is broader; kindness is the specific outward-oriented gracious-action mode. (b) Kindness vs. compassion: Eph 4:32 — kindness and tenderheartedness are distinct qualities [OBS-099-077]. Compassion feels; kindness acts from that feeling. (c) Kindness vs. grace: Eph 2:7 — grace names the favour; kindness names the disposition in which the favour is given [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Gap: boundaries implied by co-occurrence and context but not explicitly theorised. Session D needed for full boundary analysis. |
| OBS-099-T2-071 | OBSERVATION | 1Cor 13:4 | Three sets with structural significance: (a) Mic 6:8 — shared with Regs 23, 24, 67, 80, 103, 173, 177 [OBS-099-038, SP-099-003]. Justice, kindness, humility in a single normative statement. Density of shared registry anchors signals this is the C17 cluster's most central verse. (b) 1Cor 13:4 — shared with Regs 103, 116, 123 [OBS-099-039, SP-099-006]. NT equivalent of the Mic 6:8 compression. (c) Eph 4:32 — shared with Regs 68, 183 [OBS-099-041]. Kindness-tenderheartedness-forgiveness cluster grounded in divine grace. |
| OBS-099-T2-089 | OBSERVATION | 1Cor 13:4 | The full vocabulary arc for kindness spans Hebrew and Greek, noun and verb, abstract quality and embodied person, genuine expression and named counterfeit, positive attribute and negative consequence — and this breadth is itself analytically significant. Reading the arc as a whole: (a) The Hebrew root family (H2617A chesed — loyal kindness as noun; H2616A chasad — to be kind as verb; H2623 chasid — the kind person as identity; H2617B — shame/reproach as the negative consequence of its failure) covers the complete semantic circuit: what kindness is, how it is enacted, who embodies it, and what happens when it is absent or fails. The inclusion of shame as the negative of chesed reveals that chesed operates within an honour-covenant framework — its failure brings reproach, not merely disappointment [OBS-099-008]. (b) The Greek root family (G5543 chrēstos — kind as quality; G5544 chrēstotēs — kindness as abstract noun; G5541 chrēsteuomai — to act kindly as verb; G5542 chrēstologia — pleasant speech as counterfeit) similarly covers quality, abstraction, action, and counterfeit. The coinage of chrēstologia as a specific term for the counterfeit reveals that the Greek-speaking community around Paul had encountered false-kindness frequently enough to need a named category for it [OBS-099-021/023]. (c) Taken together, the vocabulary arc reveals that kindness is semantically rich in both Testaments — it is not a thin peripheral term but a conceptually dense one with its own vocabulary of enactment (verb forms), personification (chasid), negative (shame, severity), and falsification (chrēstologia). The complete semantic range encompasses: loyal covenant love (chesed), benign quality of character (chrēstos), the Spirit-produced virtue (chrēstotēs), the active exercise of love in the kind mode (chrēsteuomai), the person whose identity is shaped by this loyalty (chasid), and the imitative counterfeit (chrēstologia). This arc from concept to embodiment to action to falsification reveals kindness as one of the programme's most structurally complete vocabulary fields. |

## §4. Appendix — items linked at registry or collection level

### §4.1 Findings from contributor registries with no term-level link (837)

These findings sit on a registry that contributes terms to this cluster, but their text and anchor verses don't resolve to any specific cluster term. Candidates for term-level enrichment.

| Finding ID | Type | Registry | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-135-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R135 repentance |  | Registry 135 shows a consistent human-divine mirroring structure: nearly every human inner-being act (turning, relenting, comforting, recalling) has a divine counterpart in the same registry (God relenting, God comforting, God withholding wrath). Session B should analyse this pairing as a key structural feature of the repentance domain in Scripture. |
| DIM-142-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R142 self-control |  | Registry 142 (self-control) shows the self-governance vocabulary operating in three registers: (a) human self-control as character virtue (1106-001, 1113-001, 6203-001), (b) human volitional exercise of restraint (1107-001, 6208-001, 1109-001, 1111-001), (c) Spirit-gift of self-discipline as divine endowment (1112-001). The human effort and the divine gift coexist and the vocabulary does not sharply distinguish them. Session B should examine whether biblical self-control is primarily a virtue cultivated, a gift received, or an exercise of will — or whether all three are held in tension as aspects of the same inner-being governance. |
| DIM-19-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R019 calling | Rom 8:30; Isa 43:1; Gal 4:6 | The calling registry (Reg 19) has 13 of 24 groups (54%) assigned Divine-Human Correspondence — the highest concentration of any single registry in the programme to date. The calling vocabulary is structurally a divine-human act: God calls, names, and constitutes; the human receives, responds, and is formed. Session B should examine how the human inner person is changed by the divine call, and what distinguishes the groups where HUMAN volition or emotion is the primary inner-being phenomenon from those where the divine calling act is the governing feature. |
| DIM-19-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R019 calling | Romans 8:15; Galatians 4:6 | Group 727-001 (krazō — 'Abba! Father!', Rom 8:15; Gal 4:6) is the programme's clearest example of the Spirit acting from within the human inner being: the Spirit's cry is simultaneously divine and human. This is the most acute instance of Divine-Human Correspondence encountered so far — not the Spirit acting on the human from outside, but the Spirit expressing itself through and as the human inner person's deepest cry.
