| OBS-062-T2-097 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The data shows fellowship operating both within and across covenantal contexts. **Within covenantal context:** Mal 2:14 (marriage covenant), Psa 119:63 (covenant community of fear-of-God), group 873-001 (fellowship with Father and Son — covenantal relationship). Much of the evidence is covenantally located. **Across covenantal boundaries:** Ecc 9:4 — *he who is joined with all the living has hope* — this verse uses the joining vocabulary in relation to *all the living*, not a specific covenant community. The fellowship of common life (being alive) crosses covenantal particularity. **Ecc 4:10** — *if they fall, one will lift up his fellow* — the context does not specify a covenant community. The mutual-support function of fellowship appears to operate at the level of shared human life, not only within covenant boundaries. **New finding:** Fellowship has a covenantal form (operating within covenant at its highest registers) and a common-life form (operating across the shared boundary of human existence). This distinction parallels the difference between koinōnia vocabulary (highly particular, often covenantally located) and chavar/chaver vocabulary (wider application, including common-life contexts). This has implications for the programme's understanding of fellowship's relational scope. |
| OBS-062-T2-098 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The relational scope is graduated: **Highest scope — divine nature fellowship:** Available to those who receive God's promises and escape corruption (2Pe 1:4). The scope is not ethnically or nationally defined — it follows the receiving of divine promises. **Covenant-community scope:** Psa 119:63 — those who fear God and keep his precepts. The scope follows moral-spiritual alignment, not ethnic or national identity. **Moral-compatibility scope:** 2Cor 6:14 — fellowship of righteousness excludes lawlessness. The scope is defined by moral-spiritual character. **Common-life scope:** Ecc 4:10, Ecc 9:4 — the mutual-support and hope-of-living fellowship extends to all who share in common life. The scope here is the broadest. **Exclusions:** Moral incompatibility (2Cor 6:14), idolatrous orientation (Hos 4:17), and faithlessness (Mal 2:14) all define who is excluded from genuine fellowship at each register. |
| OBS-062-T2-099 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The occult charming groups (7565-002, 7569-001: Psa 58:5; 7569-002: Isa 47:9) constitute the most direct evidence of fellowship vocabulary applied in the spiritual-beings domain: **Occult charming as adversarial spiritual engagement:** The chavar and chever vocabulary applied to charming/enchanting (Psa 58:5) and sorcery (Isa 47:9) represents the fellowship-joining vocabulary engaged in a forbidden mode of spiritual engagement. The charmer is seeking to create a *joining* with spiritual powers through occult practice — a misdirected spiritual fellowship. This is not angelic ministry — it is adversarial spiritual misdirection using the fellowship-joining capacity in a forbidden register. The occult practitioner is joining themselves to spiritual realities (implicitly adversarial) through the enchantment mechanism. |
| OBS-062-T2-100 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The evidence implies adversarial weaponisation of the fellowship-joining capacity, though the data does not state this explicitly. The idolatrous joining mode (Hos 4:17 — *Ephraim is joined to idols*) and the occult-charming mode (groups 7565-002, 7569-001, 7569-002) together suggest that the joining/fellowship capacity can be captured and misdirected: the person's constitutional joining-capacity is engaged, but directed toward wrong spiritual objects (idols, occult powers) rather than toward God. **New finding:** Fellowship's joining capacity may be structurally the most vulnerable faculty to adversarial misdirection, precisely because it is the constitutional capacity through which the human person connects most deeply to any reality. The same faculty that enables participation in divine nature (2Pe 1:4) can be engaged to produce joining to idols (Hos 4:17) and spiritual bondage. This vulnerability is implicit in the data but not assembled as a finding. |
| OBS-062-T2-101 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Yes. Fellowship produces transformation at multiple registers, and the data distinguishes condition-change from orientation-change. **Condition change:** 2Pe 1:4 — the person *becomes a partaker of the divine nature* and *escapes from the corruption that is in the world*. This is ontological condition change — the person's constitutional status is altered through fellowship with divine nature. The person who was in corruption becomes a divine-nature participant. **Orientation change:** Psa 119:63 — sustained companionship with the fear-of-God community produces moral alignment. The person's inner orientation is progressively shaped to conform with those they companion with. This is not (primarily) a condition change but an orientation change — the person is reoriented through the fellowship bond. **Both simultaneously:** Phili 3:10 — *becoming like him in his death* — the conformity to Christ's death pattern is both a condition change (something happens constitutionally through the fellowship of suffering) and an orientation change (the person's inner posture toward suffering and death is transformed through the participatory fellowship). **Pro 20:30 and Isa 53:5:** The wound-fellowship groups produce condition change — evil is cleansed from the innermost parts (Pro 20:30); healing is received through the redemptive wound (Isa 53:5). These are genuine condition changes mediated through fellowship. |
| OBS-062-T2-102 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The data provides evidence in both directions. **Toward irreversibility:** Jer 13:23 — the leopard's spots image. The group description reads: "indelibly formed inner moral character depicted as fixed marking." The fellowship-vocabulary (chavar root) is here applied to constitutional formation so thoroughgoing that reversal is presented as inconceivable. Character conformity through sustained fellowship (including evil fellowship) can become constitutionally fixed. **Toward reversibility — the idolatrous joining:** Hos 4:17 — *Ephraim is joined to idols; leave him alone* — the divine abandonment of the idolatrously-joined person implies that the situation is reversible in principle (God could reclaim; abandonment is the current response to the misdirected joining), even if the immediate condition is one of spiritual bondage. **The wound-healing pattern:** Pro 20:30 and Isa 53:5 — the wound-fellowship produces purification and healing. These are presented as genuine and completed changes, but the data does not address their permanence or reversibility. **New finding:** The reversibility question for fellowship is object-dependent. Wrong fellowship that produces constitutional character fixity (Jer 13:23) tends toward irreversibility. Wound-fellowship that produces purification and healing may be more permanent in the positive direction. But the data does not resolve this distinction definitively. It is a Session D question. |
| OBS-062-T2-103 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Yes. Finding 062-SEQUE-001 identifies discernible sequences. The most clearly described sequences: **Sequence 1 (2Pe 1:4):** *God grants promises → person escapes corruption → person becomes partaker of divine nature*. States: (1) prior corruption; (2) promise-reception and escape; (3) divine nature participation. **Sequence 2 (Phili 3:10):** *Knowing Christ → sharing sufferings → becoming like him in his death → (implied) resurrection*. States: (1) knowing-encounter; (2) participatory suffering; (3) conformity to death; (4) resurrection orientation. **Sequence 3 (1Jo 1:3):** *Seeing and hearing → proclamation → new fellowship created*. States: (1) witness reception; (2) proclamatory extension; (3) fellowship entry by the new participant. **Sequence 4 (Pro 20:30, Isa 53:5):** *Wound-received → evil cleansed / healing received → purified/healed inner state*. States: (1) prior woundedness or sinful condition; (2) fellowship-wound as instrument; (3) cleansed/healed inner condition. |
| OBS-062-T2-104 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The sequences reveal a common structural logic: **fellowship operates as a prerequisite-to-participation mechanism** (finding 062-SEQUE-001). In every sequence: 1. Something must be received, escaped, or entered first (promise, encounter with Christ, proclamation, wound). 2. The fellowship itself is then entered or experienced. 3. The fellowship produces a transformed state (divine nature participation, resurrection orientation, new fellowship bond, cleansed inner condition). The sequence is always: enabling condition → fellowship entry → transformed outcome. Fellowship is not a state the person is already in; it is entered through a prior enabling condition and produces a further transformed state beyond itself. This is structurally important: fellowship is a *movement* rather than a *position*. |
| OBS-062-T2-105 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The data supports four distinct mechanisms. The instruction's four-mechanism typology (declarative encounter, cosmic atonement, cultic ritual, gradual formation) maps partially onto the fellowship evidence: **Declarative encounter:** 1Jo 1:3 — the proclamation creates fellowship through the act of hearing. The declared witness produces an immediate relational bond. This is encounter-fellowship. **Cosmic/redemptive:** Isa 53:5 — the servant's wounds produce healing through vicarious substitutionary bearing of transgressions. This is the cosmic-atonement register of fellowship-mechanism. **Disciplinary:** Pro 20:30 — *blows that wound cleanse away evil*. This is the discipline-mechanism: the wounding administered by a disciplinary agent produces inner purification. **Gradual formation:** Psa 119:63 — companionship with the fear-of-God community produces ongoing moral alignment. This is gradual formation through sustained fellowship: character is shaped over time through sustained relational orientation. **Grant-and-participation:** 2Pe 1:4 — God grants promises → person escapes corruption → participates in divine nature. This is neither purely sudden nor purely gradual — it is a divine enabling followed by progressive participation. It does not fit neatly into the four-mechanism typology. |
| OBS-062-T2-106 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Yes. The mechanisms are context-specific: - **Relational horizontal context (Ecc 4:10, Psa 119:63, 1Jo 1:3):** Gradual formation and encounter-proclamation mechanisms dominate. - **Suffering context (Pro 20:30, Phili 3:10):** Disciplinary and participatory mechanisms dominate. - **Redemptive context (Isa 53:5):** Cosmic/substitutionary mechanism is singular. - **Ontological context (2Pe 1:4):** Divine grant-and-participation mechanism is distinct from all others. The mechanisms are not interchangeable — each operates in its specific fellowship register and context. |
| OBS-062-T2-107 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship is deeply and distinctively connected to suffering across multiple groups. **Fellowship as the form of suffering (Phili 3:10):** The *koinōnia tōn pathēmatōn autou* — fellowship of Christ's sufferings — names suffering as the very content of this form of fellowship. Suffering is the mode in which this fellowship is entered: the person does not merely endure suffering but *shares* it with Christ. **Fellowship as the instrument of transformation through suffering (Pro 20:30):** Disciplinary wounding (fellowship vocabulary) is the instrument of inner purification. Suffering is the mechanism, not merely the context. **Fellowship as the product of redemptive suffering (Isa 53:5):** The servant's suffering produces fellowship with healing in others. The recipients' fellowship-with-healing is the product of the servant's suffering. **Fellowship as ground of hope under affliction (Ecc 9:4):** *He who is joined with all the living has hope* — being in fellowship sustains hope in the face of the mortality that afflicts all living. Fellowship is the antidote to the inner consequences of shared creaturely affliction. Finding 062-EXTRE-001 addresses this: "fellowship in extremity reveals profound depth of operation... fellowship operates even through mortality threshold." |
| OBS-062-T2-108 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The evidence supports all four relationships between suffering and fellowship: **Suffering produces fellowship (Isa 53:5):** The servant's wound-suffering produces healing-fellowship in recipients. Suffering is generative of fellowship in the redemptive mode. **Suffering deepens fellowship (Phili 3:10):** The fellowship of Christ's sufferings is not a shallow relational category — entering it requires and produces a depth of participatory knowing that cannot be achieved by other means. Suffering deepens the fellowship. **Suffering tests fellowship (Mal 2:14, Ecc 4:10):** The faithlessness of Mal 2:14 is a failure of fellowship under the test of relational difficulty. Ecc 4:10 names the fellowship that lifts the fallen — this is fellowship tested by the extremity of another's falling. **Suffering reveals fellowship's nature (Phili 3:10):** The fellowship of sufferings reveals that fellowship is not a comfort-seeking arrangement but a participatory reality that includes the whole range of human experience, including death. **New finding:** Fellowship has a suffering-depth dimension — the characteristic's deepest and most theologically significant expressions (Phili 3:10, Isa 53:5, Pro 20:30) are all connected to suffering. Fellowship at its surface (mutual support, companionship, proclamation) is visible; fellowship at its depth (suffering-participation, redemptive wounding, conformity to death) is entered only through suffering. This depth structure is assembled as a new finding. |
| OBS-062-T2-109 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Yes. Finding 062-CHAR-001 establishes this explicitly: "fellowship amplifies and conforms character to the fellowship object's nature." The longer arc of character formation is precisely what fellowship produces through sustained relational orientation. **Righteous character formation:** Psa 119:63 — sustained companionship with those who fear God produces moral alignment with righteousness. Over time, the person becomes like those they companion with. **Divine likeness formation:** 2Pe 1:4 — participation in divine nature enables progressive escape from corruption and growing participation in divine character. This is sanctification through fellowship with the divine nature. **Conformity to Christ:** Phili 3:10 — *becoming like him in his death* — the progressive conformity to Christ's pattern is the longer arc of sanctification through fellowship with his sufferings. **Wicked character formation:** Isa 1:23 (companions of thieves) and Job 34:8 (walking with wicked men) — sustained wrong fellowship produces conformity to wickedness. Character formation operates in both directions. |
| OBS-062-T2-110 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship's role in character formation and sanctification is not peripheral but **constitutive and determinative**. The data reveals that: 1. **Fellowship determines the direction of character formation:** The person becomes like what they are joined to. There is no neutral fellowship — every sustained joining either moves the person toward righteousness or wickedness (finding 062-CHAR-001). 2. **Fellowship is the primary mechanism of character conformation:** Not primarily through information, command, or effort, but through relational participation — the person is shaped by being genuinely joined to what they participate in. 3. **The highest sanctification is fellowship-based:** The divine nature participation of 2Pe 1:4 and the conformity to Christ of Phili 3:10 are not achieved through discipline or intellectual understanding but through participatory fellowship. **New finding:** Fellowship is presented in the data as the primary mechanism of character formation — more fundamental than discipline, information, or even explicit moral command. Character is not primarily formed by what the person knows or is told but by what they are joined to. This is the deepest sanctification insight of the fellowship registry, not previously assembled as a standalone finding. |
| OBS-062-T2-111 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The evidence carries a forward orientation, as noted at T0.2 Prompt 3 (new finding established there). The eschatological indicators: **Phili 3:10-11:** The fellowship of Christ's sufferings is explicitly oriented toward *the resurrection of the dead* (v.11, adjacent context). The fellowship trajectory is: suffering → death → resurrection. The resurrection is the eschatological terminus of the fellowship-with-Christ sequence. **2Pe 1:4:** Divine nature participation enables escape from corruption and progressive participation in divine nature — a trajectory that points toward the full removal of corruption and the completion of divine likeness. The present experience is participatory entry into what will be possessed in fullness eschatologically. **Ecc 9:4 — present fellowship grounding hope:** *He who is joined with all the living has hope* — hope is named as the affective orientation of present fellowship. Hope is by definition forward-facing. **Gap:** No verse in the registry explicitly addresses a future fullness of fellowship beyond resurrection (new creation, eternal fellowship). The eschatological data is present but partial — it extends to resurrection but not beyond. |
| OBS-062-T2-112 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **Present divine nature participation anticipates full divine likeness:** 2Pe 1:4 — the present *becoming* a partaker of divine nature is not the fullness; it is the entry. The future fullness would be the complete realisation of that participation without the present constraint of corruption. **Present fellowship of sufferings anticipates resurrection:** Phili 3:10-11 — the fellowship entered through suffering and death anticipates the resurrection as its completion. What is now entered through pain and death will be completed in the resurrection-mode of fellowship. **Present hope anticipates the removal of what grounds fear:** Ecc 9:4 — the fellowship that grounds hope in the present is a fellowship *with the living*. The eschatological fullness would be fellowship that is no longer threatened by death — the mortal fellowship of the living being completed in an immortal fellowship. **New finding:** The present experience of fellowship in every register is presented as partial and forward-facing: participation is entered but not yet complete; hope is grounded but not yet fulfilled; suffering-fellowship is entered but resurrection is not yet fully realised. Fellowship is constitutively *not yet* at its highest registers — it is oriented toward a fullness it is moving into. This is a structural feature of the characteristic assembled for the first time here. |
| OBS-062-T2-113 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **T2.8 found no constitutional bodily deposit for fellowship. T5.7 is formally closed.** The evidence is silent on constitutional deposit in the body, including DNA or generational consequence. No verse in the registry addresses this question directly. The Jer 13:23 image (indelible character) approaches this territory but operates at the constitutional-character level rather than the somatic-deposit level. T5.7 formally closes on the basis of T2.8 silence. |
| OBS-062-T2-114 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | From the co-occurrence table (Section 5 of the data package), the top patterns: **Highest co-occurrence (5 shared verses each, three-way tie):** - R187 — strength (5 shared verses) - R043 — desire (5 shared verses) - R057 — evil (5 shared verses) **Second tier (4 shared verses):** - R167 — unity (4 shared verses) **Third tier (3 shared verses each):** - R103 — love; R112 — mind; R121 — praise; R151 — sorrow; R192 — comfort; R198 — might; R031 — corruption; R051 — distress; R059 — faith; R061 — fear; R068 — grace; R090 — innocence; R098 — justice; R099 — kindness The `shared_term_count` is 0 and `term_sharing_ratio` is 0.0 — fellowship shares no vocabulary terms with other registries. All co-occurrence is at the verse level rather than the term level. |
| OBS-062-T2-115 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The three-way tie at the top (strength R187, desire R043, evil R057) is analytically significant and unexpected. **Expected high signals:** Unity (R167, 4 shared verses) and love (R103, 3 shared verses) co-occurring with fellowship are expected — they are structurally adjacent characteristics. **Unexpected high signals:** - **Desire (R043, 5 shared verses, highest):** The pairing of fellowship and desire as the most frequent co-occurrence is not anticipated from the surface definition of fellowship. Desire is the motivating inner-faculty that drives seeking. The highest co-occurrence may indicate that fellowship is constitutively desire-linked: the seeking of fellowship is driven by desire, and the fellowship vocabulary appears where desire is most intensely present. - **Evil (R057, 5 shared verses):** The pairing with evil at the highest level reflects the large proportion of fellowship vocabulary in contexts of wrong fellowship (idolatrous joining, moral complicity, companionship with evildoers, occult charming). Fellowship's most frequent co-occurrence context is not with righteous characteristics but at the boundary with evil. - **Strength/Might (R187, 5 shared verses; R198, 3 shared verses):** This pairing is not immediately obvious from the fellowship definition. It may reflect the wound-fellowship groups (Isa 53:5, Pro 20:30, Phili 3:10) where suffering and power are adjacent themes. **New finding:** The co-occurrence pattern reveals that fellowship operates most densely at the moral boundary zone — where the desire-for-connection meets the choice between righteousness and evil. The characteristic is not primarily a positive-association characteristic (it does not co-occur most with love, peace, or joy) but a *boundary* characteristic: it is most active where the relational joining capacity must choose its direction. This is structurally consistent with the six modes of operation (finding 062-OPER-001) — fellowship is morally neutral at the joining level and morally determined by its object. |
| OBS-062-T2-116 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Finding 062-SEQUE-001 establishes sequences. Translated to inter-characteristic sequential patterns: **Fellowship follows/is enabled by:** Faith (R059 — 3 shared verses) and grace (R068 — 3 shared verses) appear in the co-occurrence data, and the sequence implied by 2Pe 1:4 (divine promise → escape from corruption → fellowship with divine nature) suggests that grace precedes and enables fellowship in the vertical register. **Fellowship precedes/produces:** Hope (Ecc 9:4 — fellowship produces hope), praise (R121 — 3 shared verses, likely connected to worship-fellowship contexts), and character formation (finding 062-CHAR-001). Fellowship is sequential *before* character conformity is established. **Fellowship accompanies:** Love (R103, 3 shared verses) and unity (R167, 4 shared verses) are frequently co-present with fellowship rather than sequentially preceding or following it — they are companion characteristics in similar relational contexts. |
| OBS-062-T2-117 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **Causal relationship (fellowship → hope):** Ecc 9:4 presents fellowship as the direct cause of hope. This is a causal relationship: the joining with the living *produces* hope as its direct output. **Developmental relationship (fellowship → character formation):** Finding 062-CHAR-001 and T5.5 establish that sustained fellowship gradually produces character conformity. This is developmental — it operates over time through the process of sustained joining. **Enabling relationship (grace/faith → fellowship):** 2Pe 1:4 implies that the divine promises (grace) are the enabling condition for divine nature fellowship. Grace is not caused by fellowship; rather, grace enables it. This is an access-instrumental relationship — grace is the key that opens fellowship. **Correlational (fellowship + unity, fellowship + love):** The co-occurrence of fellowship with unity and love is likely correlational — they appear in similar contexts because they are companion characteristics operating in the same relational domain, not because one produces the other. **Gap:** The precise causal vs. enabling vs. correlational relationships between fellowship and its most frequent co-occurrents are not systematically analysed in the data. This is Session D territory. |
| OBS-062-T2-118 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Yes. The data establishes several causal outputs: - **Hope (Ecc 9:4):** Fellowship → hope. Mechanism: communal joining with the living grounds the affective orientation of forward expectation. - **Character conformity (finding 062-CHAR-001):** Sustained fellowship → moral character aligned with the fellowship object. Mechanism: participatory formation through relational joining. - **Healing (Isa 53:5):** Redemptive wound-fellowship → healing. Mechanism: substitutionary bearing of transgressions produces restoration in recipients. - **Purification (Pro 20:30):** Disciplinary wound-fellowship → cleansing of innermost parts. Mechanism: disciplinary contact reaches the constitutional moral core. - **Enabling capacity (finding 062-ENABLE-001):** Fellowship with Christ → capacity for shared suffering and resurrection orientation. Mechanism: participatory knowing of Christ opens constitutional capacities not otherwise available. |
| OBS-062-T2-119 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The data implies several inputs that produce or enable fellowship: - **Fear of God (R061, 3 shared verses):** Psa 119:63 — fear of God and precept-keeping ground righteous companionship. Fear of God appears to be a generating condition for righteous fellowship. - **Grace (R068, 3 shared verses) and divine promise:** 2Pe 1:4 — divine grace/promise enables divine nature fellowship. Grace produces the access to vertical fellowship. - **Proclamation/faith (faith R059, 3 shared verses):** 1Jo 1:3 — hearing the proclamation produces fellowship entry. Faith-reception of proclamation generates horizontal fellowship. **Gap:** Whether these are strictly causal (X produces fellowship) or enabling (X gives access to fellowship) is a distinction the data does not resolve. The relationship between fear of God and fellowship, in particular, deserves sharper analysis than the data provides. |
| OBS-062-T2-120 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | DIM-62-SD001 explicitly raises this question: "whether the fellowship vocabulary of Reg 62 encodes a structural relationship: that genuine human inner-relational community is grounded in and derives from participation in the divine community... This would make Reg 62 (fellowship) functionally analogous to Reg 34 (covenant) as a structural container." This is the programme's most significant structural question about fellowship: is fellowship a constituent part of unity (R167), love (R103), or covenant (R34), or are those characteristics constituent parts of fellowship? Or is fellowship the structural container within which multiple other characteristics operate? **Gap:** This question is formally deferred to Session D per DIM-62-SD001. It cannot be resolved at the current stage. |
| OBS-062-T2-121 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The `shared_term_count` is 0 and `term_sharing_ratio` is 0.0. Fellowship shares no vocabulary terms with other programme registries at the term level. The 13 OWNER terms are all exclusively owned by this registry. This is notable: for a characteristic with 13 active terms and 89 active verses, having zero shared terms is an unusually high degree of lexical exclusivity. It indicates that the fellowship/joining root family (chavar and koinōnia families) does not cross-register into other characteristics' term inventories. |
| OBS-062-T2-122 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The term-level sharing is zero, but the root-level picture is more complex. **The chavar root family:** DIM-062-SD001 notes that the chavar root family includes physical joining vocabulary used extensively in the tabernacle construction (Exo 26:3 etc.) and other structural contexts, where the physical act of joining illuminates inner-being realities through analogy. The question of whether the root *chavar* (joining, binding) generates conceptual connections to covenant (R34) or unity (R167) at the root-semantic level — rather than the term level — is precisely the question DIM-062-SD001 defers to Session D. **The koinōnia family:** koinōnia and koinōnos share the *koinos* root with koinōnō (to share) and related terms. Whether any programme registry contains koinōnō or closely related Greek terms not included in this registry is not determinable from the data package available. **New finding:** The zero term-sharing ratio, combined with the DIM-062-SD001 observation about root-level connections in the tabernacle corpus, suggests that fellowship's structural isolation at the term level may mask deeper root-level conceptual connections. The boundary between term sharing and root sharing is an analytically important distinction that the data leaves unresolved. |
| OBS-062-T2-123 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The zero term-sharing ratio reveals that fellowship occupies a relatively isolated semantic space within the programme's vocabulary at the term level. It does not penetrate other characteristics' term inventories, and no other characteristic's vocabulary penetrates fellowship's. This isolation is conceptually significant: fellowship's joining/participation vocabulary is semantically distinctive enough that it does not blend at the term level with adjacent characteristics (covenant, unity, love). The boundaries at the vocabulary level are clearer than the boundaries at the verse-evidence level (where co-occurrence patterns are substantial). **Interpretation (cautious):** The term-level isolation may indicate that fellowship names a *structurally distinct* inner-being reality — the joining-participation itself — rather than a variant of love, covenant, or unity. These adjacent characteristics operate with their own term families; fellowship's term family does not intrude into them. This supports the programme's placement of fellowship as a distinct registry rather than a sub-category of another characteristic. |
| OBS-062-T2-124 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **No vocabulary sharing at the term level is evidenced (shared_term_count = 0, term_sharing_ratio = 0.0).** The closing condition applies at the term-sharing level, though root-level connections are raised as a partial finding above. |
| OBS-062-T2-125 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The closest adjacent characteristics, based on the co-occurrence and shared anchor data: **Unity (R167, 4 shared verses):** Unity names the state of oneness; fellowship names the participatory experience that generates or constitutes that oneness. The distinction: unity is a state, fellowship is a process and participation. A group can have unity without fellowship being its name; fellowship always involves genuine shared participation, not merely structural unity. **Love (R103, 3 shared verses):** Love is the motivating inner-quality from which fellowship may flow; fellowship is the structural relational reality love creates or sustains. Love can exist without being expressed as fellowship; fellowship without love risks becoming formal compliance. **Covenant (R34, flagged in DIM-62-SD001 and SP-062-007):** Covenant names the bond structure; fellowship names the lived inner reality of participating within that bond. Covenant is contractual-covenantal; fellowship is experiential-participatory. **Gap:** These distinctions are inferential from the data pattern rather than from direct verse-level comparison. Session D boundary analysis (DIM-62-SD001) is required for precise distinction work. |
| OBS-062-T2-126 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Based on the available evidence: - **Fellowship vs. unity:** A distinction of *direction and process* — fellowship is the process of joining; unity is the state achieved. Not primarily a distinction of degree or kind. - **Fellowship vs. love:** A distinction of *constitutional level and function* — love is a motivating inner quality; fellowship is a relational structural reality. Different kinds. - **Fellowship vs. covenant:** A distinction of *analytical frame* — covenant names the bond's structure and terms; fellowship names the inner participatory experience within the bond. Not degree, but perspective. **Gap:** Confident determination of whether these are distinctions of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level requires comparison with the adjacent registries' full data. Formally deferred to Session D. |
| OBS-062-T2-127 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Yes. The shared anchor table (Section 6 of the data package) identifies six anchor verses also anchored in other registries: \| Anchor Verse \| Also anchored in \| \|---\|---\| \| Isa 47:9 \| strength/R187; sorcery/R150 \| \| Isa 53:5 \| sorrow/R151; contrition/R030 \| \| Jer 13:23 \| gladness/R186; power/R196; perverseness/R120 \| \| Hos 4:17 \| distress/R051 \| \| Phili 3:10 \| likeness/R209; deadness/R210 \| \| 2Pe 1:4 \| yielding/R180 \| |
| OBS-062-T2-128 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **Isa 53:5 (fellowship via wound) shared with sorrow/R151 and contrition/R030:** This shared anchor reveals that the redemptive wound-fellowship operates in the same verse space as sorrow and contrition. The verse names multiple inner-being realities simultaneously: the servant's bearing of transgressions, the peace produced, the healing from wounds. Fellowship-through-wounding is co-present with sorrow (the emotional register of the wound) and contrition (the inner-being response to guilt the wound addresses). SP-062-002 captures this. **Phili 3:10 (fellowship with Christ's sufferings) shared with likeness/R209 and deadness/R210:** This is analytically significant. Likeness and deadness are the two characteristics with which Phili 3:10's fellowship-with-sufferings is anchored. The verse names *becoming like him in his death* — the fellowship vocabulary (sharing) and the likeness vocabulary (becoming like) operate together. This shared anchor reveals that fellowship-through-suffering is structurally linked to the likeness/image-conformity register and the death/deadness register. SP-062-008 partially captures this. **2Pe 1:4 (divine nature fellowship) shared with yielding/R180:** Divine nature participation (the highest fellowship register) co-anchors with yielding. This may reveal that the inner disposition of yielding/surrender is the inner-being posture that correlates with receiving divine nature fellowship. SP-062-008 notes the connection to transformation cluster. **New finding:** The shared anchor pattern reveals that fellowship's most theologically dense verses (Isa 53:5, Phili 3:10, 2Pe 1:4) each anchor multiple other characteristics simultaneously. These verses are not merely fellowship-evidence but multi-characteristic junctions — places where fellowship, suffering, transformation, likeness, and hope converge. This makes them Session D priority sites for synthesis. |
| OBS-062-T2-129 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The registry header names six dimensions: Relational Disposition; Moral Character; Transformation; Divine-Human Correspondence; Emotion — Positive; Cognition. The programme's standard v1.4 dimension vocabulary does not include "Divine-Human Correspondence" — this appears to be a non-standard dimension label (noted at T1.8). The other five dimensions (Relational Disposition, Moral Character, Transformation, Emotion — Positive, Cognition) are programme-standard dimensions. Which other registries share these dimensions cannot be determined from the fellowship data package alone — it would require access to the dimension field values of all other active registries. The co-occurrence data provides indirect evidence: characteristics that share verses with fellowship likely share some dimensional territory. Unity (R167) almost certainly shares Relational Disposition; faith (R059) likely shares Transformation; sorrow (R151) shares Emotion (negative, not positive); love (R103) shares Relational Disposition and Moral Character. **Gap:** Full dimensional sharing analysis requires programme-wide dimension data not present in this package. |
| OBS-062-T2-130 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Without full dimensional data, this is partially answerable only: **Relational Disposition** is fellowship's primary dimension. Any characteristic sharing Relational Disposition occupies the same dimensional home — they are natural Session D synthesis partners. **Moral Character** is fellowship's secondary dimension. Characteristics sharing this dimension (and which co-occur with fellowship in the verse evidence) include unity, kindness (R099), justice (R098) — all of which appear in the co-occurrence table. The moral character dimension shared with kindness, justice, and related characteristics suggests that fellowship participates in the larger moral-character cluster alongside these characteristics. **Gap:** The full dimensional sharing picture remains incomplete without programme-wide access. |
| OBS-062-T2-131 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **Programme-wide dimensional sharing data is not available in this session.** The fellowship registry's own dimensions are known, but which other registries share each dimension cannot be determined from the fellowship data package alone. This is a data-access limitation, not an analytical gap — the data exists in the programme database but is not part of the fellowship data package. |
| OBS-062-T2-137 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Assembling the full vocabulary arc from act (cha.var — verb) through structural-joining (H4225 mach.be.ret — seam/joining-point), person-type (cha.ver, koinōnos — companion/participant), condition (koinōnia — fellowship state), wound-mark (chab.bu.rah — stripe/wound as fellowship-mechanism): **The arc reveals that fellowship spans from the structural-physical to the ontological:** At one end: physical tabernacle curtain joining (mach.be.ret, cho.ve.ret) — the most material, structural expression of the joining concept. At the other end: participation in divine nature (koinōnos in 2Pe 1:4) — the most ontological, transcendent expression of the same root concept. **New finding:** The full vocabulary arc of fellowship (chavar/chabbûrāh/koinōnia family) spans the complete range from physical structural joining to ontological divine participation — a semantic range that is probably unmatched in breadth among programme characteristics. The word family's range is not a sign of semantic weakness (multiple unrelated meanings) but of semantic depth (a single concept of *joining* that operates at every level of reality, from the coupling of tabernacle curtains to participation in divine nature). This semantic arc is itself a significant datum about how Scripture conceptualises the joining-participation phenomenon. |
| OBS-062-T2-138 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The registry carries two anchor verses for its largest group (873-001): Phili 3:10 and 1Jo 1:3. The anchor verse for the broadest definitional group is 1Jo 1:3 (as the verse most fully expressing the horizontal-vertical fellowship structure). **1Jo 1:3:** *that which we have seen and heard we proclaim also to you, so that you too may have fellowship with us; and indeed our fellowship is with the Father and with his Son Jesus Christ.* The term koinōnia (fellowship) functions *twice* in this verse: first as the telos of the proclamation (*so that you too may have fellowship with us*) and second as the constituting reality that grounds the proclamation (*our fellowship is with the Father and his Son*). The term functions as: 1. **The goal of the communicative act** (proclamation exists to create fellowship) 2. **The constituting ground of the capacity to extend it** (we can offer fellowship because ours is with God) The verse's argument structure: *we have seen and heard → we proclaim → you may have fellowship with us → because our fellowship is with the Father and Son*. Fellowship is both the purpose of proclamation and the ground of its possibility. |
| OBS-062-T2-139 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The fellowship evidence is carried across multiple literary forms: **Epistle (1Jo 1:3, 2Cor 6:14, Phili 3:10, 2Pe 1:4):** The majority of the koinōnia evidence is in epistolary form — letters written to specific communities addressing their particular circumstances. Responsible interpretation requires attention to the communal context: these are not abstract theological propositions but pastoral directives for actual communities. **Psalm (Psa 119:63, Psa 58:5):** Wisdom-devotional poetry. The companionship language of Psa 119:63 is part of an acrostic psalm of sustained devotion. Responsible interpretation attends to the cumulative, repetitive character of the psalm — it is not making a single logical claim but rehearsing sustained commitment. **Wisdom literature (Ecc 4:10, Ecc 9:4, Pro 20:30, Job 16:4):** Observational wisdom form. These verses are empirical observations about how life works, not direct theological propositions. Responsible interpretation recognises the observational character: *this is how it goes* rather than *this is the theological definition*. **Prophecy (Isa 53:5, Isa 1:23, Isa 47:9, Hos 4:17, Eze 37:19, Jer 13:23, Mal 2:14):** Prophetic address — often accusation, lament, or promise. Responsible interpretation attends to the prophetic *direction* of each verse (accusation, comfort, promise) and does not flatten them into neutral descriptive statements. **Narrative-argument (Mat 23:30):** Controversy dialogue. The Pharisees' claim is part of a complex rhetorical exchange. Responsible interpretation reads the claim in its polemical context. |
| OBS-062-T2-140 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **1Jo 1:3 argument:** - Premise 1: We have witnessed (seen and heard) - Premise 2: Our fellowship is with the Father and his Son - Conclusion: Therefore we proclaim to you so that you may have fellowship with us - Structural implication: Witness → proclamation → fellowship is both a temporal sequence and a logical ground-consequence structure **2Cor 6:14 argument:** - Premise: Righteousness and lawlessness are incompatible; light and darkness are incompatible (×4 pairs) - Conclusion: Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers - Structural implication: Moral incompatibility *at the ontological level* → relational incompatibility at the practical level **2Pe 1:4 argument:** - Premise: God has granted precious and great promises - Intermediate: Through these promises you may escape corruption (from sinful desire) - Conclusion: Through these promises you may become partakers of the divine nature - Structural implication: Divine promise → escape (negative condition removed) → participation (positive reality entered) **Ecc 9:4 argument:** - Premise: Being joined with the living is better than not being joined (even a dog better than a dead lion) - Conclusion: Being joined with the living has hope - Structural implication: Life-fellowship → hope; the argument moves from comparative observation to inner-being consequence |
| OBS-062-T2-141 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The fellowship evidence spans multiple contextual settings: **Covenantal:** Mal 2:14 (marriage covenant), 1Jo 1:3 (new covenant community), 873-001 (fellowship with Father and Son), Ecc 4:10 (covenantal partnership imagery). The covenantal setting reveals that fellowship's deepest operations are covenantal — they happen within the framework of binding relational commitments. **Communal:** Psa 119:63, 873-002 (horizontal fellowship among believers), Eze 37:19 (restoration of divided community). The communal setting reveals that fellowship is not a private inner experience but an inherently social reality. **Moral-accusatory:** Mat 23:30, Isa 1:23, Hos 4:17, Job 34:8. The moral-accusatory setting reveals that fellowship vocabulary is frequently deployed where moral culpability is at stake — who someone is joined to has legal and moral implications. **Suffering/redemptive:** Isa 53:5, Pro 20:30, Phili 3:10. The suffering-redemptive setting reveals that fellowship's deepest transformative operations occur in contexts of pain, wounding, and death — not in comfort. |
| OBS-062-T2-142 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **1Jo 1:3** functions as the primary anchor for fellowship's essential character: it encodes both the vertical axis (fellowship with the Father and his Son) and the horizontal axis (fellowship with us), the extension mechanism (proclamation), the ground of extension (prior witness), and the telos (that you too may have fellowship). It is the most structurally complete expression of what fellowship is. **2Pe 1:4** functions as the primary anchor for fellowship's ontological depth: divine nature participation as the highest register of what fellowship enables. No single verse captures all six modes of operation. If a single anchor must be named, **1Jo 1:3** is the most complete. |
| OBS-062-T2-143 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **1Jo 1:3** alone reveals the *chain structure* of fellowship: the verse encodes three levels simultaneously — the divine fellowship (with Father and Son), the apostolic fellowship (we who have seen and heard), and the extended fellowship (you who receive the proclamation). No other verse in the registry shows fellowship operating as a vertical-grounded chain that is extended horizontally through proclamation. The chain is: God → witnesses → community → proclamation → new participants → further proclamation. Fellowship's unique chain-structure — divine origin, witness transmission, communal participation, proclamatory extension — is encoded in 1Jo 1:3 alone. **New finding:** The chain-structure of fellowship (divine → apostolic → communal → extended) makes it structurally one of the most complex relational characteristics in the programme. It is not a dyadic (two-party) phenomenon but a multi-link chain in which each link both receives and extends. This chain-structure is implicit in the verse but not assembled as a finding in the existing data. |
| OBS-062-T2-144 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The data does not address human science frameworks for fellowship. Naming the most relevant frameworks is an inference from the characteristic's nature, not from the data. **Most relevant frameworks (by inference from the verse evidence):** **Social psychology — Attachment Theory (Bowlby, Ainsworth):** Fellowship's verse evidence emphasises the constitutional need for genuine relational joining, the consequences of isolation (Ecc 4:10), and the effects of wrong attachment (idolatrous joining). Attachment theory addresses the constitutional need for secure relational bonds and the inner-being consequences of insecure, avoidant, or disorganised attachment. The fit with fellowship's moral-relational spectrum is strong. **Social psychology — Social Identity Theory (Tajfel, Turner):** Fellowship's moral-alignment dimension (companionship with the fear-of-God community, moral complicity through fellowship with evildoers) maps onto social identity theory's account of how group membership shapes inner-being identity and moral self-assessment. **Developmental psychology — Formation through relationship:** The character-conformity finding (062-CHAR-001) aligns with developmental psychology's understanding that character is formed through relational participation — persons become like those they are relationally attached to. **Gap:** The data contains no Human Science framework analysis. All framework identification in this prompt is inference from the characteristic's structure, not from programme-conducted analysis. |
| OBS-062-T2-145 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **Attachment theory illumination:** The attachment framework makes the Ecc 4:10 evidence (*woe to him who is alone when he falls*) more coherent: isolation is not merely inconvenient but constitutionally harmful — the inner being requires relational attachment to function well. The verse's *woe* carries the weight of what attachment theory calls the consequence of insecure attachment. The framework also makes the Hos 4:17 pattern more coherent: idolatrous joining as a form of insecure attachment — the joining capacity seeks an attachment object, and when the right object (God) is unavailable or rejected, it attaches to wrong objects (idols). **Social identity theory illumination:** The moral complicity mode (Mat 23:30, Isa 1:23) is made more coherent by social identity theory: group membership shapes moral self-perception and can lead to in-group moral blindness. The Pharisees' claim (*we would not have done this*) is a classic social-identity defence of in-group identity against out-group guilt attribution. **Gap:** These framework applications are introductory inferences, not programme-conducted analysis. Further verse investigation and framework application is Session D territory. |
| OBS-062-T2-146 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **Key divergence — direction of attachment:** Attachment theory focuses on the constitutional need for secure attachment as a fundamental human drive. The verse evidence agrees that the joining-capacity is constitutional and fundamental, but adds a dimension attachment theory lacks: the joining capacity can be directed *vertically* toward God (2Pe 1:4, 873-001) in a mode that transcends human relational attachment. Attachment theory has no category for participation in divine nature — the highest attachment-object in the verse evidence is not another human being but God himself. **Key divergence — moral directionality:** Attachment theory treats attachment as morally neutral at the constitutional level (the need for attachment is constitutionally given). The verse evidence treats the *direction* of fellowship as constitutively moral — joining to wrong objects (idols, evildoers) is not merely a functional problem but a moral and spiritual failure. The verse evidence is not morally neutral about fellowship objects; attachment theory is. **New finding:** The divergence reveals that the biblical fellowship concept is constitutively *teleological* in a way attachment theory is not: it is not merely about meeting a constitutional need for connection but about orienting the joining capacity toward the right object (God, the fear-of-God community). The telos of fellowship is not satisfied by any secure attachment but only by the correctly directed one. This is a genuinely distinctive biblical insight not captured by the most relevant human science framework. |
| OBS-062-T2-147 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | **What attachment theory surfaces that the verse evidence has not yet addressed:** 1. **Developmental trajectory of the fellowship capacity:** When does the joining-capacity form? Is there evidence in Scripture of fellowship being shaped by early relational experience (analogous to early attachment formation)? The verse evidence does not address the developmental trajectory of the fellowship capacity from childhood. 2. **Repair after fellowship rupture:** Attachment theory addresses repair of the attachment bond after rupture. The verse evidence addresses the consequences of covenantal faithlessness (Mal 2:14) and idolatrous joining (Hos 4:17), but does not systematically address how a ruptured fellowship bond is repaired (beyond the redemptive wound-fellowship of Isa 53:5 which addresses a specific mechanism). Whether further verse investigation would address this is uncertain — it may properly belong to the forgiveness registry (R064) rather than fellowship. 3. **Fellowship anxiety:** Attachment theory identifies anxiety about attachment as a constitutional inner-being state. Whether Scripture names an anxiety about fellowship (fear of rejection from the fellowship community, for instance) is not addressed in the data. **Gap:** Further verse investigation on these questions would be productive if focused specifically on verses addressing fellowship formation, repair, and anxiety-states. However, given the NO_WORD_ANALYSIS constraint and the current programme stage, these are Session D questions rather than immediate programme tasks. |
| OBS-062-T2-150 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Ecc 9:4 | Fellowship as created purpose is partially evidenced. Ecc 9:4 places fellowship-vocabulary at the most basic existential level — "whoever is joined (H2266) with all the living has hope." Fellowship with the living is the created condition of existence itself, not a post-fall addition. The tabernacle joining vocabulary (H4225, H4226 — set aside as physical-only) suggests that structural joining is part of created design at the material level. Whether fellowship as sharing-in-God's-nature was part of the original created design is not explicitly evidenced from this registry's anchor verses. OBS-062-045, OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-156 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | R062 fellowship | Isa 53:5 | Yes — the wound vocabulary (H2250) in Isa 53:5 deploys fellowship vocabulary typologically. The Servant's wounds (chavarburotav — from the chavar root family) are the mechanism through which healing is produced in the community. The fellowship vocabulary here is not social companionship but wounding-as-joining: the Servant's wounding joins the consequences of sin to himself on behalf of others. OBS-062-032, OBS-062-022. |
| OBS-062-T2-157 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | R062 fellowship | Isa 53:5 | The typological direction moves from physical wound (joining force to body, leaving a mark) to redemptive wound (the Servant's body joined to the consequences of human sin, producing healing). The physical logic of wound (a joining that persists as a mark) is deployed in its fullest theological expression at Isa 53:5. OBS-062-006, OBS-062-032. |
| OBS-062-T2-159 | ETYMOLOGY | R062 fellowship |  | The chavar family (H2266–H2279): root meaning of physical joining/binding — a wound joins force to body; a seam joins curtain to curtain; a companion joins life to life. The underlying logic is being-bound-to-something such that the bond is real and consequential. The koinōn family: from koinos (common) — koinōnia is the state of having-in-common; what is shared defines the fellowship. Together: Hebrew emphasises the binding/joining act; Greek emphasises the common object shared. Both point to genuine participation as the definitional core. OBS-062-005, OBS-062-007, OBS-062-017. |
| OBS-062-T2-160 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Jer 13:23, Hos 4:17 | All three. Directional: fellowship moves toward its object — the person joins themselves to (Hithpael reflexive) or is joined to. Relational: the characteristic is constitutively relational — it requires at minimum two parties. Constitutional: habitual fellowship constitutes the person (Jer 13:23 — fixed moral character from habitual alignment; Hos 4:17 — being "joined to idols" as a fixed spiritual condition). OBS-062-007, OBS-062-042, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-162 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship has constituent elements: (1) an object (what or who is fellowshipped with — God, persons, wickedness, idols); (2) a mode of joining (volitional alignment, existential given, divine initiative); (3) a quality-transfer mechanism (the person participates in what the object is and is formed by it); (4) a duration dimension (momentary companionship → habitual alignment → constitutional formation). OBS-062-019, OBS-062-048, OBS-062-058. |
| OBS-062-T2-166 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship ends and something else begins at the point where the object is wrong (inverted fellowship → moral corruption, spiritual bondage — Hos 4:17, Isa 1:23) or the participation is superficial (mere co-presence rather than genuine sharing-in — 2Cor 6:14). The precise boundary between fellowship and companionship, or fellowship and solidarity, is not drawn from this registry's data. OBS-062-042, OBS-062-049. |
| OBS-062-T2-168 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Yes — the mode determines both the constitutional level and the inner-being outcome. Existential mode: soul/life level — Ecc 9:4. Companionship mode: character-formation level — Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23. Moral complicity mode: moral-evaluative and volitional levels — Mat 23:30. Transformative suffering mode: somatic and spirit levels — Phili 3:10, Isa 53:5, Pro 20:30. Ontological mode: spirit-soul constitutional level — 2Pe 1:4. OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-170 | VERSE_PATTERN | R062 fellowship | Ecc 9:4 | The most immediate named inner response is hope — Ecc 9:4: "whoever is joined with all the living has hope." Fellowship, even in its minimal existential form, immediately produces hope as an inner-being state. SP-062-005 captures this as a structural question. OBS-062-045, OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-171 | VERSE_PATTERN | R062 fellowship |  | Varies by mode: Existential mode — hope (Ecc 9:4). Companionship mode — character formation (righteous or corrupt, depending on object). Moral complicity mode — shared moral consequence (Mat 23:30). Transformative suffering mode — comfort proportionate to suffering (2Cor 1:7). Ontological mode — participation in divine nature (2Pe 1:4). OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-172 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Jer 13:23, Psa 119:63, Isa 1:23 | Sustained fellowship produces constitutive character formation — Jer 13:23 names the endpoint: moral character as fixed as leopard spots, immune to self-reversal. The sustained effect is not merely relational (continued connection to an object) but constitutional (the object's character is transferred into the person). Habitual companionship with God-fearers produces righteous character (Psa 119:63); habitual companionship with thieves produces corrupt character (Isa 1:23). OBS-062-052, OBS-062-048. |
| OBS-062-T2-174 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Yes. Immediate response is hope (Ecc 9:4) or comfort (2Cor 1:7) or suffering-participation (Phili 3:10). Sustained effect is constitutive character formation aligned with the fellowship object. The gap between immediate response and sustained effect is the entire character-formation arc. OBS-062-045, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-175 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The enabling inner condition for genuine fellowship is desire oriented toward the fellowship object (SP-062-001 — desire as the inner motivating state directing fellowship). Psa 119:63 is a declared volitional alignment; Phili 3:10 is an aspirational pursuit. The inner condition enabling genuine fellowship is a will oriented toward the object — not mere proximity, not external compliance, but genuine desire-directed alignment. OBS-062-028, OBS-062-048, OBS-062-055. |
| OBS-062-T2-177 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Hos 4:17 | The person who encounters fellowship with God but does not receive it is in the condition of Hos 4:17 — already joined to an alternative object. The inner-being state is one of misdirected participation: the fellowship-capacity is active but aimed at the wrong object. Spiritual bondage results (Ephraim "joined to idols" — unable to break the joining). OBS-062-042. |
| OBS-062-T2-178 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Primary dimension: Relational Disposition — confirmed. Fellowship is fundamentally about orientation and participation in relation. The person is oriented-toward and joined-to. OBS-062-001, OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-180 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Secondary dimensions: (1) Moral Character — the character-formation mode (Jer 13:23, Isa 1:23, Psa 119:63 where habitual fellowship fixes character); (2) Transformation — Phili 3:10, 2Pe 1:4, Pro 20:30 (fellowship producing inner-being change); (3) Vitality/Existence — Ecc 9:4 (existential fellowship as prerequisite for hope); (4) Divine-Human Correspondence — Isa 53:5, 2Pe 1:4 (fellowship across the divine-human boundary). OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-184 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R062 fellowship |  | The verse evidence is largely silent on explicit spirit-faculty naming as the locus of human fellowship. The spirit of God mediates fellowship (2Cor 13:14) but the human spirit as the site of fellowship reception is not explicitly named. Gap noted. |
| OBS-062-T2-185 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R062 fellowship | Ecc 9:4 | The soul (*nephesh*) is implicated in Ecc 9:4 ("whoever is joined with all the living") — the fellowship of being-among-the-living is a soul-level reality: the nephesh as the living self participating in the community of the living. OBS-062-045. |
| OBS-062-T2-186 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R062 fellowship | Ecc 9:4 | Soul-level fellowship (existential mode — Ecc 9:4) reveals fellowship as a condition of the living self — not primarily an act or choice but a given of creaturely existence. The soul that is alive is by that fact in fellowship with the living. This is fellowship at its most basic constitutional level. OBS-062-045. |
| OBS-062-T2-187 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R062 fellowship | Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23 | Yes — the heart is implicated in the companionship and character-formation modes. Psa 119:63 ("I am a companion of all who fear you") is a heart-declaration: a settled orientation of the whole inner being. Jer 13:23 names fixed moral character (heart-level constitutional formation) as the product of habitual fellowship. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-188 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R062 fellowship | Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23 | Heart-location reveals fellowship as a moral-evaluative act as well as a relational one — choosing companions (Psa 119:63) is a heart-level decision that has moral character consequences (Jer 13:23). The heart that consistently fellowships with the righteous is itself formed righteous; the heart that fellowships with the corrupt is itself formed corrupt. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-190 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship likely requires cognitive discernment of the fellowship object — 2Cor 6:14 implies the mind can assess compatibility of fellowship objects. Mis-directed fellowship (Hos 4:17) may involve cognitive failure — inability to recognise the nature of the idol being joined to. Gap noted. OBS-062-049, OBS-062-042. |
| OBS-062-T2-191 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R062 fellowship |  | Verse evidence is largely silent on explicit mind-location. The cognitive dimension is implied (discernment of fellowship object) but not named. Gap noted. |
| OBS-062-T2-194 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | R062 fellowship | Pro 20:30, Isa 53:5 | Yes — wounds and stripes are the somatic fellowship vocabulary. H2250 (wound/bruise — from the chavar root) links somatic wounding to the fellowship vocabulary. Pro 20:30 ("strokes make clean the innermost parts") and Isa 53:5 ("by his wounds we are healed") deploy somatic wound-vocabulary in inner-being registers. OBS-062-039, OBS-062-032. |
| OBS-062-T2-195 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | R062 fellowship | Pro 20:30, Isa 53:5 | The wound-body link does two things: (1) It grounds the fellowship-through-suffering mode in somatic reality — the suffering that enables fellowship in Christ (Phili 3:10) is real bodily suffering; (2) It links external physical event (wound) to internal moral/spiritual effect — Pro 20:30 "strokes make clean the innermost parts" establishes the physical-to-inner transformative direction. OBS-062-039, OBS-062-040. |
| OBS-062-T2-196 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | R062 fellowship | Pro 20:30, Isa 53:5 | Body-to-inner: Pro 20:30 — strokes (somatic) → cleansing of innermost parts (inner). Isa 53:5 — Servant's wounds (somatic) → healing in the community (inner-being). The direction runs from external physical event inward to inner-being transformation. OBS-062-039, OBS-062-032. |
| OBS-062-T2-199 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R062 fellowship |  | The wound as a persisting mark (H2250 — its physical logic) supports a body-deposit reading for the suffering-fellowship mode. Against: the inner-being outcomes (cleansing of innermost parts, healing) are internal, not somatic residue. The evidence is ambiguous. Gap noted. |
| OBS-062-T2-200 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R062 fellowship |  | The evidence is partially silent — the wound-mark persists physically, but whether this constitutes a programme-relevant constitutional body deposit is not established. Recording as gap. T5.7 to be addressed at the level of the evidence available. |
| OBS-062-T2-202 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Multiple — three distinct origin points as evidenced in Q&A-062: divine initiative, human volitional alignment, existential given. The origin differs across the five modes. OBS-062-037. |
| OBS-062-T2-203 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Yes — origin shifts across modes: divine → human → existential, corresponding to the ontological → companionship → existential mode levels. OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-205 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R062 fellowship |  | The sequence runs: external joining/encounter → inner reception and formation → constitutional character fixed. For divine nature participation: escape from corruption (outer) → participation in divine promises (relational) → sharing in what God is (constitutional). For disciplinary fellowship: physical strokes (somatic) → inner cleansing (constitutional). OBS-062-039, OBS-062-058. |
| OBS-062-T2-208 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The pattern reveals perception as a prerequisite for rightly directed fellowship and a casualty of misdirected fellowship. The person who fellowships well must first perceive well; the person whose fellowship is misdirected is progressively less able to perceive the error. OBS-062-042, OBS-062-049. |
| OBS-062-T2-210 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Rightly directed fellowship deepens cognition — fellowship with God-fearers (Psa 119:63) aligns the person with the community of those who fear God, deepening their own capacity for accurate moral-cognitive assessment. Wrong fellowship progressively impairs cognition — the corruption that comes from habitual wrong-alignment (Jer 13:23, Isa 1:23) is in part a cognitive impairment (the corrupt person cannot see their own corruption as Jer 13:23 implies). OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-211 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The pattern reveals cognition as both prerequisite for and product of fellowship direction — initial cognitive discernment enables right fellowship; habitual right fellowship deepens cognitive capacity. The reverse is also true: initial cognitive failure enables wrong fellowship; habitual wrong fellowship deepens cognitive impairment. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-213 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship may deepen or fix memory — habitual fellowship creates the fixed character of Jer 13:23, which may include a constitutive memory dimension (the person who has been habitually aligned cannot simply forget their alignment). Gap: the memory faculty as a named engagement of fellowship is not evidenced. OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-214 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Verse evidence is largely silent on explicit memory faculty engagement with fellowship. Gap noted. |
| OBS-062-T2-217 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship is constitutively affective — it is not a purely cognitive or volitional alignment but a genuine sharing in the affective reality of the fellowship object. The pattern reveals that genuine fellowship produces genuine shared affect: no shallow affective engagement is possible in true fellowship. OBS-062-031, OBS-062-055. |
| OBS-062-T2-219 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23 | Fellowship directs and eventually fixes volition. Initial volitional alignment toward an object (Psa 119:63 declared companionship; Phili 3:10 aspirational pursuit) produces habitual alignment that over time becomes constitutional (Jer 13:23 — fixed character). The volitional act of self-joining (Hithpael) is the beginning; constitutional fixing is the endpoint. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052, OBS-062-055. |
| OBS-062-T2-220 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Jer 13:23 | Volition is both the initiating act of fellowship and its progressive captive — the will that begins by choosing companions is eventually formed by them such that alternative choices become progressively harder (Jer 13:23 — "can the leopard change its spots?"). OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-222 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Jer 13:23 | Fellowship initially engages agency and then — in the constitutional mode — transforms it. Habitual fellowship with God-fearers produces a person who naturally orients toward the righteous (agency reshaped). Habitual wrong-fellowship produces a person who can no longer choose differently (agency progressively constrained by constitutional formation — Jer 13:23). OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-223 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Agency is progressively shaped and eventually fixed by fellowship choices. The pattern reveals that fellowship is an agency-shaping characteristic — the initial choice of companions is made freely, but over time the fellowship object shapes the will until alternative choices are genuinely diminished. OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-225 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship both requires and shapes moral evaluation. Initial rightly directed fellowship requires accurate moral evaluation of the fellowship object (2Cor 6:14). Sustained fellowship then shapes the person's moral evaluative capacity — right fellowship deepens it; wrong fellowship degrades it (Jer 13:23 — the corrupt cannot perceive their own corruption). OBS-062-049, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-226 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Moral evaluation and fellowship direction are mutually determining: accurate moral evaluation directs fellowship rightly; right fellowship deepens moral evaluation. The pattern reveals a positive spiral (right fellowship → better moral vision → better fellowship) and a negative spiral (wrong fellowship → degraded moral vision → worse fellowship). OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-228 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Right fellowship deepens conscience — fellowship with God-fearers produces a community of moral discernment (Psa 119:63). Wrong fellowship degrades conscience — habitual moral complicity (Mat 23:30 — the inherited blood-guilt pattern) gradually mutes the conscience's capacity to recognise the wrongness of the fellowship. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-059. |
| OBS-062-T2-229 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Conscience is the moral sensitivity that enables the initial distinction between right and wrong fellowship objects; fellowship then either deepens or degrades this sensitivity. The pattern reveals conscience as both the gatekeeper and the casualty of fellowship direction. OBS-062-049, OBS-062-059. |
| OBS-062-T2-230 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Psa 119:63 | Yes — Psa 119:63 ("I am a companion of all who fear you, of those who keep your precepts") is the paradigm conscientiousness statement within fellowship: an integrated moral alignment (those who fear God and keep his precepts) expressed as a stable relational posture. OBS-062-048. |
| OBS-062-T2-231 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Right fellowship deepens conscientiousness by embedding the person in a community of moral seriousness (Psa 119:63). Wrong fellowship degrades conscientiousness — the moral complicity of Mat 23:30 and the idolatrous bonding of Hos 4:17 both describe a progressively less conscientious inner-being state. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-042, OBS-062-059. |
| OBS-062-T2-232 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship is a primary vehicle of conscientiousness formation — the community one fellowships with shapes the moral seriousness and integrated moral response of the person. Right community → deepened conscientiousness; wrong community → degraded conscientiousness. OBS-062-048. |
| OBS-062-T2-235 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Relational capacity is both the vehicle and the product of fellowship. The pattern reveals: fellowship requires relational capacity to begin; it deepens that capacity through exercise; sustained fellowship with the right objects produces the deepest possible relational capacity (divine nature participation). OBS-062-058, OBS-062-056. |
| OBS-062-T2-243 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Ecc 4:10 | Inner conditions enabling genuine extension of fellowship: sustained desire orientation toward the person (SP-062-001); genuine participation in their reality (not mere proximity — 2Cor 6:14). The Ecc 4:10 friend who lifts has genuinely participated in the fallen person's condition enough to be present to help. OBS-062-050. |
| OBS-062-T2-246 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Inner conditions enabling reception: genuine need (Ecc 4:10 — the fallen person who cannot rise alone); openness to the participatory reality of the fellowship object (2Pe 1:4 — escape from corruption as the enabling condition). OBS-062-050, OBS-062-058. |
| OBS-062-T2-247 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Hos 4:17 | Hos 4:17 presents the paradigm case: Ephraim, already joined to idols, cannot receive the call to different fellowship ("let him alone"). The inner state is one of prior competing fellowship that blocks reception of the right fellowship. Misdirected fellowship prevents reception of rightly-directed fellowship. OBS-062-042. |
| OBS-062-T2-248 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Mal 2:14 | Yes — covenantal context makes a clear difference. Mal 2:14 grounds companionship fellowship explicitly in covenant (berith): "she is your companion and your wife by covenant." The covenantal bond defines the relational scope of the fellowship. SP-062-007 captures the fellowship-covenant structural question. OBS-062-054. |
| OBS-062-T2-250 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Scope: (1) Existential — all the living (Ecc 9:4); (2) Companionship — those who fear God (Psa 119:63); (3) Covenantal — the covenant partner (Mal 2:14); (4) Ecclesial — the fellowship of believers (1Jo 1:3, 2Cor 1:7); (5) Ontological — those who "received a faith of equal standing" (2Pe 1:4). Scope narrows as the depth of fellowship increases. OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-251 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Psa 58:5, Isa 47:9 | The occult-charming groups (H2267 — spell/charmer; Psa 58:5, Isa 47:9) involve the use of fellowship vocabulary in relation to non-human spiritual forces — charms are a form of joining with occult power. This is the nearest evidence of fellowship operating in relation to spiritual beings. OBS-062-024, OBS-062-035. |
| OBS-062-T2-252 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Hos 4:17 | Hos 4:17 ("Ephraim is joined to idols") may involve spiritual-beings-level adversarial activity — idolatrous joining may involve binding to spiritual forces behind idols. This is inferential from the verse evidence and not named explicitly. OBS-062-042. |
| OBS-062-T2-255 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Jer 13:23 | Constitutional formation from habitual fellowship is presented as difficult to reverse — Jer 13:23 ("can the leopard change its spots?") implies that deep formation becomes resistant to change. Whether this is fully irreversible is not stated, but the formative direction is resistant to simple volitional reversal. OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-257 | VERSE_PATTERN | R062 fellowship |  | The sequence reveals that fellowship works through accumulation: each act of fellowship deposits into the person's character, and over time the deposits become constitutive. The formation sequence is not dramatic-and-instantaneous but gradual-and-cumulative. The endpoint (Jer 13:23 — fixed character) is reached through many acts of habitual fellowship, not a single decision. OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-258 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Jer 13:23, Pro 20:30 | Two distinct mechanisms: (1) Participatory transfer — the person participates in what the fellowship object is, and through sustained participation, is formed into conformity with it (Psa 119:63 → Jer 13:23 line; 2Pe 1:4). (2) Wound-as-cleansing — external physical event (Pro 20:30 — strokes) producing internal purification (cleaning of innermost parts). Mechanism 1 operates through habitual relational alignment; Mechanism 2 operates through somatic event with inner-being consequence. OBS-062-019, OBS-062-039. |
| OBS-062-T2-259 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Yes — the companionship/habituation mechanism (Mechanism 1) and the wound-as-cleansing mechanism (Mechanism 2) are distinct. Mechanism 1 is gradual and volitional; Mechanism 2 is sudden and somatic. Both produce inner-being transformation but through different pathways. OBS-062-039, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-261 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Suffering deepens fellowship — Phili 3:10 makes suffering-participation the path to knowing Christ most fully. Suffering reveals fellowship — 2Cor 1:7 shows that the depth of shared suffering determines the depth of shared comfort (the fellowship is exposed and measured by the depth of sharing). Suffering produces inner fellowship-through-cleansing — Pro 20:30 (strokes → inner purification). OBS-062-055, OBS-062-031, OBS-062-039. |
| OBS-062-T2-263 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship is the primary formation vehicle in this registry — character is formed not through solitary disciplines but through participatory community alignment. The person becomes what they habitually fellowship with. Fellowship functions as the relational mechanism of sanctification. OBS-062-019, OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-267 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Generational consequence: not evidenced from this registry's anchor verses. The fellowship formations described are personal and corporate, not explicitly generational. Gap noted. OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-268 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | T2.8 found ambiguous evidence — the wound mark is a physical persisting trace but whether it constitutes a programme-relevant constitutional deposit is uncertain. T5.7 remains partially open pending Session D cross-registry analysis (SP-062-002, SP-062-004 relate to this question). Gap noted; not formally closed. |
| OBS-062-T2-269 | CROSS_REGISTRY | R062 fellowship |  | From v1 obslog Section 5 — strongest signals: R43 desire (6 shared verses — highest co-occurrence); R167 unity (3 shared verses); R184 spirit (3 shared verses); R192 comfort (3 shared verses). Secondary: R30 contrition and R151 sorrow (shared anchor Isa 53:5); R180 yielding (shared anchor 2Pe 1:4); R34 covenant (shared anchor Mal 2:14). OBS-062-028 through OBS-062-035. |
| OBS-062-T2-270 | CROSS_REGISTRY | R062 fellowship |  | The co-occurrence pattern reveals fellowship as a relational-formative hub — it consistently appears with desire (the motivating state directing it), unity (its corporate outcome), spirit (its mediating agent), and comfort (its affective product through suffering). Fellowship does not stand alone but is always embedded in this surrounding network of inner-being realities. OBS-062-028 through OBS-062-035. |
| OBS-062-T2-271 | VERSE_PATTERN | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship consistently precedes character formation (Psa 119:63 → Jer 13:23 sequence).
