It follows desire-orientation (desire directs the fellowship). In 2Pe 1:4, it follows escape from corruption and is the culminating participatory state. Fellowship precedes unity (the fellowship-act produces unity as its corporate outcome). OBS-062-028, OBS-062-052, OBS-062-058. |
| OBS-062-T2-272 | VERSE_PATTERN | R062 fellowship |  | The fellowship → character formation sequence is causal (habitual fellowship causes character to form in conformity with the fellowship object). The desire → fellowship sequence is directional (desire directs where fellowship forms, not merely correlational). OBS-062-028, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-273 | CROSS_REGISTRY | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship produces: (1) hope (Ecc 9:4); (2) fixed moral character (Jer 13:23); (3) shared comfort (2Cor 1:7); (4) inner cleansing (Pro 20:30); (5) divine nature participation (2Pe 1:4); (6) unity (Eze 37:19). OBS-062-045, OBS-062-052, OBS-062-031, OBS-062-039, OBS-062-058. |
| OBS-062-T2-274 | CROSS_REGISTRY | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship is produced by: desire (R43 — the inner motivating state directing it); divine initiative (God joins — Eze 37:19); volitional self-joining (human Hithpael act). OBS-062-028, OBS-062-051, OBS-062-007. |
| OBS-062-T2-275 | CROSS_REGISTRY | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship is constituent of: unity (R167 — fellowship is the participatory inner reality of which unity is the corporate expression); sanctification (fellowship with God-fearers is the relational mechanism of character formation); divine nature participation (2Pe 1:4 — fellowship is the mode of ontological transformation). OBS-062-029, OBS-062-058. |
| OBS-062-T2-277 | ETYMOLOGY | R062 fellowship |  | The chavar root family's root-level architecture spans wound (H2250) through companionship (H2270) — both derived from the same joining logic. The koinōn root connects to sunkoinōnos (co-sharer) and koinōnikos (generous). Root-level architecture reveals that fellowship's vocabulary encodes both its object range (anything joinable-with) and its inner-being depth (the wound is a joining that penetrates). OBS-062-005, OBS-062-007, OBS-062-017. |
| OBS-062-T2-278 | ETYMOLOGY | R062 fellowship |  | Vocabulary sharing with wound/suffering registries (H2250 shared root with H2266) reveals that fellowship and suffering are structurally connected at the lexical level — both are joining events. The wound-as-fellowship vocabulary reveals that the deepest fellowship is through participatory suffering. OBS-062-006, OBS-062-007. |
| OBS-062-T2-279 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Eze 37:19 | R167 unity is the closest adjacent characteristic — unity is the corporate/structural outcome of which fellowship is the inner participatory reality. The distinction: fellowship names the inner act and state of sharing-in; unity names the resultant oneness of those who fellowship. Eze 37:19 deploys fellowship vocabulary to achieve unity as the explicit purpose. OBS-062-029, OBS-062-051. |
| OBS-062-T2-280 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship vs unity: kind distinction — fellowship is inner participatory reality; unity is the structural outcome. Fellowship vs covenant (R34): layer distinction — covenant is the legal/structural bond; fellowship is the inner relational experience of that bond (SP-062-007). Fellowship vs desire: sequential distinction — desire directs fellowship; they are not the same act. OBS-062-054, OBS-062-028. |
| OBS-062-T2-281 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Fellowship vs unity: kind distinction. Fellowship vs covenant: layer distinction (legal vs experiential). Fellowship vs desire: sequential/directional distinction (preceding vs motivating). OBS-062-028, OBS-062-029, OBS-062-054. |
| OBS-062-T2-283 | CROSS_REGISTRY | R062 fellowship |  | 2Pe 1:4 shared with R180 yielding reveals that divine nature participation requires an inner act of yielding (escape from corruption) as its prerequisite — fellowship and yielding are structurally adjacent at the point of ontological transformation. Phili 3:10 shared with R209 and R210 reveals that fellowship-through-suffering is simultaneously a conformity event (becoming like Christ in death) — fellowship is the relational mode; conformity is the constitutional outcome. OBS-062-033, OBS-062-055. |
| OBS-062-T2-284 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Primary dimension (Relational Disposition) shared with love, mercy, compassion, and other relational-disposition registries. Secondary dimensions: Moral Character (shared with virtue/character registries), Transformation (shared with sanctification cluster), Vitality/Existence (unique to existential-mode anchor Ecc 9:4), Divine-Human Correspondence (shared with C17 cluster words). OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-285 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The Relational Disposition primary dimension shared with love and mercy positions fellowship as one of the relational triad (love, mercy, fellowship) — love is the disposition-toward; mercy is the disposition-toward the needy; fellowship is the participatory-joining that sustains the relationship. All three operate in the same relational space but at different aspects. OBS-062-001. |
| OBS-062-T2-289 | ETYMOLOGY | R062 fellowship |  | Yes — the vocabulary distinguishes: Disposition (Psa 119:63 declared companionship — settled orientation) vs act (Phili 3:10 pursuit — deliberate agentive movement). Received (Pual — Ephraim is joined; God joins — Eze 37:19) vs given/initiated (Hithpael — self-joining; Hiphil — God joins actively). Condition (Jer 13:23 — fixed constitutional state) vs quality (Psa 119:63 — stable relational orientation). OBS-062-007, OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-294 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | The vocabulary arc reveals a characteristic whose physical root logic (joining/binding) spans from material seams and wounds through human companionship through occult binding through ontological divine-nature participation — all unified by the single logic of genuine participation-in. The arc is the widest of any characteristic in the programme: from the tabernacle seam to divine nature participation, from wound to koinōnia. This breadth is not random but reflects the universality of the joining-logic: anything can be joined-to, and the nature of the joining determines everything. OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-296 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | R062 fellowship |  | Five literary forms carry the primary verse evidence: (1) Wisdom poetry — Psa 119:63, Ecc 4:10, Ecc 9:4, Pro 20:30; (2) Prophecy — Isa 53:5, Isa 47:9, Hos 4:17, Jer 13:23, Eze 37:19; (3) Apostolic letter — Phili 3:10, 2Cor 1:7, 2Cor 6:14, 2Pe 1:4; (4) Gospel/narrative — Mat 23:30; (5) Epistle — 1Jo 1:3, Mal 2:14. Each form shapes interpretation — wisdom poetry presents fellowship as existential and formative; prophecy presents it as diagnostic (right or wrong alignment); epistolary presents it as the goal and ground of Christian life. OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-298 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | R062 fellowship |  | Settings: Wisdom (existential-formative), Prophetic (diagnostic-corrective), Covenantal (Mal 2:14 — marriage and faithlessness), Apostolic (theological-pastoral — the fullest theological development of koinōnia). The apostolic setting carries the most developed theological account; the wisdom setting carries the most universal anthropological account. OBS-062-019. |
| OBS-062-T2-301 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Two frameworks: (1) Social psychology of group formation — habitual group membership shapes individual identity and moral character (consistent with Psa 119:63 → Jer 13:23 formation arc). (2) Participation metaphysics (patristic and philosophical) — "participation in divine nature" as the Platonic-theological concept of methexis, now deployed in biblical frame. Framework identification only; not introduced as analysis. OBS-062-019, OBS-062-058. |
| OBS-062-T2-302 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship |  | Social psychology confirms that group membership is the primary mechanism of character formation — not solitary disciplines but community participation shapes the person. This is precisely what the fellowship-to-formation arc (Psa 119:63 → Jer 13:23) demonstrates. OBS-062-048. |
| OBS-062-T2-304 | OBSERVATION | R062 fellowship | Jer 13:23, Hos 4:17 | Social psychology surfaces the question of fellowship's role in moral formation when the fellowship community itself is corrupted — the echo chamber dynamic, where wrong fellowship degrades the very faculties needed to recognise the error (consistent with Jer 13:23, Hos 4:17). This dimension is present in the biblical data but not named as systematically as human science frameworks would. OBS-062-042, OBS-062-052. |
| SYN-INTRA-062-001 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T1 establishes fellowship as a structurally complex characteristic whose definitional core (genuine participation-in, determined by its object) is expressed through five distinct modes and whose sustained operation produces constitutional character formation. The definition tier's key insight: fellowship is formally unified by the object-determines-character logic but materially diverse across five modes (existential, companionship, moral complicity, transformative suffering, ontological). The structural negative (misdirected fellowship) is more dangerous than absence of fellowship — Hos 4:17 and Mat 23:30 present the active wrong-direction as the primary threat, not non-participation. The definitional complexity of fellowship explains why the single English word is analytically inadequate: it covers a range from minimal existential participation (Ecc 9:4) to maximal ontological transformation (2Pe 1:4) under the same label. |
| SYN-INTRA-062-002 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T2 reveals fellowship's constitutional location is distributed across levels according to its mode: existential mode (soul/nephesh level — Ecc 9:4), companionship mode (heart level — Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23), desire-driven mode (desire-faculty — 2Pe 1:4), suffering mode (somatic — Pro 20:30, Isa 53:5). The deepest mode (ontological — 2Pe 1:4) implies spirit-level but this is inferential rather than explicit. T2's most significant finding is the desire-faculty as the constitutional engine directing fellowship: desire determines the fellowship object, and the fellowship object determines the constitutional formation. The body-deposit question (T2.8) remains open — the wound-mark as a persisting joining-trace is the nearest evidence, but its programme-relevance is uncertain. |
| SYN-INTRA-062-003 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | Reading T3 as a whole reveals that every faculty engagement with fellowship runs in both directions — right fellowship deepens and enables; wrong fellowship degrades and constrains. Perception, cognition, moral evaluation, conscience, conscientiousness, and relational capacity are all deepened by rightly directed fellowship and progressively impaired by wrongly directed fellowship. The single exception is creativity (T3.5 — data silent). The pattern is systematic: fellowship is not merely a relational characteristic but a faculty-shaping characteristic — it progressively shapes every inner faculty in conformity with its object. This makes the choice of fellowship objects one of the most consequential inner-being decisions a person makes: the consequences extend through every faculty across time. |
| SYN-INTRA-062-004 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T4 reveals fellowship as the most relationally comprehensive characteristic in the registry: it operates in all four primary directions (God-to-human, human-to-God, human-giving, human-receiving) and partially in the spiritual-beings direction. The key T4 synthesis: 1Jo 1:3's structural claim — horizontal fellowship is derived from and grounded in vertical fellowship — means the relational architecture of fellowship is hierarchical: the divine-human fellowship is primary; human-human fellowship is its extension and expression. The scope analysis (Q&A-119) reveals that fellowship scope narrows as its depth increases: all-the-living (widest) → God-fearers → covenant partner → ecclesial body → divine nature participants (deepest-narrowest). Depth and breadth are inversely proportional in fellowship's relational structure. |
| SYN-INTRA-062-005 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T5 establishes fellowship as the primary relational mechanism of sanctification in this registry — character is formed not through solitary disciplines but through participatory community alignment. The formation sequence (volitional alignment → habitual participation → constitutional fixing) runs from free choice to constrained character, meaning the initial choice of fellowship objects is made at maximum freedom and progressively constrains freedom as character forms. Two distinct formative mechanisms operate: participatory transfer (habituation producing conformity — gradual) and wound-as-cleansing (somatic event producing inner purification — acute). The eschatological trajectory (T5.6) is explicit in 2Pe 1:4 — fellowship is oriented toward divine nature participation as its telos. |
| SYN-INTRA-062-006 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T6 positions fellowship as a structurally central hub characteristic — it connects desire (R43 — strongest co-occurrence signal), unity (R167 — corporate outcome), spirit (R184 — mediating agent), comfort (R192 — affective product of shared suffering), contrition and sorrow (R30, R151 — shared anchor Isa 53:5), yielding (R180 — shared anchor 2Pe 1:4), covenant (R34 — shared anchor Mal 2:14). Fellowship is structurally upstream of unity (fellowship is the participatory inner reality; unity is its corporate structural expression) and downstream of desire (desire directs fellowship). The vocabulary analysis (T6.4) reveals the root-level connection between wound and companionship (both from chavar) as the deepest structural finding: fellowship and suffering-wounding share the same etymological root, revealing that the deepest fellowship is structurally connected to the deepest wounding. |
| SYN-INTRA-062-007 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T7 reveals that the fellowship vocabulary has the widest semantic arc of any characteristic in the programme: from tabernacle seam (physical construction) through wound (somatic joining) through companionship (relational) through moral complicity (evaluative) through ontological divine nature participation. This breadth is encoded in the root-level architecture (chavar — the joining logic applied universally). The two primary anchor verses (2Pe 1:4 and 1Jo 1:3) are complementary in perspective: 2Pe 1:4 is the vertical-ontological statement (human participating in divine nature); 1Jo 1:3 is the relational-structural statement (horizontal derived from vertical). The LSJ gap (T7.1.8) is an acknowledged data limitation — the classical background of koinōnia is unavailable from this registry's data, which means the NT's transformation of a Hellenistic civic-philosophical term into a theological term cannot be traced from this evidence base. |
| SYN-INTER-062-008 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T1's five-mode structure maps precisely onto T2's constitutional levels: existential mode (soul/nephesh), companionship mode (heart), desire-driven mode (desire-faculty), suffering mode (somatic), ontological mode (spirit — inferential). Definition and constitutional location are co-determining: the mode of fellowship determines the constitutional level at which it operates. The T1 definition (object determines character) is confirmed by T2's constitutional evidence — the desire-faculty as the constitutional engine of fellowship direction means desire determines the object, and the object determines which constitutional level is most engaged. |
| SYN-INTER-062-009 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T1 establishes that fellowship is object-determined and progressively constitutional; T3 shows that fellowship shapes every inner faculty in conformity with its object. These are two descriptions of the same reality: "object determines character" (T1) is the principle; "every faculty shaped by fellowship direction" (T3) is the mechanism. The five-mode structure (T1) explains why T3 finds diverse faculty effects: existential mode primarily engages affect and relational capacity; companionship mode primarily engages volition, moral evaluation, and conscientiousness; suffering mode primarily engages affect and agency; ontological mode primarily engages relational capacity at its deepest level. |
| SYN-INTER-062-010 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T1's five modes correspond directly to T4's relational directions: existential mode = all-the-living (widest scope); companionship mode = human-to-human (giving and receiving); covenantal mode = covenant partner; ontological mode = human-to-God (deepest). The scope-narrows-as-depth-increases finding (T4, Q&A-119) is the relational expression of T1's definition: as the fellowship object becomes more specific (from all-the-living to divine nature), the depth increases while the scope narrows. T1's structural negative (misdirected fellowship) maps onto T4's receiving-direction analysis (Hos 4:17 — prior wrong fellowship blocks reception of right fellowship). |
| SYN-INTER-062-011 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T1 establishes that habitual fellowship produces constitutional character formation; T5 maps this as the primary formation mechanism and identifies the sequence (volitional alignment → habituation → constitutional fixing). Together T1 and T5 reveal the full arc: the definitional claim that fellowship's object determines character (T1) is the endpoint of a formation process that moves through distinct phases (T5). The five-mode structure (T1) generates two distinct formation mechanisms in T5: participatory-transfer (companionship → habituation → character) and wound-as-cleansing (suffering → inner purification). T1's most radical mode (ontological, 2Pe 1:4) is T5's eschatological telos (divine nature participation as the formation endpoint). |
| SYN-INTER-062-012 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T1's five-mode structure explains T6's co-occurrence pattern. The desire-R43 co-occurrence (6 shared verses — strongest signal) reflects T1's finding that desire is the internal motivating state directing fellowship — they co-occur because desire directs fellowship. The unity-R167 co-occurrence reflects T1's finding that fellowship is the participatory inner reality of which unity is the corporate outcome — they co-occur because fellowship produces unity. The structural connections in T6 are the cross-registry expression of T1's definitional relationships. |
| SYN-INTER-062-013 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T1's five-mode structure is encoded in the vocabulary's semantic arc (T7): existential → companionship → moral complicity → suffering → ontological maps precisely onto the semantic range (Ecc 9:4 → Psa 119:63 → Mat 23:30 → Phili 3:10 → 2Pe 1:4). The vocabulary is not a loose collection but a precisely structured arc corresponding to the definitional five modes. T7's grammatical analysis confirms T1's dispositional/act/constitutional distinction: the Hithpael (self-joining act) corresponds to T1's active dimension; the Pual (being-joined condition — Hos 4:17) corresponds to T1's constitutional dimension; the adjectival terms (H2270 companion, H2278 consort) correspond to T1's dispositional dimension. |
| SYN-INTER-062-014 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T2's constitutional locations map directly onto T3's faculty engagements. The desire-faculty location (T2.5) corresponds to T3.6 volition and T3.7 agency — desire and will are structurally adjacent in directing fellowship. The heart location (T2.3) corresponds to T3.8 moral evaluation, T3.9 conscience, T3.10 conscientiousness — the heart-level constitutional engagement with fellowship produces the moral-faculty effects. The soul-level (T2.2 — Ecc 9:4) corresponds to T3.4 affect — the existential fellowship produces hope as an affective state at the soul level. Constitutional location determines which faculties are primarily engaged. |
| SYN-INTER-062-015 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T2's constitutional origin analysis (multiple origins — divine initiative, human volitional alignment, existential given) directly maps onto T4's relational directions: divine initiative (God-to-human direction — T4.1); human volitional alignment (human-to-God and human-giving — T4.2, T4.3); existential given (the condition of all-the-living — T4.5 scope analysis). The constitutional origin determines the relational direction: the mode of fellowship's origin determines who is the primary relational agent in that mode. |
| SYN-INTER-062-016 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T2's constitutional map and T5's formative account describe the same reality from space and time perspectives. The desire-faculty (T2.5) as constitutional engine maps onto T5's formation sequence beginning point (volitional alignment — desire-directed). The heart-level formation (T2.3 — Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23) is T5's constitutional-formation endpoint. The body-deposit question (T2.8) opens into T5.7's developmental consequence analysis — the wound-mark as a formative trace raises the question of whether suffering-fellowship leaves a permanent constitutional marker, a question that remains open pending Session D. |
| SYN-INTER-062-017 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T2's desire-faculty location (H2266 and desire vocabulary — 6-verse co-occurrence with R43) explains why desire (R43) is the strongest co-occurrence signal in T6. The co-occurrence is constitutionally grounded — desire and fellowship operate at the same constitutional level (the faculty that directs choice of object). T2's somatic location (H2250 wound — body) explains the cross-registry connections to R30 contrition and R151 sorrow at Isa 53:5 — all three characteristics meet at the somatic-constitutional level in that verse. |
| SYN-INTER-062-018 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T2's constitutional levels (soul, heart, desire-faculty, somatic) are each evidenced by specific lexical items that T7 analyses. Soul-level (Ecc 9:4 — H2266 Qal); heart-level (Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23 — H2270, H2266 Hithpael); somatic (H2250 — Pro 20:30, Isa 53:5). The grammatical range (T7.1.2) is the lexical expression of the constitutional range (T2): the Hithpael (volitional self-joining) is the heart-level agentive act; the Pual (being-joined — passive settled state) is the constitutional fixing endpoint; the adjectival terms (H2270/H2278 — companion/consort) are the heart-level dispositional forms. T7 provides the vocabulary basis for T2's constitutional account. |
| SYN-INTER-062-019 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T3's faculty engagements all run in the same relational direction that T4 identifies as primary (vertical — human toward God). Perception, volition, moral evaluation, conscientiousness, and relational capacity are all deepened when directed toward the right fellowship object (God and God-fearers). T3's bilateral finding (right fellowship deepens; wrong fellowship degrades) explains T4's structural negative (misdirected fellowship is more dangerous than absence — Hos 4:17, Mat 23:30): when every faculty is progressively shaped by the fellowship object, misdirected fellowship progressively degrades every faculty, not just the relational capacity. |
| SYN-INTER-062-020 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T3's snapshot (what fellowship does to faculties in the moment) and T5's longitudinal account (what habitual fellowship produces over time) together reveal that faculty deepening (T3) is the process of which constitutional character formation (T5) is the endpoint. The right-direction faculty deepening (T3 — perception, cognition, conscience, moral evaluation each deepened by right fellowship) accumulates over time into the fixed character of Psa 119:63's declared God-fearer companion, which is on its way to Jer 13:23's constitutional formation. T3 → T5 is the moment-to-character arc. |
| SYN-INTER-062-021 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T3's moral evaluation and conscience faculty engagements explain the co-occurrence pattern with desire (R43) in T6. Desire (R43) directs fellowship; conscience assesses the moral quality of the fellowship object; moral evaluation confirms or challenges the direction. The three characteristics (desire, fellowship, conscience) operate in sequence: desire directs → conscience evaluates → fellowship forms. The T6 co-occurrence signal between R43 and R062 is the shared-verse evidence of this sequential relationship that T3's faculty analysis illuminates. |
| SYN-INTER-062-022 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T7's literary forms explain why T3 finds different faculty engagements in different evidential contexts. The wisdom poetry setting (Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23, Ecc 9:4) engages primarily the volitional, conscientiousness, and moral evaluation faculties. The apostolic epistolary setting (Phili 3:10, 2Cor 1:7, 2Pe 1:4, 1Jo 1:3) engages primarily the relational capacity, affect, and agency faculties. The prophetic setting (Hos 4:17, Isa 53:5, Jer 13:23) engages primarily the moral evaluation and conscience faculties (diagnostic-corrective function). Literary form determines which faculty register the fellowship vocabulary is deployed in. |
| SYN-INTER-062-023 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T4's relational architecture (vertical primary; horizontal derived) and T5's formation arc (habituation → constitutional character) are structurally connected through 1Jo 1:3: the horizontal fellowship is derived from and grounded in the vertical (T4), and the vertical fellowship is the source from which the horizontal formation extends (T5 — the person who has received divine fellowship can extend human fellowship that is grounded in that divine reality). T4's scope-narrows-as-depth-increases finding is T5's formation telos: the deepest fellowship (divine nature participation — 2Pe 1:4) is also the narrowest in scope and the furthest eschatological endpoint. |
| SYN-INTER-062-024 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T4's relational directions and T6's structural connections are expressing the same reality from two perspectives. The God-to-human direction (T4.1 — divine initiative) corresponds to the R180 yielding connection (T6 — shared anchor 2Pe 1:4): the divine initiative at 2Pe 1:4 requires an inner human yielding (escape from corruption) as its receiving condition. The human-to-human giving direction (T4.3 — companionship, covenant, comfort-sharing) corresponds to the R34 covenant, R192 comfort, and R43 desire connections in T6. The relational directions (T4) and the structural cross-registry connections (T6) are the inner-being and cross-registry expressions of the same relational movements. |
| SYN-INTER-062-025 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T4's relational directions map directly onto T7's literary forms. The divine-to-human direction (T4.1) is primarily carried by the apostolic epistolary setting (2Pe 1:4, 2Cor 13:14, 1Jo 1:3). The human-to-God direction (T4.2) is primarily carried by wisdom poetry (Psa 119:63, Phili 3:10) and apostolic letter (Phili 3:10). The human-giving direction (T4.3) is carried by wisdom poetry (Ecc 4:10), apostolic letter (2Cor 1:7), and covenantal text (Mal 2:14). The literary form determines which relational direction is expressed: the apostolic letter form enables the most explicit vertical formulation; wisdom poetry enables the most explicit horizontal formulation. |
| SYN-INTER-062-026 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T5's formation mechanisms (participatory transfer and wound-as-cleansing) explain why fellowship is structurally connected in T6 to both desire (R43 — participatory-transfer mechanism begins with desire-directed alignment) and to contrition/sorrow (R30, R151 — wound-as-cleansing mechanism meets contrition and sorrow at Isa 53:5). T5's two formation mechanisms generate two distinct clusters of T6 structural connections: the habitual-alignment cluster (R43 desire, R167 unity, R34 covenant) and the suffering-cleansing cluster (R30 contrition, R151 sorrow, R192 comfort). Formation mechanism determines structural cluster. |
| SYN-INTER-062-027 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T5's formation sequence (volitional alignment → habituation → constitutional fixing) is grounded in T7's verse evidence. The sequence beginning point (volitional alignment) is carried by Psa 119:63 (wisdom poetry — declared dispositional alignment). The habituation arc is carried by the prophetic diagnostic setting (Jer 13:23, Hos 4:17 — naming the fixed endpoint of wrong habitual fellowship). The constitutional telos (divine nature participation) is carried by the apostolic-theological setting (2Pe 1:4). T7's literary forms track the formation sequence from beginning (wisdom poetry) through middle (prophetic diagnosis) to eschatological telos (apostolic theology). |
| SYN-INTER-062-028 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R062 fellowship |  | T6's structural connections and T7's vocabulary analysis are mutually grounding. The wound-companionship shared root (T6.4 — H2250 wound and H2270 companion both from chavar) is T7's single most significant etymology finding: the same root that produces companionship produces wound, revealing that the deepest fellowship is structurally wound-rooted. T6's primary anchor verses (Isa 53:5, 2Pe 1:4, 1Jo 1:3, Mal 2:14 as cross-registry hubs) are also T7's most analytically rich verses — the verses that carry the most structural connections are the same verses that are most exegetically developed in the primary anchor analysis. |
| DIM-66-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R066 gentleness |  | a.nah (H6031B) groups show affliction operating as an inner-being shaper in three modes: (1) affliction by others (1923-001 — experienced suffering), (2) affliction by God (1923-002 — purposive divine formation), (3) voluntary self-affliction (1923-003 — chosen submission). The same term covers all three, suggesting that the gentleness/meekness registry understands affliction as the formative mechanism of inner character. Session B should examine how gentleness is structurally connected to the affliction vocabulary — whether biblical gentleness is primarily the fruit of endured humbling. |
| DIM-67-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R067 goodness |  | The tov word family spans Theological/Divine-Human (divine goodness), Moral/Conscience (human moral character), Spiritual/God-ward (experiential good), and Character/Disposition (Spirit-fruit) — a very wide dimensional range for one term. Session B should analyse whether these are genuinely distinct inner-being phenomena or expressions of a unified category of 'goodness' that the English vocabulary separates but Hebrew integrates. |
| OBS-067-OBS-001 | OBSERVATION | R067 goodness |  | Registry description names 3 axes: aesthetic/moral/relational; human goodness is derivative |
| OBS-067-OBS-002 | OBSERVATION | R067 goodness |  | Registry-level dimension = Moral/Conscience; groups show wider spread — tension noted |
| OBS-067-OBS-003 | OBSERVATION | R067 goodness |  | Domain G failure (no researcher narrative) — documentation gap only, not analytical |
| OBS-067-OBS-004 | OBSERVATION | R067 goodness |  | XREF structure: 6/9 terms to R65 (generosity); agathos 90-verse OWNER vs agathōsunē 4-verse |
| OBS-067-OBS-005 | OBSERVATION | R067 goodness |  | H2898 (tuv) has 0 OWNER verses in R103 — possible data quality observation |
| OBS-067-OBS-006 | OBSERVATION | R067 goodness |  | H2896A meaning parse: 10 sub-senses covering the full landscape of the 9 groups |
| OBS-067-OBS-007 | OBSERVATION | R067 goodness |  | TOV root family distributed across R67 (adjectival), R65 (active doing), R103 (relational-affective) |
| OBS-067-OBS-008 | OBSERVATION | R067 goodness |  | G0019 Gal 5:22 glossed as "generosity" — semantic overlap with R65 noted |

| ANOMALY-067-002 | DATA_ANOMALY_CITATION_GAP | R067 goodness |  | Citation FK resolution exceeds 10% threshold in: sb_s2c_ch2: 7/40 unresolved (18%); sb_s2c_ch4: 11/42 unresolved (26%); sb_s2c_ch5: 8/55 unresolved (15%) |
| OBS-067-T2-001 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | R067 goodness | Psa 119:68, Gen 1:31, Psa 34:8 | The verse evidence reveals that goodness is constitutive of God's character at its most fundamental level. Psa 119:68 ("You are good and do good") establishes an ontological prior — God's being-good precedes and grounds his doing-good. This is not goodness as one attribute among many but as a character description. The doxological assertion group (884-001) — "the Lord is good, his steadfast love endures forever" — names goodness as the summative confession of who God is in his covenantal relationship to creation. Gen 1:31 (884-007) extends this: the Creator's evaluative pronouncement declaring creation "very good" is an expression of his own goodness — he can only declare good what coheres with his character. God's goodness thus images itself in the human person as the capacity for moral assessment, relational orientation, and ontological alignment with what genuinely benefits. The human image-bearer who is good reflects the same character that God is, not by self-generation but by derivation. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-026, OBS-067-OBS-036, OBS-067-OBS-037) |
| OBS-067-T2-020 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | R067 goodness | Multiple — see group anchors | 12 distinct modes confirmed (OBS-067-OBS-048, DIM-67-001 resolved): (1) Doxological assertion — 884-001: goodness operates as the content of praise and the ground of trust; (2) Human moral character — 884-002: goodness operates as the quality of the person's inner life assessed before God and others; (3) Experiential orientation — 884-003: goodness operates as the content of what genuinely satisfies, with proximity to God as the supreme form; (4) Comparative wisdom evaluation — 884-004: goodness operates as the criterion of ranking — what is better than what; (5) Covenantal word — 884-005: goodness operates as the faithfulness of God's promise operating in the inner being through reception and knowing; (6) Negative moral verdict — 884-006: goodness operates inversely — its absence is named and assessed; (7) Creative pronouncement — 884-007: goodness operates as God's evaluative creative act; (8) Volitional preference — 884-008: goodness operates as the criterion of choice — what is good-in-one's-eyes; (9) Inner well-being — 884-009: goodness operates as the shalom-condition of gladness and well-being; (10) Spirit-produced virtue — 885-001: goodness operates as the Spirit-filled inner disposition enabling righteous resolve; (11) Divine generous goodwill — 886-001: goodness operates as God's relational disposition leading to repentance; (12) Spirit-produced kindness — 886-002: goodness operates as the Spirit-produced relational character quality toward others. (OBS-067-OBS-048, OBS-067-OBS-003) |
| OBS-067-T2-023 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Psa 34:8, Psa 119:68 | Three immediate responses are named in the verse evidence: (1) Trust and refuge-taking — Psa 34:8 ("taste and see that the Lord is good; blessed is the man who takes refuge in him"). The immediate response to encountering divine goodness is the reflex of trust. (2) Praise and thanksgiving — 884-001 groups: the doxological refrain as immediate inner response to recognising God's goodness. (3) Receptive desire for instruction — Psa 119:68 ("you are good and do good; teach me your statutes"). The immediate response to recognising God's goodness is the desire to learn his ways — goodness encountered produces a pedagogical receptivity. These three immediate responses are consistent across the Hebrew evidence. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-026, SBF-067-Q004) |
| OBS-067-T2-027 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Psa 34:8, Psa 73:28, 2Th 1:11 | Yes — there is a clear distinction. The immediate response to encountering divine goodness is trust/refuge (Psa 34:8) and praise (doxological group). The sustained effect is repositioned inner orientation (Psa 73:28) and community capacity (Rom 15:14). The immediate response is reactive — a first movement of the inner being toward the good encountered. The sustained effect is constitutive — a settled state that reshapes the person's character, orientation, and relational capacity over time. The distinction is most sharply drawn in Psa 73 where Asaph's encounter with divine goodness produces a sustained reorientation rather than a momentary response. 2Th 1:11's "fulfil every resolve for good" confirms the sustained/completion dimension — goodness works toward completion, not just initiation. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-029, SBF-067-Q031) |
| OBS-067-T2-030 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Est 5:9, Jer 6:16 | Two forms of non-reception are evidenced: (1) Haman (Est 5:9, 884-009): the person who experiences a surface form of well-being (tov-lev — glad of heart) without its genuine ground. The inner state is one of fragile gladness — pleasantness without rootedness. One adverse sight immediately destroys it. This is not a person who has rejected goodness but one who has mistaken a lesser good for the genuine good. The inner state is: temporarily satisfied, morally ungrounded, and constitutionally fragile. (2) Those who "will not walk in it" (Jer 6:16): the person who has heard and encountered the good way but refused it. The inner state is one of offered-but-rejected goodness — the will has been engaged by the good and actively turned away. The inner state is: resistant, self-willed, and under the verdict of their own refusal. (OBS-067-OBS-040, OBS-067-OBS-041, SBF-067-Q028) |
| OBS-067-T2-037 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R067 goodness | Jos 23:14, Psa 73:28, Lam 3:25 | Soul-level location is partially evidenced. Jos 23:14 names "heart and soul" as the site of covenantal knowing — "you know in your hearts and souls that not one word has failed of all the good things the Lord your God promised." The reception of God's good covenantal word (884-005) is explicitly a heart-and-soul event. Psa 73:28 names the soul-level experience of nearness to God as the supreme good — the soul's orientation toward God as its reference is the inner-being state of 884-003. Lam 3:25 ("The Lord is good to those who wait for him, to the soul who seeks him") explicitly names the soul as the faculty that seeks and receives God's goodness. The soul-level location is confirmed for the experiential and receptive registers of goodness. (OBS-067-OBS-033, OBS-067-OBS-029, SBF-067-Q009) |
| OBS-067-T2-038 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R067 goodness | Lam 3:25, Psa 73:28, Jos 23:14 | Soul-level location reveals that the experiential register of goodness (884-003 — nearness to God as the supreme good; Lam 3:25 — the soul seeking God's goodness) is not a surface experience but a deep inner-being orientation. The soul as the seat of personal experience and desire is the natural site for goodness-as-experienced-good — the characteristic reaches the person's innermost capacity for orientation, seeking, and satisfaction. Lam 3:25's pairing of "the soul who seeks" with goodness received confirms that the seeking soul and the good God are structurally connected — goodness is what the soul in its deepest orientation is designed to receive. (OBS-067-OBS-029, OBS-067-OBS-033) |
| OBS-067-T2-039 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R067 goodness | Est 5:9, Jos 23:14, Eze 36:31 | Heart-location is confirmed across multiple instances. Est 5:9 (884-009): "glad of heart" (tov-lev) — the compound phrase explicitly locates the inner well-being state in the heart. Jos 23:14: "you know in your hearts and souls" — the heart is named first as the site of covenantal knowing of God's good word. Eze 36:31: "you will remember your evil ways and your deeds that were not good, and you will loathe yourselves" — the memory and moral self-assessment that precede the recognition of not-goodness are heart-faculty operations. The heart is engaged across goodness in its emotional (glad of heart), cognitive (knowing), and moral-assessment (remembering and recognising not-good) registers. (OBS-067-OBS-034, OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-040 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R067 goodness | Jos 23:14, Est 5:9, Eze 36:31 | Heart-location reveals that goodness engages multiple integrating functions of the heart simultaneously. (1) Knowing — Jos 23:14: the heart's cognitive function receives and holds the knowledge of God's good covenantal word. (2) Feeling — Est 5:9: the heart is the site of the affective gladness-state (tov-lev). (3) Moral awareness — Eze 36:31: the heart's moral self-assessment capacity is activated when not-goodness is recognised; memory and loathing are heart-operations. The heart as the integrating centre of the inner being engages goodness at all three levels — knowing, feeling, and moral awareness — which reflects the breadth of the tov vocabulary itself. (OBS-067-OBS-034, OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-041 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R067 goodness | Pro 15:16, Pro 16:32, Rom 15:14 | Direct mind-location is not explicitly named in the verse evidence. However, the cognitive register of goodness is evidenced through the dimension assignment: 884-004 (comparative wisdom evaluation) and 884-006 (moral verdict) are assigned Cognition (03), indicating that the mind's evaluative and discerning function is engaged. The comparative wisdom idiom ("better than") is a cognitive-evaluative operation — assessing and ranking goods. This is a mind-faculty operation even if the term "mind" is not explicitly used. Rom 15:14 pairs goodness with "all knowledge" in the same clause — "full of goodness, filled with all knowledge" — suggesting that goodness and cognitive knowledge are related inner-being qualities. The evidence locates goodness at the evaluative level of mind-function, but explicit mind-language is absent. (OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-025) |
| OBS-067-T2-042 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R067 goodness | Pro 15:16, Pro 16:32, Jer 26:14 | The evaluative and discerning aspect of the mind is what goodness engages. The comparative wisdom mode (884-004) engages the mind's capacity to rank and assess — distinguishing greater goods from lesser goods. The moral verdict mode (884-006) engages the mind's capacity to assess conduct against a standard. The volitional-preference mode (884-008) engages the mind's evaluative role in choice — "what seems good to you" implies an assessment before a decision. This is the mind functioning as moral evaluator — not primarily as rational analyst but as the faculty of moral discernment. The fact that Cognition is a secondary dimension for this characteristic (Q&A-036) confirms the engagement but at a secondary level. (OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-014, SBF-067-Q088) |
| OBS-067-T2-043 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | R067 goodness | Lam 3:25, Psa 73:28 | Lam 3:25 names "the soul who seeks him" — this is a whole-soul seeking orientation, not specifically heart or mind. The soul as the seat of desire and orientation (relevant to the experiential good groups 884-003) functions as a seeking faculty distinct from the cognitive or affective heart-functions identified above. However, the data does not subdivide the soul-subsets with precision beyond heart — the Hebrew anthropological vocabulary in these verses is not granular at the sub-soul level. Conscience is a potential soul-subset engagement: the self-loathing of Eze 36:31 implies a conscience operation (moral inner witness producing conviction), but this is addressed under T3.9 below. No further soul-subset locations are clearly evidenced beyond what is captured at heart and the general soul-seeking orientation. (OBS-067-OBS-029, OBS-067-OBS-033) |
| OBS-067-T2-044 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | R067 goodness | Est 5:9, Psa 34:8 | The "glad of heart" (tov-lev) compound at Est 5:9 uses lev (heart), which in Hebrew serves double duty as both the inner-being centre and the physical organ — though the phrase is clearly functioning at the inner-being level, not the physical cardiac level. No other specific body part is explicitly linked to goodness in the anchor verse evidence. The Psa 34:8 "taste and see" metaphor uses perceptual language (taste, see) that implies sensory or quasi-physical engagement, but this is figurative language for inner perception rather than a literal body-part linkage. The data is thin here. (OBS-067-OBS-007, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-045 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | R067 goodness | Est 5:9, Psa 34:8 | For "glad of heart" (tov-lev, Est 5:9): the heart-compound is emphatic — locating the inner well-being state specifically in the heart gives it a constitutive rather than circumstantial character. For Psa 34:8 ("taste and see"): the perceptual language is experiential and invitational — Scripture is using sensory vocabulary to name a quality of inner encounter that has a quasi-perceptual character. The "tasting" of goodness is not abstract cognition but participatory encounter. Scripture is inviting an embodied-in-principle inner perception — the kind of knowledge that comes from experience rather than inference. Beyond these two instances the somatic evidence is limited. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-046 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | R067 goodness | Est 5:9, Psa 34:8 | For the tov-lev (glad of heart) compound: the direction appears to run soul → expression — the inner well-being state manifests as gladness of heart (Est 5:9: Haman "went out joyful and glad of heart" — the inner state expressed in visible disposition). Psa 34:8 "taste and see" runs in the direction: divine goodness → inner perceptual encounter → inner being transformed. The direction is consistently from the divine or inner source outward/downward to embodied expression — not from the body upward to produce goodness. However, the tov-lev instance could also indicate a feedback loop: inner gladness of heart is both an inner state and expressed in visible comportment. The data is insufficient to fully resolve the directionality. (OBS-067-OBS-040, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-047 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | R067 goodness | Est 5:9 | If the direction runs soul → body (inner well-being produces gladness visible in comportment, as in Est 5:9), the consequence is that goodness is a deep inner quality that naturally expresses itself outwardly — it cannot be purely internal without visible expression. But the Haman diagnostic also confirms that the outward expression of gladness (tov-lev) can be present without genuine inner goodness-rootedness. This distinction is analytically significant: visible gladness and genuine goodness are not identical, and the body's expression of well-being can be present even in the absence of its genuine source. The directional consequence is that inner goodness properly produces outward expression but outward expression does not guarantee inner goodness. (OBS-067-OBS-040, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-053 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | R067 goodness | Psa 34:8 | The perceptive faculty is significantly engaged. Psa 34:8 ("taste and see that the Lord is good") invokes two inner senses simultaneously: taste (a contact-sense implying intimacy and direct experience) and sight (a distance-sense implying recognition and discernment). The use of perceptual language for encountering goodness is deliberate — divine goodness is not known by inference but by experiential perception. The "tasting" of goodness is an inner-perception act: a direct, participatory encounter that produces inner-being knowledge. "Seeing" goodness implies a discernment of its reality. The shared anchor at Psa 34:8 with R49 (discernment) confirms that tasting goodness and exercising discernment are structurally connected (SP-067-007). The perceptive faculty is also engaged in the comparative wisdom mode (884-004): "seeing" which is better requires evaluative perception. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-007) |
| OBS-067-T2-054 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Psa 34:8, Mal 2:17 | Goodness deepens and enables the perceptive faculty. Psa 34:8 presents the encounter with divine goodness as sharpening inner perception — those who "take refuge" and "taste" develop a refined capacity to recognise what is genuinely good. The comparative wisdom mode (884-004) requires and develops discernment — the better-than sayings train the evaluative perception to distinguish inner goods from lesser goods. Conversely, the absence or distortion of goodness impairs perception: Mal 2:17 / Jer 44:17 name the inversion of the moral perception faculty as the condition where evil is called good. This implies that goodness-present sharpens perception; goodness-absent or distorted corrupts it. The perceptive faculty and the goodness-characteristic are mutually reinforcing: genuine goodness cultivates perception; accurate perception recognises genuine goodness. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-021, SBF-067-Q065) |
| OBS-067-T2-055 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Psa 34:8, Est 5:9 | The perceptive engagement of goodness reveals that it is not primarily an abstract ethical category but an experiential reality that must be encountered and tasted. Goodness is known through contact, not inference. This is structurally consistent with the Hebrew tov's phenomenological root (what is genuinely pleasant, beneficial, and right — experienced as such). The pattern reveals that goodness has an inherently perceptual character — it calls for encounter, not merely assent. The Haman diagnostic (Est 5:9) confirms this: he experienced a surface perception of well-being without genuine perceptual encounter with goodness as rooted in God. Perception without genuine encounter produces the counterfeit. The nature of goodness is therefore inherently experiential and perceptually demanding. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-041, SBF-067-Q088) |
| OBS-067-T2-056 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | R067 goodness | Pro 15:16, Jos 23:14, Eze 36:31 | Yes — cognition is a secondary dimension of goodness (confirmed at Q&A-036). The cognitive engagement takes three forms: (1) Evaluative cognition — the comparative wisdom mode (884-004) requires ranking and assessing goods against each other. "Better than" implies a knowing that orders and ranks. (2) Moral assessment cognition — the not-good verdict mode (884-006) requires applying the standard of the good to conduct and naming the verdict. This is a moral-cognitive act. (3) Receptive cognition — Jos 23:14 ("you know in your hearts and souls") names the reception of covenantal knowing as a cognitive act located in the inner being. The mind knows and holds God's good word as an inner-being cognitive reality. (OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-033, OBS-067-OBS-034) |
| OBS-067-T2-057 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Pro 15:16, Rom 15:14, Mal 2:17 | Goodness enables and deepens cognition. The comparative wisdom mode actively trains the evaluative intellect — the better-than sayings of 884-004 require and cultivate discerning moral cognition. Rom 15:14 pairs goodness with knowledge in a single characterological portrait: "full of goodness, filled with all knowledge" — suggesting a natural coherence between goodness and cognitive fullness. The absence of goodness impairs cognition: the moral inversion of Mal 2:17 / Jer 44:17 (calling evil good) is a cognitive failure — the evaluative faculty is corrupted. The direction is clear: goodness present deepens moral cognition; goodness absent or distorted impairs it. (OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-021, OBS-067-OBS-025) |
| OBS-067-T2-059 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Eze 36:31, Jos 23:14 | Memory is substantively engaged. Eze 36:31 ("Then you will remember your evil ways, and your deeds that were not good, and you will loathe yourselves") names memory as the faculty through which moral awareness of not-goodness is produced. The sequence is: memory activated → deeds recalled as not-good → self-loathing as moral response. Memory is the trigger for the moral-recognition that precedes transformation. Jos 23:14 also activates memory: "you know in your hearts and souls... that not one word has failed" — the covenantal knowing of God's goodness is held in the inner being through remembered experience of his faithfulness. Goodness engages memory in both its positive and negative registers: remembering God's faithfulness (covenantal knowing) and remembering one's own not-goodness (moral reckoning). (OBS-067-OBS-034, OBS-067-OBS-035) |
| OBS-067-T2-060 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Eze 36:31, Jos 23:14, Jer 44:17 | Goodness enables and deepens memory in its truthful operation. The covenantal knowing of Eze 23:14 (God's faithfulness held in memory) is a deepened form of inner-being knowledge held over time. The moral reckoning of Eze 36:31 (remembering deeds as not-good) is memory enabled by an act of God — the divine work that brings the person to genuine self-recognition includes activating memory truthfully. Conversely, the self-justifying memory of Jer 44:17 ("when we burned incense to the queen of heaven, we had plenty of food and prospered") is an impaired memory — goodness misidentified produces false memory of what the good was. Genuine goodness enables truthful memory; false goodness impairs it. (OBS-067-OBS-034, OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-043) |
| OBS-067-T2-061 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Eze 36:31, Jos 23:14 | The memory engagement of goodness reveals that it is temporally extended — it works through the inner being's capacity to hold experience over time and draw present moral assessment from it. The Eze 36:31 sequence (remember → recognise as not-good → loathe) shows goodness operating through a temporal moral process, not merely a momentary conviction. The covenantal knowing of Jos 23:14 shows goodness held in memory as a form of inner-being certainty across time. This reveals that goodness has a historical and narrative dimension in the inner being — the person whose inner life is shaped by goodness has a truthful memory of what God has done and a truthful reckoning of their own conduct. (OBS-067-OBS-034, OBS-067-OBS-035) |
| OBS-067-T2-062 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Est 5:9, Psa 34:8, Eze 36:31 | The affective faculty is substantively engaged across multiple groups. The inner well-being group (884-009) is predominantly affective: tov-lev (glad of heart, Est 5:9) and the associated inner state of gladness, prospering, and well-being are emotional-experiential states. The doxological group (884-001) has an affective dimension: praise and thanksgiving are emotional responses to encountering divine goodness. The self-loathing of Eze 36:31 is a negative affective response to recognition of not-goodness. The trust-response to Psa 34:8 has an affective dimension. The experiential group (884-003) as a whole is oriented toward inner satisfaction and well-being — an affective register of goodness. (OBS-067-OBS-029, OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-063 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Psa 34:8, Psa 73:28, Est 5:9 | Goodness enables and deepens affect when genuine. Genuine encounter with divine goodness produces trust, praise, and gladness rooted in God — these are authentic affective states produced by the encounter. The comparative wisdom mode also has an affective dimension: the person who genuinely values "better than" outcomes experiences a right ordering of their desires and affections. Distorted goodness impairs affect: Haman's gladness-of-heart (tov-lev, Est 5:9) is genuine affect but immediately destroyed — it is affect without its proper root, which renders it constitutionally fragile. The diagnosis is that goodness properly produces deep, stable, rooted affective states; goodness-mimicry produces shallow, fragile affective states. (OBS-067-OBS-040, OBS-067-OBS-041, OBS-067-OBS-029) |
| OBS-067-T2-064 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Psa 73:28, Est 5:9 | The affective engagement pattern reveals that goodness is not a purely cognitive or moral-rational characteristic — it has a genuine affective root. The Haman diagnostic is the sharpest evidence: the inner-well-being state (tov-lev) is a real affective state, not a moral fiction. But its fragility reveals that affect without proper grounding in genuine goodness is structurally unstable. The stable positive affect of Psa 73:28 (the repositioned inner life of Asaph) contrasts with Haman's fragile gladness and reveals that genuine goodness produces affect that is resilient precisely because it is rooted in the enduring goodness of God, not in circumstance. Goodness is therefore affectively generative — it produces genuine, stable, and resilient positive inner states — but only when the goodness is genuine. (OBS-067-OBS-029, OBS-067-OBS-040, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-066 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Jer 26:14, 2Th 1:11, Isa 65:2 | Goodness reconstitutes volition rather than bypassing or overriding it. 2Th 1:11 makes this precise: "may God fulfil every resolve for good" — the human resolve is real and operative; God's power fulfils it (does not replace it). The structure is: Spirit-produced goodness produces a genuine resolve in the human will → God's power fulfils that resolve → the human agent acts. The will's capacity is enabled (Spirit-production enables the resolve), the will's interaction with other characteristics is shaped (goodness reconstitutes what the will chooses as its object), and the will operates under the constraint of the good as revealed (Mic 6:8 — "He has told you what is good" sets the constraint within which the will's proper operation is defined). Volition without this reconstitution produces self-direction (Isa 65:2) or wilful refusal (Jer 6:16) — the will's natural operation in the fallen condition is away from the good. (OBS-067-OBS-014, OBS-067-OBS-039, OBS-067-OBS-040, SBF-067-Q040) |
| OBS-067-T2-086 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | R067 goodness | Psa 34:8, Lam 3:25, Psa 119:68 | Human-to-God operation is evidenced through three modes: (1) Doxological worship — the liturgical refrain "for he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (group 884-001) is goodness operating as the content of the human's praise and confession toward God. The human declares God's goodness in worship — this is goodness moving upward from the human toward God as acknowledgement and adoration. (2) Seeking and trust — Psa 34:8 ("blessed is the man who takes refuge in him") and Lam 3:25 ("the soul who seeks him"): the human's movement toward God in trust and seeking is enabled by the encounter with divine goodness. Goodness produces the movement toward God. (3) Covenantal request — Psa 119:68 ("you are good and do good; teach me your statutes"): the human addresses God's goodness as the ground for petition. God's goodness is invoked as the basis of the human's request — "because you are good, teach me." (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-026, OBS-067-OBS-033) |
| OBS-067-T2-087 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Lam 3:25, Psa 34:8, Psa 73:28 | Three inner postures are named for human-toward-God movement in goodness: (1) Waiting — Lam 3:25 ("the Lord is good to those who wait for him"). Waiting is the inner posture of active expectation without premature action — a stance of dependence and trust. (2) Seeking — Lam 3:25 ("the soul who seeks him"). Seeking is directional — the soul oriented actively toward God. (3) Trust and refuge — Psa 34:8 ("blessed is the man who takes refuge in him"): refuge-taking is the inner posture of surrender and reliance. These three postures share a common structure: they all involve the person relinquishing self-sufficiency and directing the inner being toward God. They contrast directly with the closing inner orientations (Q&A-032): self-direction, wilful refusal, and departure from faith. The enabling postures are fundamentally un-self-centred; the blocking postures are fundamentally self-centred. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-033, SBF-067-Q014) |
| OBS-067-T2-088 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | R067 goodness | Psa 119:68, Psa 34:8, Psa 73:28 | The human-to-God direction reveals that the relationship is one of responsive dependence grounded in prior divine initiative. The sequence is always: God's goodness is encountered or declared first → the human moves toward God in trust, praise, and seeking → God sustains and fulfils. The human's movement toward God in goodness is not autonomous spiritual achievement but a response enabled by prior encounter with divine goodness. Psa 119:68's "you are good... teach me" names the relationship structure directly: God's goodness is the basis; human receptive orientation follows. The relationship revealed is one of: initiated by God, received by the human, responded to in trust and worship, sustained in covenantal fidelity. The human's part is not passive but it is always derivative — the human's goodness-orientation toward God is itself produced by God's prior goodness toward the human. (OBS-067-OBS-026, OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-001) |
| OBS-067-T2-094 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Jer 6:16, Est 5:9 | The verse evidence addresses non-reception primarily in the divine-to-human direction. Jer 6:16 ("we will not walk in it") is the clearest picture: the inner state is one of offered-and-refused goodness — active resistance of what is genuinely good. The will has been engaged and has chosen refusal. The person's inner state is: morally informed (they have encountered the good way), volitionally closed (they have refused it), and unaware of the full consequence of their refusal. For the human-to-human non-reception context specifically: the data is silent on the inner state of a person who encounters human goodness and refuses it. The Haman narrative (Est 5:9) names the inner state of the person who misidentifies goodness — but this is self-deception rather than active refusal of another's goodness. This is a partial gap. (OBS-067-OBS-040, OBS-067-OBS-043) |
| OBS-067-T2-098 | OBSERVATION | R067 goodness | N/A | The distortion evidence (chrēstologia — G5542 as the etymological distortion form; Mal 2:17 — calling evil good; Jer 44:17 — redefining goodness as material advantage) names distortion as a pattern. Whether this distortion is explicitly linked to adversarial spiritual activity is not directly addressed in the anchor verses. The distortion appears in the data as a human phenomenon (self-justification, inverted moral assessment) rather than an explicitly adversarial spiritual attack. The possibility that goodness is a site of adversarial attack is plausible from the pattern of distortion but is not evidenced in the anchor verses. This is a genuine partial gap. (OBS-067-OBS-021, OBS-067-OBS-024, SBF-067-Q065) |
| OBS-067-T2-102 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Psa 34:8, Psa 73:28, Eze 36:31 | The two sequences reveal that goodness works through different mechanisms depending on its register. The positive arc (encounter → trust → reorientation → overflow) reveals that goodness works by producing a progressive deepening of inner orientation — encounter leads to trust, trust leads to stable orientation, orientation leads to outward expression. The characteristic builds from first contact to sustained character. The moral awareness arc (memory → recognition → loathing → repentance) reveals that goodness works through the confrontation with its own absence — the standard of the good, when encountered, produces awareness of not-goodness that is itself a mechanism of transformation. Goodness therefore works both through positive encounter (receiving divine goodness) and through negative recognition (confronting one's absence of goodness). The sequence also reveals the role of time: both arcs are extended through time, not instantaneous — goodness is a formative characteristic, not a momentary experience. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-029, OBS-067-OBS-035) |
| OBS-067-T2-105 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Lam 3:25, Psa 34:8, Psa 73:28 | Yes — the goodness-suffering connection is evidenced in two registers (SBF-067-Q048): (1) Divine goodness sustaining in affliction — Lam 3:25 ("the Lord is good to those who wait for him, to the soul who seeks him") is composed in the context of Lamentations — the most concentrated suffering in the Hebrew canon. God's goodness is named as accessible and sustaining precisely in the condition of profound affliction. Suffering does not negate goodness; it becomes the context in which waiting for God's goodness is practised and discovered. (2) Divine goodness available in crisis — Psa 34:8 ("taste and see that the Lord is good") is written by David in the context of his distress — Psalm 34 is a crisis psalm. The invitation to taste goodness is issued from within suffering. The goodness-suffering connection in R67 is not incidental — it is structurally embedded in the anchor verse contexts. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-033, SBF-067-Q048) |
| OBS-067-T2-106 | VERSE_PATTERN | R067 goodness | Psa 73:28, Lam 3:25, Est 5:9, Eze 36:31 | The evidence supports all four modes but with different emphases. Deepening: Psa 73:28 — Asaph's suffering (crisis of doubt, seeing the prosperity of the wicked, Psa 73:1–16) precedes and produces his reorientation toward God as the supreme good. The suffering is the condition that makes the goodness of nearness to God visible by contrast. The characteristic is deepened by the suffering — Asaph discovers what is truly good precisely through the crisis. Revealing: suffering reveals that goodness rooted in God is resistant to circumstantial disruption (Psa 73:28 — stable inner orientation despite unchanged circumstances); it also reveals the fragility of goodness not so rooted (Est 5:9 — Haman's gladness destroyed instantly). Testing: Lam 3:25 — waiting for God's goodness in the context of catastrophic national suffering is a test of whether the person's confidence in goodness is genuine. Producing: the Eze 36:31 sequence (suffering of exile → moral reckoning → repentance) suggests that suffering can be the context in which goodness is ultimately produced through the confrontation with its own absence. (OBS-067-OBS-029, OBS-067-OBS-033, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-126 | CROSS_REGISTRY | R067 goodness | N/A | R67's confirmed dimensions: Moral Character (05) — primary for human-subject groups; Divine-Human Correspondence (11) — primary for divine-subject groups; Cognition (03) — secondary; Volition (04) — secondary. Each dimension is almost certainly shared with multiple other characteristics: Moral Character (05) is likely the most widely shared dimension in the moral-character cluster (C10 and adjacent). Volition (04) is engaged by will (R173), desire (R043), and others. Cognition (03) is engaged by knowledge (R100), wisdom (R174), and others. Divine-Human Correspondence (11) is engaged by all characteristics that have both divine and human expressions. However, the formal dimensional sharing data (which registries share which dimensions) requires the cross-registry comparative analysis of Session D. The current data package does not contain a dimensional sharing table across registries. This is a partial answer: the dimensions are confirmed; the sharing pattern requires Session D. (OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-017, SBF-067-Q126) |
| OBS-067-T2-127 | CROSS_REGISTRY | R067 goodness | N/A | The analytical pattern is inferrable even without full cross-registry data. Moral Character (05) shared with adjacent moral-character registries (honesty, integrity, righteousness) suggests goodness belongs to a cluster of characteristics that together constitute the moral inner life — they are companions, not competitors. Volition (04) shared with will and desire suggests goodness and volition are structurally connected — what the will chooses reflects its goodness orientation. Cognition (03) shared with knowledge and wisdom suggests goodness operates alongside intellectual characteristics rather than independently of them — consistent with Rom 15:14's pairing. The full cross-registry dimensional sharing analysis is Session D territory — specifically SP-067-025 (root family distribution and analytical framework assessment). (OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-017, SP-067-025) |
| OBS-067-T2-128 | CROSS_REGISTRY | R067 goodness | N/A | Cross-registry dimensional sharing data is not available in the current data package. The dimensions of R67 are confirmed; the formal comparison of which other registries share each dimension requires Session D's cross-registry analysis. This gap is noted explicitly. The analysis is not blocked — the findings at Q&A-165 and Q&A-166 are based on the R67 data alone, with the cross-registry comparison deferred. |
| OBS-067-T2-135 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | R067 goodness | Lam 3:25, Psa 119:68 | No dedicated supplication term for goodness is present in the OWNER vocabulary. However, Lam 3:25 names "the soul who seeks him" in the context of seeking God's goodness — seeking (biqqesh / darash implied) is the posture. Psa 119:68 ("teach me your statutes") contains a petition grounded in God's goodness, functioning as a seeking-from-God. But these are not dedicated goodness-seeking terms — they are the general seeking/petition vocabulary applied in goodness contexts. A dedicated supplication form (like "ask for goodness") is absent. Partial gap. (OBS-067-OBS-033, OBS-067-OBS-026) |
| SYN-INTRA-067-001 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R067 goodness |  | T1 as a whole reveals that goodness is constitutively multi-modal but unified in origin. The characteristic's 12 distinct operational modes (Q&A-022) span quality, disposition, condition, and evaluative act — and yet all share a single definitional structure: goodness is what proceeds from and returns to God. The immediate response varies by encounter type (Q&A-025-026), the sustained effect is constitutive rather than reactive (Q&A-030), and the dimension structure is bipartite — Moral Character for human-subject goodness, Divine-Human Correspondence for divine-subject (Q&A-034). What the tier as a whole reveals is that the apparent definitional complexity of goodness (12 modes, multiple dimensions, varied immediate responses) resolves into a coherent characterological portrait: goodness is the quality of alignment with the character of the one who is Good — variable in its expression but unified in its source and standard. |
| SYN-INTRA-067-002 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R067 goodness |  | T2 as a whole reveals that goodness has a comprehensive multi-level constitutional presence — from Spirit-level origin through soul-orientation, heart-engagement, and mind-evaluative functions to outward expression — but with a marked asymmetry in evidence quality. Spirit-level (Q&A-037-039), soul-level (Q&A-040-041), and heart-level (Q&A-043-044) are all substantively evidenced. Mind-level is partially evidenced through cognitive dimensions. Body-level is thin — the gladness-of-heart compound (tov-lev) and the taste-and-see perceptual metaphor are the somatic markers, but no constitutional body deposit is evidenced (T2.8 silent). The most structurally significant T2 finding is the six-step constitutional movement (Q&A-064): Spirit-level origin → soul reorientation → heart engagement → volitional alignment → agentive resolve → outward expression. This movement is top-down and outward, with no evidenced reversal from body to inner being. Goodness is constitutionally deep but does not leave a somatic deposit. |
| SYN-INTRA-067-003 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R067 goodness |  | T3 reveals that goodness is the most comprehensively faculty-engaging characteristic in the programme's current analytical record. Every faculty except creativity (T3.5 — silence confirmed) shows substantive engagement: perception (tasting and seeing goodness), cognition (moral evaluation and ranking), memory (covenantal knowing and moral reckoning), affect (stable rooted gladness vs fragile surface gladness), volition (reconstituted toward the good), agency (active expression required and enabled), moral evaluation (goodness as the normative anchor of the evaluative faculty), conscience (goodness calibrates the conscience's standard), conscientiousness (integration of all faculties into sustained moral expression), and relational capacity (dual-directional overflow). The tier as a whole reveals that goodness is not a specialist characteristic — it is an integrating one. The person who possesses genuine goodness has every faculty engaged and aligned. This comprehensive faculty-engagement is what distinguishes genuine goodness from its surface forms: surface goodness (the Haman problem) engages affect without engaging conscience or volitional alignment; genuine goodness integrates all. |
| SYN-INTRA-067-004 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R067 goodness |  | T4 as a whole reveals that goodness operates across all relational directions simultaneously — God-to-human, human-to-God, and human-to-human — but with a fundamental asymmetry: the God-to-human direction is prior, generative, and abundant in evidence; the human-to-God direction is responsive and derivative; the human-to-human direction is overflow from prior reception. This is not a simple tripartite scheme but a structured cascade: God's goodness initiates and flows toward the human person (Q&A-099-101); the human responds in trust, seeking, and worship (Q&A-103-105); the goodness received overflows toward others through instruction, justice, and kindness (Q&A-107-109). The most structurally significant T4 finding is the unconditional/conditional distinction (Q&A-100): God's goodness as a character attribute is unconditional; the human's ongoing experience of its relational benefits is conditional on continued trust. This distinction has direct implications for how goodness is taught, received, and sustained. The spiritual beings interface is largely silent — the relational architecture of goodness is thoroughly human-divine in orientation. |
| SYN-INTRA-067-005 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R067 goodness |  | T5 as a whole reveals that goodness is one of the most developmentally rich characteristics in the programme. Three transformation types (condition change, orientation change, character transformation — Q&A-123), two distinct transformation sequences (positive encounter arc and moral awareness arc — Q&A-126), and three context-differentiated transformation mechanisms (encounter, Spirit-infusion, recognition-confrontation — Q&A-129) together produce a portrait of a characteristic that reaches the person through multiple developmental pathways depending on their condition and context. The formation/sanctification section confirms that goodness plays a dual developmental role: it is a product of formation (Spirit-formed character) and an agent of formation (the good person enables the formation of others — Q&A-136). The relationship to suffering deepens this: suffering is not incidental but a context in which goodness is deepened, revealed, tested, and even produced through confrontation with its absence (Q&A-133). T5.7 closes formally — no somatic deposit evidenced. The eschatological trajectory is present but underdeveloped in the data (Q&A-138-139). |
| SYN-INTRA-067-006 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R067 goodness |  | T6 as a whole reveals that goodness is structurally one of the most connected characteristics in the programme — both in co-occurrence breadth (top-25 co-occurrence list spanning the full dimensional spectrum), shared anchor density (7 shared anchors including the two highest-density synthesis nodes in the programme: Mic 6:8 and Gal 5:22), and root-level vocabulary sharing across three root families (TOV, AGATHO, CHRESTO) distributed across multiple registries. The structural picture confirms what T1 indicated definitionally: goodness is not a specialist characteristic but one that permeates and connects the whole inner-being landscape. The most significant T6 finding is the dual constitutive relationship (Q&A-152): goodness is simultaneously a constituent of the Spirit-fruit whole (Gal 5:22) and itself constituted by a tri-fold structure (Mic 6:8). This means goodness has both a containing relationship (it is part of something larger — Spirit-formed character) and an internal structure (it contains three orientations — justice, kindness, humility). The boundary distinctions (T6.5) remain the weakest component — all three nearest-neighbour boundaries (love, kindness, generosity) require Session D cross-registry synthesis. The R187 (strength) co-occurrence anomaly at 32 shared verses remains an open question for Session D (SP-067-027). |
| SYN-INTRA-067-007 | SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER | R067 goodness |  | T7 as a whole reveals a characteristic whose evidential foundation is both exceptionally strong in some dimensions and genuinely constrained in others. Lexically: three OWNER terms across two languages with distinct semantic ranges, a full root-family analysis, a NT coinage (agathōsunē), and a comprehensive vocabulary arc from phenomenological breadth (H2896A) to virtuous precision (agathōsunē) to relational precision (chrēstotēs — Q&A-177). The lexical evidence is rich. Literary: the primary anchor (Mic 6:8) uniquely combines revelatory and covenantal-obligation frames with an internal tri-fold structure that no other verse replicates (Q&A-183); the anchor corpus spans prophetic, wisdom, psalmic, and epistolary forms across multiple contextual settings (Q&A-181). Human science: virtue ethics is the closest parallel framework, with the critical divergence at the source of goodness (divine gift vs natural achievement — Q&A-186). Constraints: NO_WORD_ANALYSIS flags on both Greek OWNER terms limit the LXX analysis (Q&A-175); LSJ data is absent. The evidential foundation is strong at the lexical-semantic and verse-literary levels; it is constrained at the LXX-continuity and human-science elaboration levels. This asymmetry is itself an analytic finding — it identifies where future investigation would most strengthen the registry's evidential base. |
| SYN-INTER-067-008 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R067 goodness |  | What T1 named (goodness as primarily a quality — a settled characterological property) is confirmed and located by T2. The constitutional movement identified in T2 (six-step: Spirit → soul → heart → volition → agency → expression) explains how a characterological quality (T1's finding) is not a static property but a dynamic disposition that moves through the person from its deepest level outward. T1's four constituent poles (ontological, moral-character, experiential, volitional) map directly onto T2's constitutional levels: the ontological pole operates at Spirit-level origin; the moral-character pole at soul and heart; the experiential pole at soul-seeking; the volitional pole at the volitional step. The two tiers read together reveal that goodness is a quality that is simultaneously constitutionally located and constitutionally mobile — it has its deepest root at the Spirit level and its expression at the social-ethical level. |
| SYN-INTER-067-009 | SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER | R067 goodness |  | T1 identified goodness as an integrating characteristic with 12 modes of operation. T3 confirms the mechanism of integration: goodness is integrating because it engages and aligns every inner faculty simultaneously.
